An extensive history of the Japanese invasion of Burma in 1941/2, accompanied by twenty-four unique, company-level scenarios designed for “I Ain’t Been Shot, Mum!” but easily adaptable to other systems.

The book includes attacks, counter-attacks, encounter battles, fighting retreats, ambushes, and even an attempt to rescue POW’s! beginning gently with a couple of infantry-only actions in the jungles of southern Burma, then rapidly progressing to full scale combined arms warfare (infantry, tanks, artillery and aircraft) on the plains and in the dense woods around Rangoon.

Finally, the action moves to the high ground and river valleys to the north of Burma, as British, Indian and Chinese troops desperately try and hold off the rampaging Japanese for long enough to make their getaway!

boardgame: Cymru Am Byth: The Welsh Guards at War 1939-45
description: A first for IABSM, a supplement covering one Regiment through all its major battles of the war. With sixteen company-level scenarios designed for “I Ain’t Been Shot, Mum!” but easily adaptable to other systems, this covers actions from France in 1940, Tunisia 1943, Italy and northern Europe in 1944 and '45. It also presents a unique campaign system for the actions around the Albert Canal bridgehead in Belgium in 1944 when the Welsh Guards took on the Herman Goring Division.

Containing twenty scenarios, this supplement also has an extensive section on tactics of the Great War and the first six scenarios are taken directly from military training manuals of the period, giving the gamer the opportunity to learn the ropes before progressing on the the extensive selection of historical scenarios.

These range from Rommel's first ever battle in August of 1914 to the exploits of the US “Lost Battalion” in October 1918, with lots more in between covering the whole war on the Western Front, from Loos, to Verdun, the Somme, Cambrai, Passchendaele and Belleau Wood with Germans, French, British, US, Canadian, New Zealand and Australians all represented.

boardgame: Terrible Sharp Sword: A Supplement for Gaming the American Civil War with Sharp Practice
description:
Terrible Sharp Sword is a supplement for Sharp Practice, covering the American Civil War with a complete list of rule amendments that allow you to recreate this heroic and desperate struggle on the tabletop.

In addition to the complete rule changes for the Civil War Terrible Sharp Sword includes a complete campaign system, allowing you to recruit your force, appoint officers and NCOs, equip and train them then take to the field.  A range of scenarios and tabletop maps allows you to fight your war, to develop your men's skills and your leaders' abilities, to take your force from being greenhorns through to veterans and beyond.

boardgame: With Fire and Sword: Skirmishes and Battles in South Carolina June to August 1780
description:
With Fire and Sword is a supplement for Sharp Practice covering the activities in South Carolina between June and August 1780, a critical time for the American Revolution in the south after the fall of Charleston.

The skirmishes and small battles that affected the north of the State were to decide whether the Revolution in the South continued, and were to gaina significance way beyond their actual size.

Chris Stoesen of Georgia has carefully pieced together the events during this period of intense and often vicious warfare and created twenty-four scenarios for Sharp Practice that will provide the gamer with a selection of scenarios, from a dozen or so men a side up to over 100 figures each covering milita, provincial and regular troops.  Even Banastre Tarleton gets to make an appearance.

boardgame: This Land Divided: Skirmishes and Battles in Wilkes County February and March 1779
description:
This scenario supplement for Sharp Practice is set in Georgia in the American War of Independence and focusses on the campaign fought between loyalist, rebel and Indian forces during February and March 1779.

Meticulously researched by native Georgian, Chris Stoesen, this supplement presents twelve scenarios set in Wilkes County, Georgia during 1779, along with a comprehensive historical background, analysis of the troops who fought in the area, troop ratings for miltias and regular forces, and complete  rule amendments to allow the gamer to use the main rules for this campaign in an American Revolution setting.  Each scenario has a full colour map, briefings for both sides and umpire's notes to assist the game organiser

Black Diamonds is a game for two to four players that covers over one hundred years of Northeastern Pennsylvania coal mining. Players take the role of mine owners who are attempting to mine anthracite, or, "black diamonds" from the rich coal fields of this region to become the most profitable mine.

During the game, players must hire miners, improve their mine and its safety, extract the coal, then ship it down a unique canal system to the market that simulates the manner in which coal was actually shipped during this era. The coal is then sold at a limited market which creates fierce competition for the most profit as multiple mine owners try to ship and sell their coal.

All of this economic and transportation activity is set against a backdrop of historical events which can influence the course of the game. These include the development of the railroad as an alternate means of transportation, the great flood which nearly destroyed the Lehigh Canal, the rise of Bethlehem Iron and Steel as a major national industry that must be supplied with coal, and the rise of labor unions. During the game, mine owners walk the fine line between profit and safety, payroll, graft, and layoffs which can cause worker discontent to rise to the point that a union is formed. If the union comes to their mine, mine owners must negotiate with their workers to continue operations. The player who best manages their mine and their labor force to make the most profit will become the King of Black Diamonds!

The game is an original design by Geof Gambill with original artwork by Chris Cassidy.

Yugo is a promotional figure for Krosmaster: Arena.

Eliatrope were the first humanoid beings in the universe. They were able to harness Wakfu (live force) and forged alliances with many other powerful races at that time. However, as the result of a treacherous event caused the destruction of the majority of the race, leaving only a small handful left in the present day. Yugo is the reincarnation of one of the kings of the Eliatrope and is the protagonist of the anime Wakfu. He is able to use Wakfu to create phantasmal weapons and open portals to transport himself and his allies to faraway places.

Faithful to his anime character, Yugo, in Krosmaster: Arena, can use Wakfu to attack his enemies. Though it is a short range attack, it has the distinction of being one of the few attacks in the game that deals neutral damage, allowing it to ignore elemental resistances. He is also able to create portals to move himself or his allies to other locations nearby, giving him great utility to any team's mobility. If paired up with his teammates from the anime, who are also promotional figures, his spells will become more efficient, allowing him to use more of them during his turn.

The ChangPing Campaign is the first free wargame to be published by War Drum Games.

The Battle of Changping (長平之戰) was a military campaign that took place during the Warring States period in China. It concluded in 260 BC with a decisive victory by the State of Qin over the State of Zhao, and ultimately allowed Qin to conquer and unify China decades later. The campaign is considered one of the most lethal military operations in history, although most of the Zhao casualties were captured soldiers executed after the battle.
family: History: Warring States Period of China

description:
A workout for the brain.  This mind teaser card game includes riddles, puzzles, and mysteries to be solved.  Skills used to solve these brain teasers include deductive reasoning, critical thinking, and outside of the box analyzing.

The game can be played individually or as a group racing to solve the conundrum first.  Player draws one card from the deck, which could be an image or text problem, and then works to solve it.  If only one player, then he/she "wins".  If more than one player, then the winning player keeps the card as his/her point.  Play goes on until determined number of wins.  The goal is to solve as many problems as possible without having to look at the answers on the back of the card.

The Artful Dodger Game Booster adds the nimble-modded Series II Firefly to your games of Firefly: The Game.

The Artful Dodger starts with a faster drive core and trades in some Cargo Space for bigger Crew Quarters, making it a good ship for misbehaving in!

This game booster expansion includes everything you need to ship out in the Artful Dodger, including 1 Firefly Playing Piece (Turquoise), 1 Custom Dice, 1 Artful Dodger Ship Card and 1 Dodger Specific Mark II Drive Core Card. With this booster you can now take games of Firefly: The Game up to five players (or even more with the Firefly: The Game – Pirates & Bounty Hunters expansion)!

NOTES

+  The Artful Dodger was originally released as a promo item which included the Artful Dodger ship playing piece and matching ship card only.

+  The promo item was only obtainable in two ways: Originally it was released as a promo with issue #162 of GTM (Game Trade Magazine). It is also included as standard in the English First Edition (larger box) version of Firefly: The Game, in the German Deluxe Edition and also in the Spanish First Edition.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

boardgame: Least I Could Do: The Card Game
description:
Two to four players portray young men competing for the attention of women. Each player plays cards to increase their Charm, Looks, and Money statistics. (If you happen to be actor Nathan Fillion, all of your statistics get an extra point.) Before awarding the woman in question to a player, a six-sided die is rolled to determine with one statistic is doubled in value for all the players. The players, then, total their statistics and the player with the highest total is awarded the woman. The player who first scores ten points worth of women is the victor.

The game is based on the eponymous web comic.

Laughing Stock is a light, family dice game that can be taught in less than 5 minutes and can be enjoyed by gamers as young as eight years old. Inspired by “Texas Hold Em”, players take turns rolling dice to try and score sets of animal tiles from the center of the table. The player with the best “hand” gets to add the matching animals to their collection. But watch out for the hyenas! These pranksters will limit what sets you can make and eventually end the game! At the end of the game, the winner is the player with the most majorities of each of the six types of animals.

Mana Wars is a fast-paced deck building board game where players compete as mages in an arena battle to the death. As players channel the different types of mana, they will be able to customize their deck and adapt to their opponents. The seven types of mana are: Fire, Ice, Earth, Thunder, Light, Dark, and Arcane.

In Mana Wars, players each start with a beginning deck of cards and draft different cards based upon the mana types that they channel. As the game goes on, your mage will change considerably.

The 3 types of cards in Mana Wars are Spells, Reactions and Augments, and Aspects. Spells are cards that are cast on your turn, while reactions and augments affect spells or aspects that are being cast. Aspects are powerful defensive enchantments that allow your mage to gather mana to increase their power.

Each round, players are able to do a variety of actions with the main goal of killing the other players. Players can either move and cast a spell or aspect card, move further if no spell is cast, or they may channel mana, allowing them to purchase new spell and aspect cards.

Fiefdoms is a 2-4 player dice based, area control-type game. Each turn players roll four dice from which they create two grid references - determining where on the six-by-six map they can place armies and workers; occupying castles to gain points or controlling villages to gain extra powers. Once placed, your armies will have to fight for the most important locations - using a positional, dice-free combat system.

Thematically the game is inspired by the influx of Norman barons into England during the reign of William Rufus - all vying to grab lands and castles. Despite the theme, this is very much a Euro-game - rather than a wargame.

In keeping with the printandroll promise - to provide everything but the dice - the pdf includes a page of cut out and fold armies and workers. However, you may find it easier and more satisfying to use pieces from another game. As with Siegecraft, the rules are printed on the board for ease of reference.

The files for the game can be found in the files section, below.

A game on Metric Length! Published by Milton Bradley, created by Dr. George Spooner. Track Mete's copyright is 1976. Game comes with a spinner and cards to convert the different meters: deci, centi, milli and so on.

Game description from the publisher:

Unlike other word games, in WOW: World of Words there's no waiting for turns. When the timer starts, all players make as many words as they can in just one minute. Turn over a blue card for the first letter and turn over a red card for the last letter. Players score points for each unique word of five or more letters, with bonus points for longer words. Special cards add exciting twists.

'Report from "World of Words" annual convention:'
I have news that I hope you'll be happy about.  Our Games Gang play-tested 12 new word games.  WOW was undoubtedly the simplest of the 12 games we played, and this is a tough crowd.  But we all loved playing it.  Sometimes simpler is better, especially in a situation like Words Weekend when getting up and running on a new game is so great.  Gloria totally concurs, too.  We loved the elegance and purity of the game -- nothing is extraneous or fussy.  I think this may be the first time we have ever played a card game at Mohonk.

The Crazy War is a game for two players only.

The history is going wrong! Assassins have to kill the robots; janissaries are fighting against scientists; arrows versus missiles. It seems normal, but it is middle ages!

Control your armies, make your own plan, and lead your troops to victory!

Players have to control their troops and occupy 8 spaces on the game board to win. There are variants of cards like troop cards, building cards, and plan cards. You have to pay for the cards to put(launch) them. Also, you have to choose the best position on the 3×4 game board to put your cards to block your opponent. Think about your plans, it’s Crazy War!

The game contains:
·150 cards
·some woden cubes (damage marker)

Munchkin Pathfinder: Gobsmacked! is a 15-card expansion for Munchkin Pathfinder, Munchkin, or any other standalone Munchkin game. Add these cards to your Munchkin Pathfinder game and you'll need to fight off the "Goblin Babies", "Cog Goblin", and "Goblin Hood with the Gobliterator"! Just don't get "Gobsmacked" while doing so...

In this Rummy-style card game, you and your opponents will be competing to build nonsense sentences out of the cards in your hands, with each card featuring a single word. Every turn, you draw, and then may meld a complete sentence if you're able. You also may add words to other players' sentences, but only if the end result is still a complete sentence.

The more cards you're able to play, the lower your score will be. The round ends as soon as one player empties his or her hand. After a number of rounds, the lowest score wins!

With 99 word cards, the combinations are effectively endless!

This collection contains cards, dice, a 2-sided board, counters, and a 72-page book with 106 puzzles and answers, as well as rules for the following games:

- Number Nightmare: Number counters are randomly laid out on the squares of the checkerboard.  A number card is drawn and players look for 3 adjacent numbers on the board that can be combined in an equation to equal the card's number.

- Puzzle Piracy: Parchisi with squares that require you to answer a randomly-drawn puzzle card to progress.

- Word Wizard: Like the Memory game, except that you are trying to combine a word on a card with one on a die to make a valid compound word.

- Syllable Storm: Players try to make words from the syllable cards in hand and letter counters drawn from a bag, scoring the total of the values on the counters.

- The traditional dice games Craps, Rounders and Mexican.

boardgame: The Lone Ranger: Shuffling the Deck Card Game
description:
Game description from the publisher:

In The Lone Ranger: Shuffling the Deck Card Game, the Lone Ranger, Tonto and other characters from the 2013 movie are all trying to grab as much silver as they can while jumping from car to car of a speeding train. Hide your true intentions and try to determine which characters the other players control. If you guess correctly, you can snatch up half their silver!
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Players roll a die and move plastic horse pieces along a path printed on a play mat. The trail has plastic hurdles/obstacles they have to jump along the way.

From box:

Be the first to cross the finish line as you ride your horse through a beautiful equestrian course. Test your skill with actual jumps and other challenges along the way. Be careful, you need to approach each obstacle just right to advance. Miss a jump and you'll have to go back and try again.

The world is under attack! Can you pilot your Jaeger units to victory against the Kaiju? Recreate the epic battle scenes from the movie, or create your own!

Recreate all of the fantastic action from the Pacific Rim feature film with the Pacific Rim HeroClix Mini-Game, featuring two HeroClix figures, an exclusive fold-out game board, a quick-start rules sheet, a scenario sheet, 32 Horde Tokens, and dice.

Includes:

2 Collectible Miniature game pieces
2 Character cards
1 Full-color game board
32 Tokens
2 Dice

Game description from the publisher:

Bodyguards rattled their weapons on the other side of the wall as the Shogun mumbled something in his sleep. Silently, a shadow dropped from the ceiling beam, landing soundlessly on the floor. The ninja paused for a moment, wondering if he should use this opportunity to decapitate the enemy army, but he had not been given such an order. His mission for today was already complete. Besides, he could get the Shogun next time – and the morning will bring a very unpleasant surprise on the battlefield! Soundless, the Ninja leaped out the window, crept behind the guards, and vanished into the maple forest...

The nature of warfare will change forever with Samurai Battles: Ninja Attack, the first expansion for the unique military history board game Samurai Battles. Within this box lurk clever ninja. Though not as strong on the battlefield as samurai, they are indispensable spies, assassins, and saboteurs. Will you use their stealth to undo your enemy's plans?

Meanwhile, fast and powerful mounted samurai archers can dash across the battlefield and strike down foes from anywhere, while stalwart samurai foot archers rain down death from afar. Why engage the enemy up close when you can attack from a safer distance?

Whether you're using the "Commands & Colors" and the "Art of Tactic" rule system, Samurai Battles: Ninja Attack includes eight new scenarios for each rule system.

Munchkin Bobblehead Edition is a special release for Barnes & Noble that contains both the Bobblehead and the base Munchkin game. This version also contains a translucent red d6 instead of the standard white die that comes with every other Munchkin base set.

The Munchkin Bobblehead adds a new rule to any game of Munchkin. By having him present, you are able to re-roll one die per game.

NOTE: This is the 2009 Holiday Edition, not to be confused with the 2010 Holiday Edition.

This special Holiday Edition comes in a box with a unique cover design. It includes all the classic Munchkin cards, in full color for the first time! It also includes the brand new expansion, Waiting For Santa, with 15 new monsters and treasure to add Christmas spice to the Munchkin fruitcake!

This product was released before May 2010.

From the publisher:

The protectors of life and light are rallying behind a second summoning stone! Samuel Farthen joins the Summoner Wars as the Vanguards' Second Summoner. This Second Summoner deck is a full faction deck with its own Summoner, set-up card, Champions, Commons, Walls and Event Cards. It requires a Summoner Wars Master Set or Starter Set to play.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Amalia is a promotional character for Krosmaster: Arena.

The Sadida are lazy tree huggers. They protect the natural world around them, and though they usually prefer to lay around and watch the trees, they can become savage when threatened. Amalia is the princess of the Sadida people and loves to travel, earning her the title of Adventure-Princess. Despite her frequent bouts of sauciness, she is devoted to her friends, and can become extremely angry if they are harmed. Her Sadida origins allows her to control plants for offensive and defensive measures.

In Krosmaster: Arena, Amalia is a close range, area of effect, caster. She is capable of unleashing multiple blast per turn, while her charms can be used on an ally to bring them closer to her. If paired with her teammates from the anime Wakfu, she gains the ability to heal one of them once every turn when it comes to her turn for free.

Ruel Stroud is a promotional figure for Krosmaster: Arena.

Enutrofs are bounty hunters and treasure diggers. They love nothing else than to find loot. Many adventurers surrounds them, waiting for them to be alerted to the next gold mine. Ruel Stroud is a very well known Enutrof and is a very stingy one. He, however, is a very respectable fighter and welds a gold plated shovel.

Ruel is a mid-range combatant, using his shovel to slam away at his opponent's health. His shovel is also capable of stealing his enemy's movement and passing it onto him, letting him just skirt right outside his melee opponent's reach. If Ruel is used alongside his teammates, who are also promotional characters, he will gain armor, to help keep his fragile old bones safe, and a little extra nimbleness to help him escape his enemies if they get too close to him.

Percedal is a promotional figure for Krosmaster: Arena.

Iops are known for being courageous knights. They will fly into the thick of battle to save their allies without hesitation and perform feats that would break even the most experienced fighters. Percedal is a very talented Iop, being a student of Goultard, one of the strongest Iops in their history. He welds the demon-sword, Rubilax, which hosts a demon of the same name, and is in charge of keeping the demon from escaping into the real world.

In Krosmaster: Arena, Percedal is a an exclusively melee fighter; his only offensive skill requires him to be right next to his target to attack. To help him get close, he can use his other skill to jump around to different areas, giving him one of the highest mobility in the game. If he is paired up with some of his teammates from the Wakfu anime, who are also promotional figures, he gains the ability to hit harder with his attacks while also allowing him to have an easier time preventing enemies from escaping from his grasp.

Nox is a promotional character for the game Krosmaster: Arena.

Nox is the main antagonist from the French anime Wakfu. A former human and now an insane Xelor (time mage), he seeks to drain the world of enough Wakfu (life force) to power the Eliacube, a tiny cube of immense power. He hopes to use the Eliacube to reverse time to save his family from their unfortunate deaths.

As a character in Krosmaster: Arena, he takes on the role of a mid-range attacker/disruptor. His skills as an Xelor allows him to steal and disrupt his victim's time flow, reducing the actions they can take while fueling his own to create devastating and debilitating combos. His heavy armor will keep him alive in the thick of battle, and when his armor fails, he can warp away to safety.

boardgame: Krosmaster: Arena – Goultard the Barbarian Exclusive Figure
description:
Goultard the Barbarian is a promotional figure for Krosmaster: Arena.

Iops are known for being courageous knights. They will fly into the thick of battle to save their allies without hesitation and perform feats that would break even the most experienced fighters. Goultard is considered one of the strongest Iops in history. In a twist of fate, he was possessed by an extremely powerful demon, resulting him becoming an avatar of chaos and destruction.

Just like his normal form, Goultard the Barbarian is a close range fighter who uses his health to fuel his devastating attacks. As he continues to attack with no regard for his health, he gains additional range on his attacks. And thanks to his godlike strength, he can execute clusters of low power enemies with little effort, allowing him to quickly level teams that chooses to fight with numbers.

Völuspá: Lightning Bolt is a six-tile expansion for the Völuspá base game or for the base game and Saga of Edda expansion. Just as the gods use lightning to zap their enemies, when you add a lightning bolt tile to the playing area, you zap a tile in play and replace it with the lightning bolt, scoring that line at the same time.

boardgame: Islands of the Damned: Wake Island and Peleliu
description:
The game has been OOP but ATO has done a limited reprint, only available directly from them. You can take it as part of a "Pick 3," or just grab one at a time here:  https://www.atomagazine.com/Details.cfm?ProdID=136&category=4

ATO #43

Any study of the early weeks of WWII in the Pacific is certain to notice the campaign for Wake Island, referred to in those dark days as “the Alamo of the Pacific.” Though the “Send more Japs” radio line was never actually sent by those on the island (it turned out to be a radio code deception), the spirit of their defense heartened a nation shocked over their losses at Pearl Harbor and bracing for invasion. When the word came out about the island sinking two destroyers and inflicting hundreds of casualties, it gave a nation inspiration and seemed to teach the Japanese that the Americans were not as ‘soft’ as they anticipated.

But the island did fall.

As a contrast, few studies of WWII in the Pacific will even mention the Battle of Peleliu, an island of marginal value, which might not have been attacked at all, except those in charge expected it to be so easy. Major General William Rupertus, USMC - commander of 1st Marine Division - predicted the island would be secured within four days. The five battleships bombarding the beaches complained that they had “run out of targets” after a few hours. Both were very wrong. Due to Japan's well-crafted fortifications and stiff resistance, the battle lasted over two months, and has the highest US casualty rate for any battle in the entire Pacific war. (You’ve heard of Tarawa and Saipan and Okinawa...and this was worse?) Against the Odds Magazine issue #43 will be a two-game set, called Islands of the Damned. The issue has two separate solitaire games inside:

Given Up for Dead covers Wake Island and Utmost Savagery takes on the lesser-known invasion of Peleliu. Designer Steven Cunliffe tackles both battles with innovative systems that challenges players to mix boldness with good tactics. They must gain objectives but minimize casualties...as the historical commanders would have liked to do.

The Islands of the Damned comes with 175+ large, full-color 5/8" diecut counters, and two separate 17”x 22” full color mapsheets.

You will be commanding the American forces in each game, challenged with doing far better than the historical outcomes. Can you save most of your troops from the Islands of the Damned?
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Tactics & Tachyons is a game of starship combat. It was designed to offer players a simple combat system for resolving large fleet actions between opposing starship forces within a reasonable block of time. T&T can also be used to resolve battles from a campaign setting.

In 2041 an alien space ship crashes in Washington state. Using that technology the USA develops humanity's first starships. In the meantime a cold war is brewing between the US and its allies and China and her allies. Habitable planets are discovered and in 2050 the exodus from humanity's cradle is on with the colonization of New Earth. Planets controlled by China and her allies secede from Earth and in 2126 Worlds War III begins. The Earth based states claim neutrality in the war. In 2128 with the war still unresolved an alien Starfleet attacks and captures Earth. With a tenuous truce arranged, the relief of Earth begins. The human fleets engage a new foe.

The game manual includes rules on movement, sensors, tachyon guns, torpedoes, probes, fighters, point defense, armor, tow beams, damage resolution and more. The rule book includes guidance for customization and the creation of your own ships. Four combat scenarios are included.

Also included in the rule book are a number of items designed to be photocopied for play. These include ship fleets, torpedoes, fighter squadrons, ship design forms, ship log forms (both long and short) as well as several charts and tables. The game is played with stand D6 dice (a minimum of three are required).

A line of 1/2400 starships model kits in plastic were created by Detailed Casting Products.

Eureka City is a dice-and-card city construction game. Players all have a hand of cards and a common pool. They take turns setting up a "building plan" for their city by playing a series of cards that represent buildings, then rolling a number of dice and distributing them on the cards they have played. Each building completed gains the player some special effect. Buildings can be taken over by opponents unless you're careful. Once a player has five buildings of the same color, he is entitled to build a monument of that color, which also grants him some special effect.

Once all available monuments have been built, the game ends and the player with the most points wins.

Last Seven Days is a solitaire card game with a story theme from a Japanese indie publisher. The game consists of a deck of cards; each turn the player draws as many face-down cards as he likes from the deck and applies them to two of five locations. The locations' special effects may or may not be activated depending on the cards drawn. The player then sets these cards up on a timeline, following certain number restraints. After seven days have been completed this way, the player may choose to end the game and count points, or to loop the week to try to improve his points by adding and/or replacing cards on the timeline.

The completed games forms a "story" of the last week of the world, which characters the player encountered, and what finally happened.

Last Seven Days allows for cooperative play if two card sets are available.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: Players are dealt 5 letter cards, and a Key Card is revealed.  Players play rummy-style, drawing and discarding until they can spell one of the two words on the Key Card, and earning that word's points.  After all the Key Cards are awarded the one with the most points wins.

I-GR-Making is a dodgy trick-taking game with an idol group theme. From a deck of 24 cards, players draw 1-5 cards each to use for the trick-taking. The cards that remain in the deck decide how many rounds the game will last, and what rule each round will follow: highest wins, lowest wins, etc.

Once the deck is finished, the game ends. Each player scores as many points as the cards of the color he has the most of, minus the value of the rest of his cards. This method of scoring, combined with the fact that having a small hand gives the player advantages, makes for a pretty weird game.

Noah's Order is a simple indie card game. Players all have a set of ten animals that are supposed to board Noah's Ark. There are also neutral animals with no owner.

Each round, players play a number of their animals face down. All animals are then revealed, and the round rule is revealed determining in which order the animals will line up. The first six animals enter the Ark, gaining points for their respective owner. The quirky scoring mechanism is that the neutral animals are scored by the player who has the most distance between his first and his last animals in the queue; hence the game is about guessing what the next sorting rule will be, combined with good planning and out-thinking the other players.

Time warp yourself back to the fun and friends of Bayside High with this Saved by the Bell board game.

Zack and Kelly reveal the "who", "what" and "where" of your date.

Maybe they're the romantic type and you go to a scary movie and then out for a picnic...

...Or maybe your date is athletic and you go to a baseball game and have pizza. You may even get to change a date so that it's everything you want it to be!

(From the back of the box)

OBJECT: Play three "pranks" on other players, then go out on a special date to win.
family: TV Shows: Saved by the Bell

description:
Promenade is a dance-themed, temporary-partnership indie card game. Players are dealt cards numbered 1-6 and colored black or white. Each dance, players form couples of two by playing cards face down, then revealing them, while trying to synch their play as well as possible. To facilitate this, you get to look at your partner's hand for 30 seconds before each dance.

The closer the players are in played numbers, the better, but depending on whether the cards are black or white, the couple will perform a certain series of dance steps (walk, turn or sway), which earns them a differing number of points, depending on what the current dance is.
An alphabet learning game, similar to Old Maid, where players match the upper and lower case cards of the same letter together, and try not to be left with the Mr. ABC card.

Gridiron is a simple fast card game that simulates football for 2 to 4 players. 
The game contains 112 cards and 2 dice.

Players play yardage cards to move the ball forward and score touchdowns or field goals. Defensive cards can be used to stop progress or five a player a loss in yardage. Field goals and extra points are scored by using dice.

The point of the game is to score a certain number of points: players play to score anywhere from 14 - 45 points. The final score needed is decided by the players before they start.

Rakpoka is an easy to learn seven card poker card game for 
two or more players aged 8 to adult.

Unlike poker there is no bluffing or gambling involved in the game.

Play is on a Rakpoka mat using two traditional decks of playing cards (including jokers) in tile form.

Each player, in turn selects and lays combinations of traditional poker hands in a crossword fashion in order to secure the highest possible score.

(Score charts are printed on each corner of the play mat).

Scores can be doubled, trebled, quadrupled or quintupled by laying hands over Bonus Squares (labelled x2, x3, and x4).

If you have ever played Scrabble, you will pick this up very quickly....

The rules booklet can be found on our website.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
"Slow Motion" is a filler board game about snail racing. Players must bet on snails in a race, and help their snails to get good positions, in order to make more money and win the game. The game takes about 30 minutes.

During the game, players have three possible actions: play a action card (basically moving the snails); buy action cards; and buy betting tickets. Each player runs one action per turn.

When the first snail reachs the finish line, the game ends immediately. Then, each player adds up his betting tickets and his remaining money. The player with the most money is the winner.

boardgame: All of the Word Games: Yes or No
description:
'All of the Word Games' is to word games as console is to video
games. The word cards in this series of this project will be compatible with almost every word games included in the project.

'Yes or No' is the first game of this project. Players choose some cards from the word cards. one player is dealer and choose a word card. To deduce the card choosen by the dealer, players ask the dealer some questions. In this process, the dealer can lie to confuse them. So prove the lie in the answers and find the word card choosen by the dealer.

'단어게임의 모든 것'과 단어게임의 관계는 비디오 게임과 콘솔의 관계와 같습니다. 이 프로젝트의 시리즈에 있는 단어 카드들은 프로젝트에 포함된 거의 대부분의 단어게임들과 호환 될 것 입니다.

'Yes or No' 는 이 프로젝트의 첫 번째 게임입니다. 플레이어들은 단어카드 중 몇 장을 선택합니다. 한 명의 플레이어는 딜러가 되고 한 장의 단어카드를 선택합니다. 딜러에게 선택된 카드를 추론하기 위해 플레이어들은 딜러에게 몇 가지 질문을 합니다. 이 과정에서 딜러는 그들을 혼란 시키기 위해 거짓말을 할 수 있습니다. 그러므로 그 대답 중 거짓을 밝혀내고 딜러가 선택했던 카드를 찾아내십시오.

Like Rack-O with words.

There are over 200 letter cards in 2 colors, and 80 word cards, each of which has 1 word each of 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 letters.  One word card is dealt to each player.  In each round the letters are mixed in a face down central pile, and players fill up their racks with 10 letters.  Then they draw and discard until they spell one of the words on their word card starting at the front of their rack.  The score is the word's length plus the sum of the letter values, doubled if all of one color, minus the values of unused letters. Others get scores for partially correct words.

As your total score increases, you cannot score shorter words: with 70+ points you must to 7 letters or more, 90+ 9 letters or more, etc.  150 points wins the game.  For 2 players, draw and discard twice during the turn, and play to 200 points.

A set of letter cards with rules for 5 games.

These games are all played by giving out 1 card per player, and having them find other players to form valid words.  This is meant to be played in a large room so players can move around.

Authenticity, the new innovative relationship board game designed to discover the true essence of you and your mate. The game of Authenticity was created to provide couples that are dating, engaged or newly married the opportunity to learn more about their mate in a fun, interactive yet informative format. Seven categories; Finance, Life, Actions, Situations, Religion, Family and Sex and Romance address those questions that are crucial to every relationship. To achieve the full benefits of the game you and your mate must be completely authentic. To be authentic is to truly be yourself, be proud of your past and your present. The focus is not winning or losing, but learning and connecting with your mate so that you can determine if there is a love connection. This is the most fun you will have getting to know your mate. Object: The object of the game is to Authentically answer the questions so that you can learn about your mate.

Equipment: 1 game board, 1 die, 2 game pieces, 39 life cards, 39 finance cards, 39 family cards, 39 situation cards, 40 actions cards, 40 sex and romance cards, 40 religion cards.

How to Play: You and your mate will start with a kiss at the start space. Every time you pass start you must give your mate a kiss. Roll the die, the mate with the highest score goes first. Move your piece around the board. When you land on a space, pick up the corresponding card and ask your mate the question. Your mate SHOULD answer the question as authentically as possible. Once the question is answered, add the card to the bottom of the deck.

     Pick your Pleasure: If you land on this space you have the autonomy to ask your mate to perform an act, performance, whatever you choose!
    The Ball is in your Court: If you land on this space ask your mate any question that you like. Be creative


The first player around the board wins.

The Oleg Story: Survival is a character driven prison survival game, with a wide range of comprehensive detailed miniatures. You and six other friends can control and cooperate 6 different rival gangs

Play the game that allows you to control individual gangs and conquer other gangs. You will be able to play existing gangs and engage in their active and ongoing battles. If you possess the right cards, you can join alliance with a fellow players and plan your attack on other players or even betray your allied peers.

You can play the stories of 618 Nation, Ryder Brotherhood, The Asian Family, The Mobsters, Russian Spies and finally None Gang Members also known as NGM who’s led by Oleg are in your hands.

The global politics of the late 1920’s and early 1930’s were in a constant state of flux. The alliances of the world’s major nations seemed to change every moment. The American Armed Forces needed to devise plans for any number of possible sudden conflicts. Scenarios were written based on current equipment, technology, and strategy. The Washington treaty of 1922 that limited the tonnage of ships for the major navies never happened. The military’s solution was WAR PLAN.

War Plan: Orange was the code name given to the Navy’s scenarios dealing with a threat from Japan. Plan Orange was constantly modified as new ships, aircraft, technology, and strategies were improved. Equipment available to both sides at this time would have been fascinating to see in use, as is the focus of this game.

This will be the first of a series of “War Plan” games from Historical Board Gaming. This boxed game will come complete with all components needed to play. Fight the intense battles between the Japanese and American Navies during the early 1930’s that never were. Utilizing fantasy ships like the Lexington Class battle-cruiser as designed before it was converted to an aircraft carrier, and the Colorado class Battleship that was scrapped before building, the American forces will take on Japanese ships like the Tosa, Amagi, Kii, and No. 13 Class Battleships as well as the infamous 3-flight deck versions of Kaga and Akagi. These beautiful ships, as well as aircraft and airships, will be represented in this game with actual plastic ships, planes, and airships based on designs from pre World War II. With pieces that match the scale of other famous games in the genre, fight the Plan Orange scenarios and then add them together with other games to make your own! Never before has a War Plan Orange board game been designed like this!

HBG’s War Plan series of games will use the game engine debuted in another of HBG’s popular games, The Battle of Midway. Expanding the combat sequence to include new units, these great ships will battle for supremacy of the Pacific. The movement and combat system utilizes an area movement dynamic and D6 damage resolution. Units take turns moving based on unit type, in an attempt to out maneuver enemy units and destroy key targets for victory points.

War Plan: Black is the next planned game in HBG’s War Plan series.

Contents will include a map, Japanese and American plastic game pieces, rules, and a battle Board.

Coming in 2014

August 1943, outside the city of Kursk, Russia…

Germany prepares to force the Russian line back in the largest tank battle of World War II! As hundreds of Tiger and Panther tanks amass outside Kursk with the support of the new deadly Elefant tank-destroyers, the Soviets have their famous T-34 tanks set to counter the Nazi threat aided by KV-1 heavy tanks and Su-152 self-propelled artillery.

Fight this famous battle of armor versus armor, and use the aircraft, artillery, and many of the tank units made famous there to outwit and out maneuver the enemy. With a full set of unique playing pieces in the same scale as other well known games of the genre, the battle of Kursk will be recreated using a D12 combat system, area movement and area control game mechanics.

Contents will include a map, German and Russian plastic game pieces, rules, and a battle board.

Coming in 2014

Horse racing simulation that allows historical races to be replayed, or fantasy races to be run. Base game comes with real-life horses that competed in the US in 2009-10. Game comes with a quick play version that generates race results in about five minutes, and a longer version that allows the player to watch the race unfold segment by segment. The longer version takes about 15 minutes to play.

In both the quick play and full play versions, various ratings assigned to real life horses are consulted/compared/combined, along with charts and dice rolls to generate race results.

The game is distributed as a downloadable pdf from the publisher's website. Expansions include the 1937 racing season and 100 all-time great horses.

Designed by Lon Whitehead.

For youngsters (5-12).  This Parker Brothers game requires a correct answer to a safety question. When a player gives one,  they get to pick out of the box,  a random piece  of one of the puzzles in the game. The player who eventually first completes a puzzle wins the game.

Trenches of the Great War is a strategy board game set during the grind of trench warfare in World War One.  Two-to-four players are pitted against one another as one of the four main powers on the Western Front.  You will command both common and specialist troops from the armies of Britain and France or Germany and Austro-Hungary, including soldiers distinctly unique to each.

Every play-through will be different.  Hazards are deployed before the start of each match, including barbed wire and craters.  The mechanics for every weapon is distinctly different.  The Maxim and Vickers guns will chew an arc across the board.  Snipers will pick off single targets daring or foolish enough to step up to the edge of their trench.  Riflemen will make their slow, bolt-action attacks or grapple in melee combat with bayonets and hand-to-hand weapons until one comes out the survivor.

Combat outcomes, be they melee or ranged, will be decided with alterable randomness.  The default chances to hit are decided between the unit’s skill and cards drawn from the deck.  Each player is dealt a new hand of cards at the start of each of the eight turns.  First the attacker has the opportunity to influence an event where the random card drawn is not in their favor.  The player can choose the card best suited from their hand to turn it in their favor.  In return, the defender can then counter, if they so choose, with a better suited card they choose from their own hand.  This influencing with cards can go back and forth until one player runs out of cards or decides they have expended enough.  It will be a bloody day along the trench.  Will it be that of your soldiers or the enemy’s?

Adding to the replay potential are the missions that players will be issued to complete.  Players could be tasked to capture and hold locations in the dangerous No Man’s Land, to make hazardous excursions to retrieve pilots and the film from downed biplanes, or even going Over the Top to seize the enemy’s trench.  Each of these missions, and others, change the nature of the experience.  The tactics to cover a sortie to a biplane won’t work to invade the enemy’s trench.  How to place machine guns for maximum effect, when and where to bombard the enemy with poison gas, and where to drive a charge of riflemen towards are all in the player’s power.  Wise tactics will mean the difference between success and yet another bloodbath.  It will take more than firepower to live through the day.  Each commander will need cunning, thoughtfulness, and a patience to time their attacks and defenses to best effect.

boardgame: Freedom: The Underground Railroad – Promo Cards
description:
Freedom: The Underground Railroad is a card-driven, cooperative game in which players work for the abolitionist movement to help bring an end to slavery in the United States. The players use a combination of cards, which feature figures and events spanning from Early Independence until the Civil War, along with action tokens and the benefits of their role to impact the game.

Freedom: The Underground Railroad – Promo Cards is a set of 18 promo cards containing Abolitionist Cards, Opposition Cards, and Reserve Cards. Each set was introduced as a different stretch goal during the Kickstarter. But since all three goals were met, they were all shipped together in one pack. These were also available as one pack from the BGG Store.

- Freedom: The Underground Railroad - Abolitionist Cards is a mini-expansion that adds resolution and historical proponents for all three eras of the game, including Josiah Henson, Thomas Garrett, Anna Elizabeth Dickinson, John Parker Hale, Robert Purvis, and more. Each card includes text that introduces players to these important and influential historical figures.

- Freedom: The Underground Railroad - Opposition Cards is a mini-expansion that represents enacted laws and regulations that helped the slavery States and were blows against the Abolitionist Movement. These include the Fugitive Slave Act, U.S. Marshals and more. Each card hinders the Abolitionist movement.

- Freedom: The Underground Railroad - Reserve Cards is a mini-expansion that adds pro-Abolitionist groups and ideas such as The Quakers, the Personal Liberty Laws, the Principles of '98 and more. Each card helps the Abolitionist movement.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Romolo o Remo? God Demands Expansion
description:
The Romolo o Remo? God Demands Expansion contains 18 game tiles in three categories: military, civilian, and economic. Each tile represents an objective that players can accomplish during the game. Scoring a tile allows a player to immediately gain 1 resource, in addition to victory points at the end of the game.

This expansion is in a limited edition available only for preorders and for the Essen Fair game launch.

In this loosely based variant of the classic card game WAR, the suits have broken up, teamed up, and are at war with one another. The Clubs and Spades have teamed up against the Diamonds and Hearts. Unlike traditional WAR, cards are not revealed at random. Instead, players are able to choose the card or cards they would like to play in a variety of combinations. As each battle is fought, the losing side loses their cards and victory will come to the player with cards left standing. Trying to keep your best cards together could be a great strategy but beware the Royal Assassins and the Suicide Bomber disguised as the Aces and Joker respectively.

Generálové (Commanders) is a dynamic war game in which the players test their skills on the battlefields of Napoleonic wars. Through skillful planning, careful preparations, commitment and a little luck, one player will emerge victorious.

Generálové (Commanders) differs from other wargames by simultaneous order planing and resolution, by the austere elegance of Chess, and by the incredible variety of combat situations on mere nine battlefield spaces. Infantry, cavalry and artillery clash in a series of fights in which the victorious player usually is the one who positioned his troops more skilfuly and better used unit abilities in a combat. Even a slight twist in another section of the battlefield can influence the outcome. Situation is constantly changing and it is usually not until the last moment one can be sure of victory.

Rules offer a range of optional mechanics and is suitable both for beginners and for seasoned wargamers.

"Nonsense - the hilarious game of big government spending" board game created by A. Berkeley Compton, approved by Loren Dunton and published by Fair-Games, Inc.

Preparation: each player receives $76,960,300.00, separate 4 DEEDS from card decks, place "directive cards" and "white house clemency cards" in a single pile, face down.

Game play: low roller of dice shuffles "directive cards" deck, has player to the right cut the deck and then deals the first 12 cards face up around the field, starting at space 1. Dealer also places the next 6 cards from the deck face down near the playing field. These are bonus cards and are drawn from if the instructions on the directive card for that turn say to dra2 a bonus card.

Player to the right of dealer starts play and rolls dice to determine which numbered space to advance to and which corresponding "directive card" instructions are to be followed for that turn. Any directive card that says it is to be held by a player is removed from the playing field when the player lands on that space. Any player landing at a space with no directive card next to it does nothing more for that turn. After each person has played, an "administration" is completed, and a new "administration" starts.

Object of game: players roll dice, take cards, follow directions and compete to be the first to declare bankruptcy by spending all their money and earn the title "Certified deficit spender".

boardgame: Jurassic Park Electronic Battling Raptors Game
description:
Make them Battle! Hear them Roar!
You control the head-to-head battle!

They Claw! They Thrash! They Roar! And you control these two BATTLING RAPTORS in their head-to-head fight to the finish - complete with ferocious Dinosaur Sounds!

Grab your controller, and start the fight. CHOMP! STOMP! SLASH! Swing that tail! Hit the other Raptor's weak spot, and send him flying. Score 1 on your controller. Win 5 battles - and you rule the LOST WORLD! BATTLING RAPTORS... Only one will survive - will it be you?

This game is played similar to the classic game Rock 'Em Sock 'Em Robots where the players control their own raptor's battle movements and the first raptor to knock the other off of their pedestal is the winner of the round.  The game can be played over a single round or multiple.

Ages 5 and up. 4 AA batteries required.

It's soccer time again, and the teams are ready for the match; whether playing on a grass field or clay court, it's all the same to them.

Everybody wants to win the fast-playing soccer game Schnipp Es!, but to do so you need the right feel in your fingertips because you need to snap your figures in just the right way so that the ball will land in the opponent's goal. If you risk too much and your pawn tumbles, well, bad luck as your attack is over. Only the one who has placed his pawns in the right places will have a chance to score as time is running out...

This is a simple game where an active player would have a hanging note around his/her back of the head  with the name of some famous person listed. The other players then would try to get that active player to properly guess who they were by giving the active player clues about their identity. Successfully figuring out who you were let you win the game. Giving a clue that helped someone identify themselves also scored points and was instrumental in winning the game. You might be Churchill or Roosevelt or Hitler or Chamberlain or anyone else who was famous back then.

Bloody Jane is an additional leader for Titanium Wars.  Her homeworld is the Ship-world Lexander, and she bears the standard income, building slots, fleet size, and hand size.  Her ability allows her to sabotage a Conquest participant's Unit, of a semi-random type.

Literumi is a 4 player partnership trick-taking game, similar to Bridge or Spades, but with a word-making twist, like Scrabble or Bananagrams.

Success in Literumi requires critical thinking, careful planning, deliberate teamwork and a quick wit.

At first, teams bid with the number of words they think they can make. The winner of the bid gets to call trump, but they have to complete their contract to make any points. Then teams take tricks, to get as many letters as possible. Finally, both teams have two minutes to turn those letters into words. The more words you make, the more points you get.

A fun and colorful adventure full of laughs and Veggie Values! Embark on a VeggieQuest where players turn over cards and then move their Veggie playing piece to the corresponding Veggie on the board. Keep your eyes peeled for “Veggie Values” cards...when one of these are drawn you get to move to the next Veggie Value space on the board. Be the first to the finish and you win.

A small trivia game about love with 100 questions. The game itself looks like a notebook (with spiral binding).

Each page contains two questions with multiple choice answers; the back side tells the correct answer and provides additional information.

To succeed in this quiz about the federal state of Schleswig-Holstein knowledge of both old and young teammembers is needed.

Answer questions from five different topics:
People
Fauna and Flora
Music and Sports
Olden Times - Newer Times (History)
Art

boardgame: Romolo o Remo? Hidden Terrain Expansion
description:
This expansion contains 3 special tiles and 6 silver resource cubes. They are: the Silver mine, the Swamp, and the Heremit Cottage. These tiles can be mixed with other Terrain Tiles to add more variability and fun to the game: the silver mine provides silver resources, the swamp hold Military Units and block farm feed, the Heremit Cottage contains a special character.

This expansion is in a limited edition available only for preorders and for the Essen Fair game launch.

Let's Start a Band! is a board game in which you manage your own band by hiring musicians, writing your own unique hits, and rising high on the charts. Keep your band members happy and make them famous. Make them too famous, though, and they will leave your band and start their own career. Bummer, dude.

It is a special game, because only 100 games are made. To make this possible, the cards are printed and carefully laminated. Besides cards, there´s a game board, player boards, lots of wooden cubes, tokens, a 4 sided die and more. The game comes in a cool looking metal box.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Emperor's Courtyard is a quick, light tile-laying family game for 2 to 6 players. A game takes approximately thirty minutes to play and has simple rules that only take a few minutes to read or teach.

In the game, the Emperor helds a competition to find the best mason of his kingdom. The players will lay tiles to pave the Emperor's courtyard, and get Reward points for a job well done, which consists in completing circles of any of the six existent colors. Later in the game, they will also earn bonus points for adding circles of specific colors to the tiling.

The player who collects the most Reward markers will prove to be the Empire's most talented mason and win the competition!

Knight’s Quest conjures tales of Dragons, Fair Maidens, and Brave Knights in an easy-to-learn family fun game right out of the box!

Knight's Quest is easy to learn, with players themselves providing plenty of twists and turns along the way. Be the first to play every card from your hand by matching the color or number of the previous card. Use Joust, Dragons, Wizards, Companions, and other special cards to improve your chances of winning!

Knight's Quest is a standalone card game for 2-6 players, ages 8 and up. You can play a game in 30 minutes or less—short enough to play while waiting for that monster board game to get set up. Knight's Quest is designed for lovers of classic card games like Crazy 8's, with gorgeous artwork by renowned fantasy artist Jeff Easley, and rising-star illustrator Peter Bradley.

boardgame: Crypt: The Pharaoh's Curse – The Crypt Companion
description: From the back of the package: “Included are three new Characters and eight new, and randomly inserted, Hexes for The Pharaoh’s curse and expansion Hexes for the Echoes of Atlantis.”

Lightin the Lamp plays similar to Fifteen Minute  Baseball.  In about 15 minutes, you will have a final score, shots on goal, goal scorers, full goalie stats, and full stats for a about three or four selected skaters.

Every player has an offense rating and defense rating.  Each period, you choose two forward lines and one defensive pairing from each team.  You compare each player's OFF and DEF rating to come to an overall rating.  Use that rating along with a roll of the dice to determine how many shots are taken that period.  Then roll on the goalie's card to see how many of those shots were let thru.

After the game goes final, you can then roll for stat lines of a few selected players.  Stats kept are goals, assists, SOG, and PIM.  Since you already know the shots and goal totals, you can already generate a full stat line for the goalie.  I'll also provide a chart to allocate any other goals scored to be able to list who scored the goals (regardless if you want to track that player or not)

Welcome to the world of Diggin Deep Boxing!!! With Diggin Deep Boxing you can experience all the majesty and drama of boxing on the tabletop.  Diggin Deep Boxing is a round by round simulation of the game of boxing, complete with everything you would find in a real life boxing ring.  You might even find a few surprises as you would in an actual boxing match.  Maybe you suffered a vicious cut above your eye.  Will it heal?  Will it affect your ability to continue to fight?  Maybe one of the judges is paying off a gambling debt.  Will he be scoring the bout in your favor, or in your opponent’s favor?  Anything is possible with Diggin Deep Boxing.  A full bout takes as little as 20-30 minutes to complete. There is also a quick play option where bouts are decided in one throw of the dice.

Cuttin’ Down the Nets is a dual layered game that features a simplified method in which games can be completed in 20 minutes and an advanced game in which a game with full stats will take about an hour. Neither version will require constant external chart look ups or other methods that can slow down a game’s pace. With Cuttin’ Down the Nets all of the action comes from each team’s rating sheet.

Let me stress that this is NOT a quick play game. All action is resolved possession-by-possession, just as in real basketball. With the simplified method, you are tracking only the team’s score and fouls which allows for quicker game times. However, you’ll still visually see why a team wins or loses. With Cuttin’ Down the Nets each team’s card is formulated to accurately replicate all aspects of a team’s play. Each team is rated offensively for field goal percentage, three point shooting, propensity to commit turnovers, ability to get open shots, propensity to commit offensive fouls, free throw shooting, offensive rebounding, and ability to hit contested shots. On the defensive side, each team is rated for their ability to contest shots, propensity to commit fouls, ability to force turnovers, defensive rebounding ability, and two point and three point field goal percentage allowed.

Cuttin’ Down the Nets also features a unique Power System that incorporates strength of schedule as well as home court advantage. With the Power System you can accurately match-up power house teams against the cupcakes and be assured that the results will mirror real life.

The advanced game features all of the above, but also brings individual players into the mix. Every player for every team is accurately rated to perform as they did in the college basketball season. Cuttin’ Down the Nets features a unique system that doesn’t require manual substitutions on the part of the gamer. This system means that you don’t have to track player minutes or other minutia for realistic performances. With Cuttin’ Down the Nets every player will perform as they did during the college season. You’ll have nights where your starters are “off” and nights where a bench player provides a spark, leading your team to victory.

And while the substitutions are handled within the game engine, don’t think that coaching decisions were left out. As the coach, you decide when to implement the full-court press. You’ll decide when to “sag” to stop the opponent’s inside game or stretch your defense to pressure the perimeter shooters. On offense you decide the pace in which your team plays. Do you fast break, or use the entire clock to limit the opponent’s possessions? Best of all, each coaching option is just that, and option. If you have designs on being the next great college coach, you can implement them all. If you prefer to watch the game unfold, you can implement none. Either way you’ll still end up with realistic results!

Take the court today and see if you can guide your favorite team to the ultimate goal of Cuttin’ Down the Nets!

In Akindo, players are merchants in Japan during the Edo period. Everyone starts out with a small store, and has to do their best to make it as big as possible.

Each round in Akindo has two phases: Purchase and Barker.

During the Barker phase, the active player rolls three dice and divides them between himself and one other player. This will gain those players coins, depending on the distribution of these dice. However, depending on how things turn out, you may be forced to pay to alter the numbers rolled, so you need to be careful about assigning the dice.

During the Purchase phase, goods are sold on the east and west markets. The players bid on the goods, with the highest bidder gaining the goods.

Collecting sets of goods gains you points. The player who scores the most points wins the game.

The game has its players taking turn playing a Hero (Conan, Red Sonja, Bêlit...). The rest of the players play Hero’s foes, fight a round of combat, and then the next player plays his Hero. Hence, only one player is playing a Hero at a time while the rest play his foes. If a player is playing Hero’s foes, their Hero is currently inactive and doesn’t factor into play. The Hero wins renown points by defeating his enemies. There are various victory conditions (the first player to reach 40 renown, the last player with a surviving Hero, or the last player with cards left to play wins the game).

Each player starts with one Hero card, a foe deck and a draw deck. Players randomly decide who will go first. That player becomes the active player, and places his Hero card in the center of the table and is considered to be playing the Hero. All players then draw cards form their draw deck and play begins. The Hero player then can place into play any allies and locations that he or she might have and also place items and weapons on his Hero card for use them in the upcoming combat. Whether or not cards can be placed into play depends on several factors, but the main mechanic for both foes and the Hero is their pool of prowess points. Most cards have a prowess cost that gets subtracted from this pool in order to place a card into play.

Then the foes draw from their foe deck a number of cards partly based upon how many much renown the Hero has. For every foe the Hero kills, that player places that foes card into the area known as the chronicle. Each foe has a renown score, and the total of all the felled’ foes renown score in Conan’s chronicle is his renown score.

The more defeated foes in a Hero's chronicle, the more foes the opposing player gets to put into play, i.e. the more famous the Hero gets, the more enemies want to take him down. Once the foe pool is drawn, the foe players then decide which of these foe cards they want to put into play. There are major and minor foes and rules governing how many foes from the foe draw can be put into play. Once the foes decide which of their foe cards they are able and want to play, they place their foe cards into 4 zones around the Hero(front, left, right and rear). They then place “move” cards from their draw deck for each foe they have assigned to do battle with the Hero. These cards are placed face down and represent both offensive and defensive elements. The Hero player then responds to these move cards with his own move cards, placed face up, to counter each foes’ move card. Hero’s move cards also incorporate both offensive and defensive elements. The foes then reveal their move cards and combat is resolved. If any of the foes are left alive, all players draw cards from their draw deck, and the surviving foes place new move cards onto the table. The Hero once again counters, combat is resolved, etc. until either the Hero retreats or defeats all his foes. If the Hero retreats he loses renown. If he defeats all of his foes, the Hero then can decide to rest and replenish his prowess pool and a small fraction of his life points. The Hero can also “press on.” If the Hero presses on, he starts a new round of combat, but does not get to replenish any prowess or life. Once the Hero rests or retreats, the next player then becomes the Hero.

Play continues until a victory condition is met (the first player to reach 40 renown, the last player with a surviving Conan, or the last player with cards left to play).

Each player must provide two decks which meet the appropriate requirements. The Draw Deck must contain at least 40 non-foe cards. No more than 3 copies of any given card are allowed, with no more than 1 copy of any unique card. The Foe Deck must contain at least 25 foe cards. No more than 3 copies of any given foe card (or 1 copy of any unique card) are permitted. The total renown of the foes in the Foe Deck must total from 95 to 105 points.

description:
Anniversary edition of Alias

Alias consists of many cards, each card has 6 words written on it. Each word has a number, from 1 to 6. Words are various - from very easy to very hard. Some are made of several words defining one thing (example: "Silver Medal"). There is also a board with tiles. Each tile is marked with number from 1 to 6.

Alias is played in teams, usually in teams of 2, but more people are also allowed. The "explainer" must change every turn. Each team gets a figure representing them on the board. Their goal is to get through the board. Each time a figure stops one team member must take cards from the pack (not showing it to the other team member(s)) and explain the words on every card that have the same number as the space their figure landed on. The hourglass (also included) is set just before the team member starts explaining the word. (Looking at the first card and thinking about how to explain the word before the time is set is allowed.) The Player must get the other team member to say the word that is written on card by using sentences to describe the word. For example, if the word is "Silver Medal", a player can say "something that a sportsman wins at the Olympic games". But if other team member says just "Medal", it isn't correct. Then the first player must further define what the hint might be. Second player can answer as often as he/she wants until the time runs out. If time runs out or first player accidentally uses the word which is written in card, the team loses a turn. Variations: opposites and/or foreign words are allowed.

Each player rolls the die - ending on a marked space follow instructions printed on the cover backside (e.g. back to field 8, pass your turn, roll the dice one more time...).
Objective: Be the first moving up to field 50.
Published before WWII.

boardgame: Walk Like A Manopoly: The Jersey Boys Game
description:
This is a Monopoly clone based on the Broadway play Jersey Boys and commemorates the plays worldwide tour. All the deeds, spaces on the board and cards are themed in line with the play.

Players roll dice to determine how far they move. Landing on a space gives the player the option of buying the property. When a player collects all the properties in one color group, the properties can be improved by building. When players land on a space owned by another player they must pay rent. Rent is determined by the location of the property and the number of improvements on the space. Some spaces instruct a player to draw a card which can be beneficial or harmful to the player. Players must avoid going bankrupt to stay in the game. The winner is the last player left in the game.

Note: this was a limited production, themed-monopoly edition that was given to cast and crew of the then current productions (Broadway, on Tour and London) of the Jersey Boys musical.

boardgame: Summoner Wars: Fallen Kingdom – Second Summoner
description: Only death awaits for those who love life. Mad Sirian joins the Summoner Wars as the Fallen Kingdom's Second Summoner. This Second Summoner deck is a full faction deck with its own Summoner, set-up card, Champions, Commons, Walls and Event Cards. It requires a Summoner Wars Master Set or Starter Set to play.

description:
For 1000 years the two rival citadels of the lands were at war with each other. Each citadel was ruled by a mighty master who marched their armies to battle for control over the vast magical pools known as Esper. The Esper imbued their great civilisations with magic, miracles and power. Decade after decade the violence and bloodshed escalated and the increasingly desperate masters performed cursed rituals to gain the advantage. Successive masters and their courts performed dark blood magic and offered tainted Esper to the gods of the cosmos.

Then one night at the sign of the Dread Moon a meteor crashed into the realm and obliterated nearly all life. The Esper turned into fiery Embers which cursed and twisted the lands and its inhabitants beyond imagination. Now nothing remains. Vile Phantoms, deformed creatures and shadows stalk the few remaining survivors.

It has been another 900 years since that fateful impact. The old masters have suddenly been bought back to life having been reanimated by the cursed Embers. They are but mere shadows of their former selves. Their only memory being the wars, anger and hate for each other. Both masters seek to resurrect their long lost champions, drain the lands of its few remaining resources.  Their quest is to rebuild their fallen citadels, summon elemental servants and raise Warbands from the dead. You are the master of the citadel! War will come again.

The goal of Master of the Citadel (MotC) is to create powerful Warbands capable of laying siege to your opponents citadel gates and destroying their city. You begin the game with a champion who you must advance into a powerful character capable of defeating powerful creatures summoned from beyond the spirit realm. Victory in these battles will reward you with the resources you need to scavange legendary weapons and armour, summon elemental servants to do your bidding, develop your citadel and construct deadly warbands.

MotC uses a unique battle system in which your champion summons the spirits of fallen enemies to fight in deadly combat. By performing a cursed ritual you set the difficulty of the encounter and the rewards you get scale accordingly. Victory may yield fate cards that provide hidden bonuses.

Summon elemental servants to boost your champions equipment when needed using MotC’s unique flexible battle system.

Build paths, activate mysterious obelisks, explore abandoned keeps and harvest resources such as wood, stone, gold and cursed embers. 
 Map tiles are colour coordinated and randomised meaning the available resources and strategic locations vary with each game.

Use your citadel board to enchant your champions' equipment. Build warband training grounds, citadel buildings and manage your resource pool.

Accumulate vast resources to build dreaded Warbands of wretched knights, dark mages and undead rangers. Use these warbands to fortify strategic locations, build mines, scout for stat boosting relics.

Then once your powers are at it's peak you are ready to siege and destroy your opponent.

Rock 'N Tour it's a trivia game where you can explore more than six decades of Rock and Pop music in the best trivia in spanish language.
Remember bands, classic songs, significant moments in rock history and discover the truth over facts and myths around the rock world.

Contains:

1 Board
306 Trivia Cards (with over 1,400 questions)
2 Tuckboxes
8 Concert Ticket Tokens
18 Musician Tokens
1 Die
4 "Rock On" Pawns
1 Rulebook

Trivia block with 120 questions and answers about Musik where quickest correct answers score You a points.

Player with the most points wins.

Trivia block with 120 questions and answers about Ancient Roma where quickest correct answers score You a points.

Player with the most points wins.

Trivia block with 120 questions and answers about Dinosaurs where quickest correct answers score You a points.

Player with the most points wins.

Trivia block with 120 questions and answers about German where quickest correct answers score You a points.

Player with the most points wins.
Pie - A 5 point Extra card to mix into your set of Burgermeister!

Adidoku is a new generation of puzzles with a fun mix of characters, places and phrases. Guess the connections and stimulate your brain !

To resolve Adidoku, distribute on a table all the pieces showing the "Pancho Villa" tiles side. Connect the pieces by checking the words and phrases that seem related. For example: connect "Speedy" with "Gonzalez" or "Mexico lindo" with "y querido".

Contains:
222 puzzle pieces
2 cloth bags
1 score pad
1 pencil
1 rulebook

Near Space is a strategic 4X war game that turns the dice you own into a fleet of ships to conquer the universe. In the game, two fledgling empires stare across the void at one another.

The Terran Alliance explores space eager for new frontiers and resources to exploit. They are a cantankerous collection of conflicting factions united only in their refusal to accept any limitation to their individual ambitions. They are at their strongest — and happiest — when challenged.

The Alpha Centauri Regime advances into space to meet the alien threat and eliminate it. They are a single people united under a single banner. The State gives every citizen a purpose, and every citizen gives their all to the State. Anything less is unacceptable.

Now aware of the existence of the other, both sides advance eagerly for the conflict to come. The one thing they share is an appetite for war. Seize the stars!

In game terms, players take on the roles of stellar empires fighting for survival and supremacy amongst the blackness of space. The field of cards between the home worlds of the opposing forces defines Nearspace and the resources that both players have to work with. As players explore their surroundings, each card reveals new threats to overcome and new resources to capture. The war ships that the two opponents bring to battle are dice. Any polyhedral die works as a ship for Near Space. The number of facets on a die are the strength of its armaments and defenses. The more facets, the more powerful the ship and consequently the more costly to field. Polyhedral dice come in a multitude of colors that are used to define the job of a ship and the design specifications involved. Four-sided fighters race forward in the vanguard while d10 battleships bring their firepower to bear at a more ominous pace. Each fleet must explore uncharted space, control colony worlds, pacify unaligned forces, and prepare to battle the ever-advancing enemy!
