Game: The Lord of the Rings – The Card Game (LCG)

description:
An adventure board game in a post apocalyptic universe. Your goal is to find the city of New Eden before others players.

Each player will play a character with 8 characteristics: fighting, shooting, driving, chating and having mechanical, medical, discretion and foraging abilities.

Each turn, players choose one of the 5 possible destinations. On his way to the chosen destination, each player will make an encounter and most of the time a fight will result. But all encounters are not the same. Depending on your opponent you'll try to run away, to pay what he wants or to use one of your 8 characteristics to fight him. If you own a car you also can try to accelerate to evade. Your characteristic plus some items and a dice will decide if you succeed or not.

Depending on the place you've chosen different actions are possible: trying to hide to recover, collecting items, selling or buying items, fighting with other players or exchanging items in between players. Items are in your hand. You may use most of them but some have a cost you must pay to use them.

But in this post apocalyptic survival is not easy. Each turn you must eat and drink.  You can get wounded or contract diseases that players with good medical abilities can heal or cure. Players can also improve weapons, vehicles or armors using their technical abilities.

The first player to arrive at New Eden, one of the 5 destinations, wins. But the road to new Eden is more challenging: you'll face 5 encounters on your way. And before entering New Eden you must be free of any disease.

Two additional scenari are available, one with a mission to be accomplished before trying the road to New Eden, and one for a single player.

boardgame: Happy Hour: No winners, just losers!
description:
The best drinking game ever is in your hands!
 No swearing, no pointing and no toilet breaks. Respect these 3 laws or you will have to... drink. So keep an eye on your opponents and fill glasses, rise to the tasks (speed, sequences of numbers, general knowledge...). But don’t forget the law!
 Happy Hour, the game where everyone loses but wants to play again!
 This game is for players 18 and over. You are responsible for your own actions and decisions. This game is not intended to be played with alcoholic drinks.

Gameplay: Each round, a player draws a card at random. Whether it's a Challenge card or a "drink" card, you'll need to be smart, cunning, and/or quick, or simply know how to step aside to avoid being forced to take a drink! 
Furthermore, you are strictly forbidden from taking "pee breaks," except by drawing a "Restroom" card. 
The player who draws the 4th and final "Happy Hour" card loses the game and will be forced to down the central glass... the contents of which have been previously chosen by the other players!

Challenges: Throughout the game, take on exciting challenges, whether they're speed challenges, number sequences, or general knowledge questions. Fill the glasses and make sure you stay competitive. Happy Hour offers a variety of challenges to spice things up.

Follow the 3 Laws: In Happy Hour, follow the simple but crucial rules: no swearing, no pointing, and most importantly, no bathroom breaks! Breaking these laws means you have to... drink! Keep an eye on your opponents and prepare for some laughs.

A game where you predict the future! 
Combine action cards with player cards to make a prediction, lay it face down on the table next to you. Turn it over when it comes true, and score!

Two games are taking place at once. A guessing game where you want to predict what the other players are doing, and a worker placement game where you send your assistants to get money, more actions to predict and even more assistants.

Simple game in which players spin a top and attempt to hit tiny balls into the scoring area.

Each player, in turn, spins the top in the circle in the center of the box, attempting to spin so as to keep the top going as long as possible. While the top is spinning the player will drop the six colored wooden balls through the hole in the platform so that they may roll down the sides of the platform and strike the spinning top. The balls will bounce off the top in a lively manner and will fall into holes thus scoring.

If the top jumps out of the center the SCORE DOES NOT COUNT. The player loses his opportunity to score during that play. Try to spin the top so as to keep it spinning, and keep it in the center circle. A grand total score of 3000 points wins.

Promo Set #2 includes extended, full-art, English versions of the following maids:

Claudine De La Rochelle (PR06) 
Aline Du Roy (PR07) 
Clymene Silvestri (PR08) - Usable in any Tanto Cuore base game
Shion Tsuwabuki (PR09) - Usable in any Tanto Cuore base game
Eve Valentine (PR10) - Usable in any Tanto Cuore base game

The set has been distributed with purchases of Tanto Cuore: Expanding the House.

Family members, or at least friends who know each other well, take turns answering questions from a booklet while the remaining players attempt to correctly predict their answers. Correct guesses allow your pawn to advance towards the goal.

The Artifact is a game for 2-5 players where betraying your friends and stealing from them is as important as discovering resources and exploring the cosmos.

The Artifact uses an innovative map dynamic which means every game is unique and different.
Set in the far-future and featuring startling artwork, over 100 cards, exotic locations and constantly evolving play, The Artifact is a game like no other.
Win by strategy, tactics or blind luck!

Players can choose from 8 different factions, each with their own ideology, abilities and agenda.  
Everyone starts from Earth and plays location cards to build the game board and open exploration.  This gets larger and more expansive every turn!  
Moving around the universe, players attempt to capture Artifact Markers from different locations such as planets, derelict ships, spaceports, even Alien colonies!  All the while hamstringing other players! 
Using events, items, special abilities and fuel, players can fight, steal, strand or even send players in the wrong direction, all to achieve the ultimate goal; gain 4 Artifact Markers and begin the Race for the Artifact!

Designed by a fencing instructor, La Piste is a fast paced, 2-player fencing simulation card game.  Each player selects a fencer with special skills and a weapon (foil, epee, or saber).  Fencers move along a strip and play a variety of cards representing various fencing moves.  A six-sided die determines certain outcomes.

This game focuses on fencing moves and tries to capture the strategy and speed of real fencing.

Extra parts needed to play:

1 6-sided die
2 tokens to track score

boardgame: Crootle: A Card Game of Kaleidoscopic Proportions
description:
Crootle is an abstract game that entails matching colors. There are 112 Color Tiles in a standard deck, along with 20 Corner Tiles.  Each tile in the deck is 1.375" square. Crootle uses four colors, red, green, blue, and yellow.  Each Color tile is divided into quadrants, each quadrant one of the four colors. A Color Tile may have each of its quadrants a different color, or they may be all the same, or a combination. Because there are four colors, there are 256 color combinations possible, 112 of which have been selected to make this standard deck. A Corner Tile is the same size but is solid in color. A set of Corner Tiles consists of one of each of the four colors in Crootle.

The object of the game of Crootle is to connect two corners of the playing field together. Each game the Corner Tiles are randomly set, and each player randomly selects their path, or rather their two corners to connect. Four players could feasibly be on the same path as each other.

Through timed turn play, each player draws Color Tiles and matches them side to side, trying to connect, or block their opponents from connecting, their two corners.

Fun for anyone who sits down to play.  Playing head to head with players of equal ability can prove to make a challenging game.  Test playing has found audiences in children 7+, adult party games, senior players, and even some autistic and other mentally challenged persons have enjoyed the task of connecting Color Tiles.

WarFields is a two player fantasy card game of warriors, summoners, beasts and undead. You'll build an army of the unique characters and progress across fields to meet your opponent head on. Break down your opponent's army and take down your opponent’s king before he takes out yours.

Each turn has four stages: Currency Stage, Build Stage, Move Stage, and Attack Stage. During the Currency Stage, you collect gold from workers and selling cards. Gold drives the game and is use throughout each turn. During the Build Stage, you use gold to add characters to your army or use powerful scrolls. During the Move Stage, you may choose to move your army closer to your opponent or farther away. Lastly during the Attack Stage, you attack you opponent's army with your unique army.

The goal of the game is to break through your opponent's army and take out their King. The first player to take out the opponent's King is the winner.

A pentomino is a five-square puzzle piece. If you took five squares, and laid them against each other, edge to edge, then that would be a pentomino. There are exactly twelve different ways you can arrange five squares. In the original puzzle, the task was to create interesting patterns using the pieces.

This version of Pentominoes is played on a 10" x 10" board ruled into 100 squares. (Solomon W. Golomb's original design for a pentomino game featured an 8x8 grid of 64 squares.)  Each player has one set of Pentominoes and these are placed anywhere on the board in turn. The object of the game is not to make patterns, but to place pentominoes on the board in such a way that the last player cannot place a shape on the board.

Limits to placement:
1. A vacant space of at least five squares must be left after placing a piece on the board.
2. The winning player may place some of his remaining pieces in the vacant spaces to score double points for one and triple points for two.

The rules add a tip: It is advisable to try to reserve a space in the shape of one of your pieces after your opponent has played that shape.

In Arcane Duels the players are wizards fighting each other using their spells while at the same time they try to complete an arcane ritual.

Each player has 2 actions per turn to gather energy, cast spells and complete one part of the ritual, then the next player takes his turn. The game goes on until all but one wizards are eliminated from the game (by reducing their hitpoints to zero) or until one ritual has been completed (in this case the player who contributed the most to all the rituals in play wins the game).

The catch of this game is that each wizard with all his abilities and spells is represented by a single card which will be rotated after each time that wizards has used a spell, giving him/her new options for his next action(s).

boardgame: Doctor Who: Facts & Trivia Quiz Game
description:
Amazing spinning TARDIS answer mechanism!

The Doctor is terribly clever and quick-witted - he always seems to know the correct answers, so how much do you know about Doctor Who facts and trivia?  Think you can beat him?

In this fun facts and trivia question and answer quiz game, test you and your friends' knowledge about Doctor Who and learn all about this excited TV show.  Turn and point the TARDIS to a question and as if by magic it will spin around and show you the correct answer.

In this upcoming card game based on the old dice game Beetle, you draw and play cards and hope to create an entire alien before something horrible happens.

Players have a hand of 5 cards from a deck of 108 that contains body parts, as well as actions.  Draw a card from the deck, then play a card.

You can add a body part to your alien - you need to collect a body, a head, two antenna, two arms, two wings, two legs and a tail - or you can play a card that affects an opponent by stealing one of their body parts or one of their cards.

Watch out for the red cards - these must be played immediately and could wipe out your hand.  And there's one card in the deck that destroys your entire alien!

Red riding hood is going to grandmother's house with all kinds of treats. But in Grandmother's bed the big bad wolf is waiting for her.

The game board consists of 4 puzzle pieces and a 3D-bed in which grandmother lays. In the beginning of the game a mask has to be put on grandmother, so she becomes the wolf. Red riding hood is being placed on a small rotating disc at the end of the bed.

All players have to take three treats to grandmother. Every turn a player has to move one pawn according to the number of pips on the die.  If the turn ends on a stone, the next player takes a turn. If the turn ends in grandmother's house, the player turns red riding hood. If the wolf stays asleep in bed, the player takes his pawn and lays it in front of him. He then continues play with his second pawn. If the wolf wakes up  the player has to start over with that pawn. The first player who places his third pawn in front of him, wins the game.
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Little Red Riding Hood

description:
A fox-and-rabbit themed press-your-luck game for children.

Theme
Roger Rabbit and his friends are having a picnic at the park, but in order to participate, everyone has to bring a carrot. But Frido the fox knows about the picnic too and he's lying in wait! As soon as the alarm call sounds, all the rabbits run off. Will you be the one who is best at calculating the fox risk and manages to collect four rabbits and four carrots on your picnic blanket first?

Goal
Collect four rabbit and four carrot tiles to earn a candy token. Earn two candy tokens to win the game.

Game play
The tiles (16 rabbits, 19 carrots, 7 foxes) are put in the tile bag. 3 tiles are removed from the game, without anyone knowing what they are (there is a variant with no tiles being removed). Each player gets a board showing a picnic blanket with designated spaces for four rabbits and four carrots. Players take turns. On your turn, you draw a tile from the bag. 
If it is a carrot or rabbit, place it next to your board. Then you may keep drawing tiles as long as they show either a carrot or rabbit. You can stop drawing tiles at any point. When you decided to stop, you first place the carrots on your board. For each carrot, you may also place a rabbit. Tiles for which you don't have enough space on your board, or rabbits for which there are no carrots, go back to the bag.
If you draw a fox, the rabbits flee and you have to put all the rabbits and carrots drawn during this turn back into the bag. The fox tile is put into the fox den (i.e. it doesn't go back to the bag). Then the next player's turn starts.

The round ends when a player has collected four carrots and four rabbits on his/her board. The player gets a candy token. All carrots, rabbits and foxes are returned to the bag and a new round starts.
The game ends when a player has collected two candy tokens. He/she wins the game.

description: A variant of Trivial Pursuit where the questions are exclusively about the UNC Chapel Hill basketball team, during the Dean Smith era. Instead of collecting pie wedges, you collect rings, which you place around your Old Well playing piece. Categories are broken down by time period, general questions about UNC Basketball history, and questions about the coaching staff. Players win by collecting rings in all colors, and then moving to the picture of Dean Smith and answering one final question about the staff.

From the publisher:  The Mercenary's Handbook is a BattleTech and MechWarrior supplement describing the mercenary way of life.  It details everything from troop capabilities to a mercenary unit's expenses, and from the ins and outs of contract negotiations to the nature of 31st century warfare.  Commentary and views on the Succession Wars mercenary provide a solid background for gamers who need to learn the basics of the business of war, while game rules translate these ideas into simple systems for creating a merc outfit, finding and signing with employers, and resolving an entire military campaign.

This 104-page book contains more than 50 full-color illustrations and mercenary crests.  The complete history and operating procedures of three example mercenary companies are also included, the Eridani Light Horse, the Waco Rangers, and Wilson's Hussars.

description:
From the publisher:  The Ronin Wars.  The Andurien/Magistracy invasion of House Liao.  The Anton Revolt and subsequent Marik Civil War.  Against the empire spanning conflicts of the Fourth Succession War, Clan Invasion and FedCom Civil War, historians all too often forget such important conflicts, relegating them to the category of "low intensity warfare."  Yet it is these very conflicts that tend to shape the internal politics and culture of each faction, often with more profound impact than the largest wars.

The Historical: Brush Wars campaign sourcebook details the actions involving every line unit in the most important "low level conflicts" of the early thirty-first century.  Maps and full regiment listings convey additional details, while ample campaign rules provide a framework allowing players to replay detail of each war.

Silhouette Cards is played with a deck of cards with silhouette images.

Two cards are drawn from the deck. The first player to come up with a word that matches both cards keeps them both, and two new cards are drawn. When the deck is exhausted the player with the most cards wins.

From box back:

Few Freyjan's ever leave the frozen spires of the Frostbyte Reach.  Those who do are captivated by wanderlust and endless curiosity.  Nyan-Nyan shares the curious lucky streak common among her people, allowing her to escape the most dire situations unharmed.  Now your adventuring party can press their luck by enlisting this unique Hero to the cause of defending Crystalia.

Trains: Gen Con 2013 is a single-sided game board for use with Trains that features the landscape of Indianapolis and Indiana (USA); it includes the Gen Con logo as part of the city of Indianapolis. This expansion was included as part of AEG's "Swag Bag" for attendees of the AEG 2013 Big Game Night event at Gen Con 2013.

description:
(from DriveThruCards)

Are you sure that you can trust your friends and family?  Perhaps it’s time to put them to the test.  That’s Beez Wax challenges a person’s integrity as well as their ability to cover up a lie.  Player’s take turns laying cards face down in sequential order in an effort to empty their hand.  Those caught lying will get buried by “The Hive.”  Those falsely accused will experience sweet redemption.  The player who avoids the “Stinger” and tastes the sweet nectar of victory will be forever known as King or Queen Bee.

Once a player has won a round, all other players must add up the point values of the cards remaining in their hands.

The player with the lowest score after 5 rounds wins the game.

Available at GenCon 2013

This is a ship part (single card) that provides specific anti-fighter defenses to your ship.

Santorini is a unique 2-player tile-placement game featuring a nearly vertical playing surface. Your goal is to be the first to place all of your hotels so that their views are captivating.  Players take turns placing interlocking hexagonal tiles on the inclined board, stacking upwards from the shoreline at the bottom. Each tile shows an isometric view of a portion of the village.

A hotel's view is defined as the collection of tiles below it. Each hotel caps a triangular shaped view. Blue domes, a signature architectural feature of the Santorini region, enhance the view (+1). Your opponent's hotels detract from the view (-1). You don't want your guests looking out at your competition, right?

Players start with 6 hotels. Each hotel has a number of windows.
One 1-window
Two 2-window
Two 3-window
One 4-window

In order to place a hotel, its view must contain at least as many blue dome tiles as the hotel has windows. Your opponent's hotels subtract from this number.

The first player to place all 6 of her hotels is the winner.

More options, more fun and tons more destruction! It's the Gunship: Arsenal!

This expansion collects 3 of the previous expansions into one set. Additionally, it adds new carriers to allow proper 4 players games.

This set includes:

From Gunship: First Strike! X-perimental Weapons
80 Upgrade Cards - Awesome new gunship weapons plus fighter and carrier upgrades!

From Gunship: Crewmates!
18 Crewmate Cards - Recruit characters with special abilities to crew your vessels!

From Gunship: Asteroids!
36 Asteroid Cards - New mini-game and battlespace terrain!

From Gunship: Green and White Carrriers
2 Carrier Boards - Unlocks 3-player and 4-player free-for-all modes!

2 12-sided dice - For use with the new carriers!

From Gunship: Tokens Expansion
1 Sheet of Punchboard Tokens - Die-cut ships for more sci-fi theme!

For centuries, Samoan warriors have spun and tossed their weapons in dazzling demonstrations of courage and skill. The traditional wooden war clubs were later replaced with hooked steel machetes. In 1946, a young knife dancer, “Freddie” Letuli, was performing in San Francisco at an event that also featured a Hindu fire eater and a girl who twirled a baton with light bulbs on the ends. Letuli wrapped some towels around the ends of his knife, dipped them in fuel, set them alight, and invented fireknife dancing.

The fireknife dance (siva afi) is now a major feature of Samoan culture and the pinnacle of any lu’au.

In the Fireknife! card game, players take on the role of fireknife dancers, trying to impress the audience with the best routines. The first player to earn enough points (acclaim from the audience) wins.

There are 100 cards in the deck. Most of these represent moves that a dancer might perform, but a few are mistakes to be played on other players. In addition to the cards, players will need pencil and paper to keep score.

Fireknife! is intended as a light or family game. Mechanically, it is related to Mille Bornes or Mystery Rummy: Jack the Ripper. There are also echoes of Ticket to Ride (“Should I take more stuff or use what I've accumulated?”) and Kill Dr. Lucky (“Should I stop the leader from scoring or hope someone else can do it?”).

description:
This was a promotional item available at the Munchkin Tavern from the 2013 GenCon.

This item can be used in any game of Munchkin or the expansions.

For each style of Munchkin Tavern tote bag you have at the table, you may carry (and use, if legal) one extra Big item.

This was a promotional item available at the Munchkin Tavern from the 2013 GenCon.

This item may be used with any version of Munchkin or the expansions.

Once per game per different style of Munchkin Tavern pint glass, you may go up a level on your turn when you drain the glass. (The drink need not be alcoholic!)

This was a promotional item available at the Munchkin Tavern from the 2013 GenCon.

This may be used with any Munchkin game or the expansions.

Once per game per different style of Munchkin Tavern shot glass, when someone else is losing a combat, you may "take a shot for the team." Take a shot from the Tavern Shot Glass and then roll to Run Away from a single monster on behalf of the player you're helping. If you succeed, or the monster's Bad Stuff would not affect you, then nothing happens. If you fail and the Bad Stuff does affect you, draw a face-down Treasure card after you resolve the Bad Stuff, unless you are Dead.

boardgame: Le projet du siècle: Jeu sur la construction des ouvrages du Complexe La Grande de la Baie James
description:
[Adapted and translated from the French-language rules]

The development of the energy potential around James Bay is without a doubt the greatest technological adventure in Quebec's history. To transform this vast network of lakes, rivers and forests into an enormous hydro-electricity complex is a great challenge. Le Projet du siècle invits you to think on this challenge and to experience all the steps of constructing a hydro-electricity complex.

Le Projet du siècle spans a diverse territory: camps, dozens of construction sites, giant dams, levees and other building works. For this game, we used actual technical drawings from the LG2 dam, encompassing the power station, the 2.8 km dam and over 30 levees, for a total of 46 million square kilometers of building works. Other illustrations refer to other parts of the La Grande Complexe.

The aim of the game is to be the first to construct a hydro-electricity complex. Each their turn, players will develop their own construction techniques. It is important to read the rules book for each step of the game.

The game has two main phases, each with their own track on the board. First is the Organization phase, where players acquires Exploration, Project, Financing and Worker cards to begin a Construction site. In the Construction phase, players will build dams, levees and their power station. Opportunities to act will be determined by the places on the board a player rolls and moves to.

boardgame: The Duke: Middle Ages Siege Engines Expansion
description:
This expansion to the The Duke core game adds additional siege engine tiles as well as an engineer to utilize the siege engines to their full potential.

The players lay the five tiles from the expansion pack on the side of the board, then start a standard game. Whenever a player pulls one of the five tiles noted below, they can choose at the moment to replace the drawn tile with the appropriate tile from this expansion pack. If the player decides not to replace the tile, he cannot replace the tile at any future time. Yes, this means a single player could have all five tiles on their side.

These tiles replace the following tiles in the game:

Battering Ram = Marshal
Trebuchet = Knight
Catapult = General
Ballista = Priest
Engineer = Oracle

There are several new icons introduced with this expansion; Smash, Hammer, Area-Effect, and Non-capturing Move/Jump icons. Additionally, two new Enhanced Abilities are added, Redeploy (V) and Link (VI).

"Trip Hopeful" is singleplayer board game based on the indie game from PC, "Organ Trail Director's Cut". It is a experience roleplay. The random will decide the fate of your character (with a dice). The game consist of twenty cards of event, the table-score and map (U.S. map).

You will cross the United States to find a safe settlement for win. You'll go from point to point until the end. On each dice roll will advance through these points. There will be large and small roads that will consume different amounts of gasoline and battery. There are also radioactive areas in which you can lose health. Depending on your tierada you can find places you can get a supplies as ammunition or medicines. On your way you will meet shortcuts by which you can cross but all depends on your luck with the dice. You'll dincount or count point while advance.

The game is available in Spanish and English. And soon, I'll make expansions with more maps and event cards (maybe multiplayer)

Most importantly, the game is complete and all the rules for the two language

Parker Brothers' strategy board game, "Dewey's Victory: Never Beaten" carries a bold box cover graphic showing Admiral Dewey himself. The game doubtless appealed to boys who idolized Dewey after his famous naval victory at Manila Bay during the Philippine War, in 1900.

A promotion game by Radio Cologne (Germany). The title means listening, gaming, radio.

The game is played on a board that resembles the city map of Cologne. Players move by rolling a "pyramid die" (pyramid shaped die, each side has 3 numbers on it, the number on the bottommost side counts). Each player gets a mission card like "do cover news at site x", must move there, and gets a new one after fulfilling it. After 5 mission cards are completed a player can move back to home base. The first one completing 5 missions and returning to base is the winner.

The game board also contains yellow "program fields" where players must draw a program card and answer questions about Radio Colognes programs. If answered correctly they get a reward, if incorrectly a punishment like 1 step back. Red "chance fields" means a chance card has to be drawn and this means maybe a free turn etc.

"Be thou Sire or Slave?"

Lord of the Realm is a fast paced Trading-Building-Conquest collectible card game that challenges your negotiation, tactics and building strategy.
 You are a noble vying for domination of the Forests, Plains and Mountains with other nobles, in the fantasy realm of Everon.

You win by building up your land, getting rich by making metal, getting victory points by upgrading your buildings or by sending units to capture another’s buildings. Only the richest or strongest will become Lord of the Realm.

How to win by Victory points:
You can win upgrading most basic buildings to advanced buildings with a V symbol (victory points). You need only 5 to win, but they can be captured if not defended!

How to win by making metal:
Buildings that produce metal (coin). You need 9 coins to win. Again, if not defended these can be captured together with all the metal in them!

How to win by conquest:
Build defences and buildings that can recruit the Men, Elves and Dwarves that populate the realm. You can upgrade these troops with metal via a blacksmith or armoury!

Eclipse: Ship Pack One includes six unique sets of plastic ship models—Interceptors, Cruisers, Dreadnoughts and Starbases—for the alien species in the Eclipse base game, a Terran/Magellan player board, and some other new components.

DESCRIPTION:
New discoveries await you in this exciting expansion for the award-winning Eclipse, with powerful new technology, ancient alien vessels, and two new species. Try the new turn order variant, explore the new outer sector, and win power for your faction through judicious use of the security council.

Best of all, Eclipse: Ship Pack One includes 108 new plastic models, enough for every player to have different ships on the board! With so much to add to the base game, players will find all new reasons to take to the stars.

FEATURES

Amoebelli is played by two, each player having six stones of black or white, on a board made of staggered alternating rows of five or six squares.  (There are five rows of six squares with four rows of five squares sandwiched between them so that each square in the short rows shares a border with two squares in the adjoining long rows.)  Twelve central squares are highlighted, six each in two colors; these are the starting zones for each player.

Players begin by placing their stones in the starting squares, then take turns moving and capturing each other's pieces.  Pieces jump in any of six directions to an open space opposite the jumped piece, and jumps may continue in a string if more jumps are possible.  Jumped pieces are removed and then added again to the board at the end of the turn, in any open square adjacent to the piece that was used to jump them.  If there are no open spaces, the pieces are removed from the game permanently.

The game is lost if a player fails to have one of his own pieces on one of his starting squares at the end of his turn, or if a player finds he has no available moves.

description:
Necropolis is a 2 player domino draw game.

A necropolis is a large ancient cemetery. The word derives from Ancient Greek literally meaning "city of the dead".

You will need a set of double six dominoes.

Object - Lowest score at endgame.

Play - Start by shuffling face down the set of double six dominoes. Leave them in a pile face down between the 2 players, from this point on this will be known as the necropolis.

Next decide who goes first, then play alternates.

On your first turn draw a tile from the necropolis and place the tile face up in front of you, this area will be known as the grave site. Your opponent now does the same.

From this point on, on your turn, you may do one of the following actions.

- Draw a tile from the necropolis and place it adjacent to an existing tile at your grave site or

- Draw a tile from the necropolis but keep its value hidden from your opponent by placing it face down in front of you, but behind your grave site (This area behind your grave site will be known as the mastabas) or

- Draw a tile from your mastabas and place it adjacent to an existing tile at your grave site or

- Draw a tile from the necropolis or from the mastabas and start a new grave site, the old grave site is now considered abandoned and must not be played to for the rest of the game or

- Exchange a tile from your mastabas with the necropolis.

Now this ends your turn.

Tile Placement - The top number on a domino is known as its rank and the bottom number is known as its suit. You must match either the rank or suit to an existing domino in an active grave site.

When placing a tile to your grave site, the tile being placed must be placed touching an existing tile along its long edges on either side of the existing tile.

Scoring - The game ends when the necropolis is empty or when players are unable to add tiles to their gravesite. Players then count the pips on their remaining dominoes in their  mastabas and add +1 points to their totals for the number of grave sites they started.

The player having the lowest score wins.

Duel, is a french set of miniature tactical (1 miniature represents 1 man) skirmish rules.

The game uses a creation system similar to roleplaying games as each fighter is created individually with such characteristics as strength, stamina and agility and indivual equipment.

The combat system is based on movement programming and simultaneous resolution of actions.

The rules booklet contains historical lists of units that goes from ancient times to renaissance and cover civilisations from all continents.

Parollette is a dice rolling game consisting of a dice cup, 10 traditional six-sided dice, a wood and felt dice tray for rolling, and a peg board for keeping score.  Rules include four games: Lo Score, Hi Score, Parrollette, and Bottoms Up.  Players roll dice to determine score and adjust pegs to record it; game is won or lost based on that variation's scoring objective.

MTR Railway Journey is a card game which is published by Hong Kong Mass Transit Railway (MTR) company.

All cards are Hong Kong MTR stations. The cards are divided as 8 groups of different railway lines (3 short lines combined as one).

Players get 7 cards in their hands and race to empty their hands.

In turns, players attempt to play cards by matching its color, number, or word to the topmost card on the discard pile. If unable to play, players draw a card from the draw pile, and if still unable to play, they pass their turn.

After player play a card, if he gets any card with the same number/action, he can play all of them within the same turn. 
e.g. the last played card is a RED card, A can play a RED 2, BLUE 2, ORANGE 2 all together.

Card Types:

    Number, all carry the names of a MTR stations. The number ranges from 1-13(the longest line) to 1-4 (the shortest one).
    Interchange Station, it belongs to 2 railway lines (colors) and change the game play direction.
    Stop, to skip the next plyer. The effect can be accumulated to stop several players.
    Notice (+3), make the next player draw 3 cards. Player can play other Notice or Railway Journey card to avoid the effect and accumulate the effect on the next player.
    Wild Changing Color, player select a new color for the next player.
    Railway Journey (+4), make the next player draw 4 cards. Player can play other Railway Journey card to avoid the effect and accumulate the effect on the next player.

ENGLISH:
A deadly virus escaped from a maximum security laboratory in Brasilia, spreading like a plague. The Angra dos Reis Nuclear Power Plant exploded, radiation spreading radiation across the country. An indian ancient curse was awakened in the north. Nazi scientists, refugees from the Second World War, unleashed his experiments with animated corpses in strategic locations.

The undead dominated Brazil and there is nothing anyone can do to change that. Now, leading a group of survivors, you must explore ruined cities in search for supplies, defend yourself from hordes of bloodthirsty undeads, and confront rival groups of survivors.

Or, in the latter case, throw it all away, become an undead and go hunting brains around ...

Um terrível vírus mortal escapou de um laboratório de segurança máxima de Brasília, disseminando-se como uma praga. A Usina Nuclear de Angra dos Reis explodiu, espalhando radiação por todo o país. Uma antiga maldição indígena foi despertada por conflitos agrários no norte. Cientistas nazistas, refugiados da Segunda Guerra Mundial, soltaram seus experimentos com cadáveres animados em locais estratégicos.

Os mortos-vivos dominaram o Brasil e não há nada que ninguém possa fazer para mudar isso. Agora, liderando um grupo de sobreviventes, você deverá vasculhar cidades em ruínas em busca de suprimentos, defender-se das hordas sanguinárias de mortos-vivos e confrontar grupos rivais de sobreviventes.

Ou, em último caso, jogar tudo para o alto, tornar-se um morto-vivo e sair caçando cérebros por aí…

"Korsun 1944" is another wargame of "Great Battles" system (WB-95). It is operation-level wargame presenting clash of arms that started just after new 1944 year came. Game scenario allows 2-5 Players to play all battle or its variants with 5 available scenarios. Battle involved all German mobile reserves on Eastern front which was exhausted and the losses were hardly recovered.

The battle started on January 24th. Red Army’s target was very simple: surround and destroy as many as possible German units in Korsuń pocket. 2 Fronts were to assault concurrently in order to definitely cut off the pocket. It was done after 4 days but it didn’t mean the end of the operation.
On January 28th German forces begin to counterattack which grew in strength every day. Battle of Korsuń pocket was very dynamic, neither side managed to entrench therefore almost every position was advanced and attacked immediately. Every German attack was answered with Soviet counter.
Hitler’s orders denied retreat and shortening the front line so Germans attack constantly, supported by forces in the pocket vitally interested in the success of relief offensive. Russians who had all the aces at the beginning run out of all the reserves and had to switch into defensive positions. Only when reinforcements of 2nd Armored Army arrived the offensive was resumed.

The air in the city is electric as the hottest nightclubs host evenings of intense dance competitions. You're the manager of the best new dancers on the scene, ready to show off your team's sweet-sweet moves. Winning at a nightclub will earn your team prestige for the night, and most prestige wins a point. Win five nights of dancing to be crowned the King of Clubs — but to earn your points, you have to dance!

King of Clubs is a fast-paced card game of sweet-sweet dance moves for 2 or 4 players. In the basic two-player game, each player has a team of dancers valued 1-5. In each round, four of the nine nightclubs (valued 2-7) are revealed, with nightclubs of the same value being paired for a couple's dance. Each player assigns one dancer to each nightclub (two dancers for a couple's dance), then players reveal the values of the dancers with the highest-valued dancer(s) winning the prestige value of the nightclub; the back-up dancer held in hand breaks ties. Whoever wins the most prestige in the round earns a point, and the first player to win five points wins the game. In the four-player team version, each teammate sends a dancer to each nightclub (without consulting one another about who's being sent where), and the highest low-valued dancer in each nightclub wins the prestige; if the lower-valued dancers are tied, then the higher-valued dancers are compared.

In the advanced game, each player also has a hand of five "move" cards. After revealing dancers in a nightclub, whoever has the lower-valued dancer can play one or more moves on the dancer, as long as he can chain the icons shown on the bottom of the dancer card and the bottom of the move cards. After determining who wins a nightclub, the player draws move cards equal to the "move" number shown on the back-up dancer still in hand, discarding down to five move cards, if needed. In the team version, the symbol on the first move card must be present on both of the team's dancers.

description:
You are the head of a vast enterprise, chartered by the Crown to engage in commercial activities in the eastern hemisphere.  As such, you will maneuver your company representatives (factors) into prosperous regions.  You will leverage your influence with the established commonwealths.  You will weigh the need for a merchant navy.  But above all you must navigate changing economic conditions...

Game Play

The board is divided into five continental regions.  Associated with these is a deck of prosperity cards that match the regions.  Each player is dealt a couple cards; in addition some cards are dealt face up in common.  As play proceeds, players try to acquire locations they think will be prosperous.  They deduce this based on the cards they can see, plus the actions of other players.  At the end of a number of turns, all prosperity cards are revealed and regions are ranked based on number of matching cards.  Factors within those regions are then increased or decreased based on this ranking.  This sequence is repeated several times, after which the game ends.

Player actions include drawing influence cards, expanding factors, building ports and fleets, and movement.  At times, open locations will be taken over by a player.  At other times a hostile takeover will be necessary, in which case the right influence and a nearby fleet will make all the difference.

The goal is simple: to have the most factors on the board at game end.

Counterblast: Adventure Battle Game is a miniature skirmish game with a 1920s-'30s space pulp feel.

From the game's intro pdf:

"COUNTERBLAST Adventure Battle Game is miniatures combat set in a retro-inspired Sci-Fi universe where each player commands heroes (or villains) referred to as solos and their companions (or henchmen) referred to as teams. Together, solos and teams form a crew. Players deploy their crews (usually according to scenario rules) and roll a die to determine who will be the “first player” at the start of the game. The highest number rolled (re-rolling any ties) is the “first player”. Each round the “first player” shifts, switching back and forth in a two player game and clockwise in a game of three or more players.

"The game is played over one or more rounds where each player will have the opportunity to activate and perform actions with each member of their crew. Crew members are activated in order of initiative (highest to lowest). In the case of a tie the “first player” that round activates one crew member and when done the next player activates a crew member with that same initiative and so on until all crew members for all players with that initiative have been activated. Once all crew members have had a chance to perform actions the round is complete and a new one begins. Crew members may choose to hold their actions until a later point during the round. Activation of crew members that held their actions may be done between any later activations that round. In the case of more than one player wishing to activate a crew member that held their action the “first player” gets first choice and so on.

"Once activated a crew member may not choose to perform one action and save the other. Any unused actions are lost."

"The year is 2027 and the federal government of the United States, hobbled by the second great depression, is on the edge of collapse. A number of Insurgent factions have sprung up hoping to undermine the government and send the country into a state of anarchy, in the belief they stand to gain power or wealth in the coming lawlessness.

Choose your side: Become one of three insurgent factions bent on sowing chaos by launching ever greater terror events, or make one last stand on behalf of the United States. If you can stand firm and keep this country from descending into anarchy you will restore the people's faith in its government and keep the American dream alive."

Threat Level Red is a game for 4 players where three players play the role of insurgent factions attacking from within the united states, and one player takes on the role of the hobbled, fading federal government.

As an insurgent faction your goal is to increase the anarchy level in the US to 20 by launching insurgent events. Each turn begins with a hidden token deployment phase where you must secretly plant sleeper cell and insurgent event tokens in major cities across the country. Once all the insurgent player's plans are set players then take turns revealing one secret token at time. Each sleeper cell token you activate increases the action points available to you to play your event cards, but also gives the US player more information about where you are planning your next event. Do you carry out small safer operations to draw additional event cards, cell tokens, and earn resources, or do you try to go all out and launch a major, but risky offensive by playing one of your major event cards? Once your organization has grown strong enough anything is possible- from a simple bomb threat, to hacking the nation's closed circuit TV network, or for the most powerful and violent factions building the dreaded suitcase nuke, but if the american player can deduce where your next event is taking place you stand to lose everything.

As the government player you seek to foil as many insurgent events as possible. If you can foil 12 insurgent events, or survive 5 game turns without the anarchy level rising to 20 points you are victorious. Unfortunately you are understaffed and nearly out of resources to face the burgeoning threats facing the nation. You begin the game with a small number of agent units that you must allocate between the major geographic "zones" of the united states. At the start of each turn you gain a small number of reinforcements: additional agent units and counterplot cards. Your counterplot cards will let you set up wiretaps, border patrols, undercover agents. Each time it is your turn you may send up to one agent token per "zone" to investigate a face down enemy token. If you correctly select a location of an insurgent event that event is discarded and you gain 1 stability point, but if the hidden token you reveal turns out to be an enemy sleeper cell the agent will most likely be lost in the ensuing gunfight. Since you have far fewer agents then your enemies have sleeper cells if you were to send your agents in to investigate every hint of insurgent activity you would soon be out of agents and quickly overrun. So instead, watch closely, let the insurgents overextend, give them enough rope to hang themselves before making your move. If you wait too long you risk letting another terror attack slip through your fingers, but if you strike at the right time even a small handful of well-trained agents can topple these immense terror networks.

Botchee Deluxe is a dice game where players will be attempting to create specific number patterns using one of six different modes of game play. The dice are sometimes rolled and sometimes simply used to represent a player's number. The game comes with two different plastic grids that the dice site in, allowing for customizable play and shifting of game difficulty. Most of the games play much like Suduko, with number patterns being required across rows and columns. But some of the games also allow for speed play and solo play. The entire game fits in a small plastic case, making it an easy travel game.

A few examples of the game modes of play include:

o Region: This game mode uses columns and rows in the game frame. A row and a column region extends from one side of the game frame to the other. A box  region can only be located in one of the game frame corners. Players take turns selecting one of the dice and placing it in the game frame. The goal is to be the first player to complete a number sequence using one of the 3 regions.

o Speed: This game mode is all about solving the same Sudoku like puzzle using the Region play mode as quickly as possible. A stopwatch or other timekeeping device is necessary to play this game mode. The winner is the player with the best time.

Big Angry Monsters is a board game where players assume the roles of Giant Monsters trying to be the last Monster standing by destroying each other and the Island of Nacob in the process. Players start the game by building the Island of Nacob by randomly placing city tiles on the board, then placing their Monsters in the City Roads or Ocean waters. Monsters will then duke it out with a press your luck dice rolling mechanic where monsters can attack monsters and buildings, do super moves, gain power to buy crazy upgrades and effects in order to be the last Monster standing. Big Angry Monsters will comfortably seat 2-8+ players(more player may need a larger island!) and take between 30 minutes and an hour to play.

Noir Nights is a Kickstarter Project coming very soon***
        
    


Noir Nights:Blood on the Badge is a fast paced deck building game designed by Joseph Bridges and John Gannon with artwork by John Gannon Commisions and Concepts. Noir Nights is a deck building game where players use Leads and Arrest Evidence points on cards in their deck to arrest Mobsters. It offers a dynamic play experience where players have to react and adjust their strategy accordingly.

Game play is similar to other deck building games, with the player adding cards to their deck by "purchasing" them from a central deck, which has the top three cards revealed and available for purchase. When cards are removed, they are replaced with a new one from the top of the deck. Badge Honor points are earned in two ways: 1)Some Assets/Policemen cards acquired during game play are worth one or more Badge Honor points; 2) Badge Honor points can be gained by arresting Mobsters with Arrest Evidence Points featured on several of the Asset/Policeman cards.
