description:
A promo card for the Russian edition of MammuZ. The game is language independent, so the card could be used with any language edition.

Tyrannosaurus is distributed through game conventions and hobby stores as a free bonus with a Sauries purchase.

Game mechanic: when the card of Tyrannosaurus is revealed by one of the players, this player takes Tyrannosaurus into his or her hand, and all other cards of the herd are discarded.

The object of the game is for 2-4 players/owners to purchase and race with the option of betting on horses.  
During the game, ownership cards for 2- and 3-year old trotters, pacers and thoroughbreds are gained through a roll and move mechanic, where spaces on the game board represent the different horses.  If the horse is currently unowned, the player may purchase the horse with the price equal to the number on the horse multiplied by the dice total times $100.  If the player declines to purchase for that price, the player pays a penalty of $50.  Any fines or other monies paid due to "Risk" cards go into the "Winner's Circle" in the center of the track.  Players go around the track twice before each of the four races occurs.  The first player to finish each tour is given Post Position #1, the second #2, etc.
During the racing phase, owners determine if their horses will race, with a fee of $100/horse being paid.  Placings in the race are determined by the post position times the roll of the dice.  Awards for win/place/show in each race are given in a table in the rules, with each successive race having a larger purse.  Horses that win/place/show are marked with a colored clip.
During the races, betting can occur beforehand with each horses odds ti win are determined by the number on the ownership card times the number rolled on a single die.  Maximum bets are $100/horse.  There is an odds dial to keep track of each horses' odds.  Odds to place or show are reduced by 1/2 and 1/4, respectively.

Winners of the four previous races are entered into the "Blue Ribbon Invitational" at the end of the game for the largest purse.  Before the start of the invitational race, money is counted by the players and the total recorded on a piece of paper before all monies are returned to the bank by the players before the invitational race and the total recorded.  Betting is unlimited in this final race and you can bet on any horse.

The winner is the player that has the most money at the end.

Divisional and Corps level rules for refighting the big battles from 1900 to 1920.  Primarily focused on World War One, Great War Spearhead II can be used from Russo-Japanese War, 2nd Balkan War and Russian Civil War.

Great War Spearhead II is a stand alone set of miniature rules and is a step forward from the original Great War Spearhead. It encompasses some new rules, new TO&E for Russo-Japanese War and three new scenarios.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Portraits is a solitaire patience style game which was originally called "Peddler" (released in 1992) and redeveloped in 2003. It was released in an Italian gaming magazine and also online via Glastyn Games website. It uses a standard 52-card deck and is playable in 20 minutes.

The goal in Portraits is to collect all the face cards into your personal vault using other, similar suited cards. You can collect a face card if you are able to discard cards of value greater value than a face card and also of the same suit. There are also several ways to manipulate the cards at your disposal in order to better your position and avoid getting locked out or otherwise losing the game.

Kingdom Blitz is a fast-paced fantasy card game based on the MOBA genre. Wielding powerful elemental magic, lead your armies of fantasy creatures across multiple lanes in an attempt to topple your opponent’s castle. Lead your Kingdom! Blitz your enemies!

You play as one of six different kingdoms: Knights, Demons, Undead, Elves, Dwarves or Orcs. In addition, you pair one of these kingdoms with one of the four magical elements (fire, ice, earth, and air) to create a single deck of fantasy awesomeness. The play area is constructed in a horizontal line using castle and terrain cards, and the turn is divided into four steps: action, combat, movement, and draw. During the action phase, players place new creatures and cast spells. Then, if any spaces contain creatures from both team, they brawl it out. After combat, all friendly creatures move towards the enemy castle with little regard for their own safety. Finally, the player finishes their turn by drawing up to their hand limit. Players win by moving their creatures to the enemy castle and taking down its defenses.

Build armies, cast spells, and gain momentum to achieve ultimate victory!
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Divoshi is a two-player abstract strategy board game themed as a battle of two savage tribes. While similar to chess at first sight, the rules are very different. It is played on a square/rectangular board which may vary in size somewhere around 7x7. Like in chess, players make alternative turns, attacking and capturing opponent pieces and trying to capture the chief. But here the similarity ends.

Divoshi has five types of pieces, and all of them may move in eight straight directions like the chess queen, but within a limited scope. What is most specific for Divoshi is the rule of pushing/retreat and capture. If a piece enters a square occupied by an opponent piece, the latter is pushed off one square further. If there is no room for the retreat, the piece is captured.

The rules favor an aggressive playing style in which having your own chief under attack usually entails losses. The freedom of movement and the possibility of moving two pieces in one pushing move make the game unusually dynamic.

12/21/12 is a pure chance of the draw game card game targeted at those looking for some crude and low blow jokes. The humor ranges from telling off your boss to pedophilia.

This base game was designed in mind with the most basic mechanic of drawing a card to either gain, lose, or steal points from the other players. You can add mechanics to this game with each expansion which features their own single mechanic. These expansions for the most part can also be played separately from the base game but we recommend getting all your favorite mechanics together and mixing random amounts of cards from each set for a different game each time.

The Mayans we assume predicted the end of the world with their amazing sense of prediction that didn’t see the end of their civilization coming thousands of years before hand. Oh well! It’s that time now and only you can figure out exactly how you will spend your last day on earth and if it will be more epic than any of your friend’s!

General description:
Cyber War is a card strategy game played on a game board divided into squares. Set in the style of the classic spaceship videogames, the players should use their resources wisely, launch coordinated attacks and defend their bases for the win. The objetive of the game is to capture the enemy bases while avoiding the opponent take ours.
During the game we’ll control an army of robots, ships and defense towers created in automated factories located somewhere in the cyberspace, where an endless war takes place between information nodes, adopting the form of small pixelated war constructs of only a few bytes of information. A relentless war takes place in the digital space, perhaps in your own computer, in your phone or inside the ADSL wiring of your home.

Players: 2 or 4.
Recommended age: 12 years and up.
Game length: 40-90 minutes.

Game components:
- Game board.
- 180 cards, including 4 different game sets (blue, green, red and yellow), each one including a 32 cards Playing deck and 6 Base cards, and 15 Terain cards.
- 2 combat dice.
- 70 tokens.
- Rule booklet.

Theme
They finally did it, and we survived it: the greatest nuclear war. In the ruin of humanity, our tools of destruction lie in wait for their next master. As survivors flock to your settlement, they turn their hearts to you to create a future secure from the other savages. Choose your allies carefully and hold your plans close. Maintain dialogue between the other leaders, trade resources, plan coordinated attacks, and promise aid or revenge. It has become clear that only you and your newly formed Security Council can secure this world by suppressing your opponents' ambitions.

A Game for the Nuclear Family
Traditional war games scare away casual gamers. Instead, Security Council presents a diplomacy mechanic that is engaging enough for veteran players & approachable unit mechanics for casuals. Security Council is modular, scalable, quick to setup, intuitive mechanics, self explanatory cards and graphics, and it breeds discussion and post game memories!

Mechanics
Security Council is a tile based modular zone-control, resource management, and diplomacy war board game. Own a war game that makes people laugh, conspire, shout, shake hands, and even flip a table or two. Security Council has reminiscent mechanics to classic war territory control war games but relies heavily on the diplomacy mechanic between players. Trading resources, forging alliances, planning coordinated attacks, and promising aid or sweet revenge is all a massive part of this game.

Command troops in six glorious colors and expand your territory. Build and fire nuclear missiles to rain destruction upon your foes or to buy yourself an insurance policy. You and your friends are the Security Council. As an overview, begin by vying for territory with traditional troop warfare. To some, troop warfare is reminiscent of other war games but is resolved in a different way. Build up your nuke arsenal or nuclear deterrent! Next, fire your nukes or evacuate from your friends nukes! Can you strategically escape the end of days?

Finally, take what is yours in the aftermath of humanity! All that remains are a resemblance of what your armies once were. Will you finally overwhelm your friends? Or will you miscalculate and end on the wrong side of history?

History
Security Council began as an idea where a colleague simply wanted a board game where we could 'nuke' each other. It was sort of a running gag. From the start, I knew I didn't want a game glorified nuclear. The way Security Council plays makes nuking your friends seem utterly devastating as far as a means to progress through the game. Players incorporate a lot of thought into their diplomatic actions around the table and I believe it gives appropriate weight to the issue of nuclear war. Board games are about the people playing it! Truly that is why we play board games, to convene with those around us and Security Council is a medium that creates friends, adversaries, and special moments and memories for you!

That being said, this is not an educational game, per say. Any thoughts formed about nuclear war is all a by product and up to each individual. It is filled with memories of players trying to be friends but enemies too. Many deals are struck and then rescinded when game conditions change. The act of playing with your friends is very fun and after each game new players are always eager to play again.

I began with an existing board from another game.  At the time, I envisioned the game would have it's own unique board.  After some play testing, we saw that a board would create two problems.  First, it would be difficult to balance.  Second, it might limit novelty.

Security Council was successfully funded on Kickstarter.com in December 2015.

Parts List
270 Plastic Color Cubes, 54 Game Cards, 36 Hex Tiles, 30 Factory Tokens, 12 Nuclear Missile Tokens, 1 Six Sided Die

About
Players 3-6, Age 10+, 45-180 minutes, UPC 634041498498

boardgame: Primer: The Gamer's Source for Battles from the Age of Reason
description:
For twenty years, the "Battles from the Age of Reason" (BAR) game series has been the ultimate gaming expression of mid-eighteenth century warfare. The BAR games feature a highly detailed, realistic and interactive game system, gorgeous map and counter graphics, thorough research and exciting battles throughout Europe and America.
However, because of rules depth, detail and period "elan", many gamers have been perplexed in learning the system. This Primer is designed as an illustrated learning tool to accompany and enhance the Third Edition BAR rules. This Primer features illustrated rules demonstrations, game tactics, learning modules and 280 upgrade and errata counters for previous BAR games.

This Battles from the Age of Reason Primer can be used with all BAR games and even with older rules sets, but is designed around the Third Edition BAR rules and ownership of Fontenoy is required for use of the "Learning Modules" sections.

It includes:
1-An introduction to BAR
2-Command Control
3-Formations
4-Fire Combat
5-Close Combat
6-Disorder
7-Morale
8-Basic BAR Tactics
9-Learning Modules & Exercises
10-Updating games to BAR v3
11-BARv3 errata, game support and credits

family: Series: Battles from the Age of Reason (Clash of Arms Games)

description:
Computerize! is a game about assembling and using a home computer, from the days when that was relatively new and interesting. Players move around the board via die rolls to get opportunities to purchase major components and minor accessories, which are checked off on record sheets. The object of the game is to check off everything on your sheet.

Money can be raised in a number of ways. Players can sell parts back to the bank for half of the purchase price. There is an inner section of the board which can be entered (via the "input core buffers"). While there you may land on a space which lets you draw  a software card (shaped like a 5 1/4" floppy); if your system meets the software requirements, you collect money. Each player also starts with "state of the family" cards which can be turned in for cash, but you have to end the game with the same number of these you started with.

Stawiam na kolej is a strategic and economic board game.
This game not only entertains, but also educates.  The players (2 to 4) are investors who buy shares of Polish railway companies. 
Companies are building a rail network, carrying a variety of tasks.
Players who own shares of companies benefit from these investments. 
At the end of the game rail network is fully developed. The player who undertook the best investment decisions and earned the most wins the game.
---
Strategiczno-ekonomiczna gra planszowa „Stawiam na kolej” nie tylko bawi, ale też edukuje. Gracze (od 2 do 4) wcielają się w niej w inwestorów, którzy kupują udziały polskich spółek kolejowych. Spółki budują sieć kolejową, realizując różne zadania, np. „Zmodernizuj linię Wrocław – Zielona Góra, zelektryfikuj ją”. Gracze, którzy są właścicielami udziałów spółek, czerpią zyski z tych inwestycji. Na koniec gry sieć kolejowa jest całkowicie rozwinięta. Gracz, który podejmował najlepsze decyzje inwestycyjne i zarobił najwięcej, zostaje zwycięzcą gry.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Tower of Babble is a language education game. On a turn a player draws a card which has words in French and Spanish on one side and attempts to guess what they mean in English. (There are pictures which may help.) If a player guesses correctly they are paid chips by the other players.

Cards can also be placed on the players' individual mats, matching symbols on the cards to symbols on the mats. The object of this is to complete meaningful sentences in French or Spanish in rows of cards.

Rules are provided for solitaire play and several variants.

When the name of the game is horror; SLASHER will leave you in stitches!

SLASHER is a game of dark humor which concerns itself with the slice-n-dice brand of horror film that seems to be so popular in today's cinema. The game centers on the making of a film about an evil Slasher who is plaguing the fraternities of a college campus. The players, who each represent one of the besieged fraternities, provide the action and plot twists to our ill fated story. The winner of the game emerges as the hero-/heroine of our spine chilling, shriek filled, gore-o-matic tale.

Triple Ha! is the game of cardboard credit rating agencies. Introduce yourself in the big circus involving states, rating agency and crisis phenomena!

Each player takes charge of a State and starts with a more or less average (of course, Italy is worse off than Germany to start) notation. The State card is used to record scores.

Each turn, one of the states plays the role of the CRA.

The CRA chooses a Crisis card among the first three on the top of the pile and puts the States to the test:

1) CRA requests States an estimate of a real quantified data. The more a state is mistaken, the lower its rating will fall...

2) CRA suggests a crazy imitation on the subject of the Crisis card. The most convincing State may increase its rating by three grades.

After 9 turns (about 30 minutes), the game ends and the State with the best rating will be declared "Survivor of the Crisis."

"Triple HA!" is both informative and humorous and deliberately shifting between tragedy and comedy, in order to discuss the crisis without over-dramatization. This game will lighten up your evenings even when the CAC 40 is down 1.15%!

In the humorous card game Cult Classic, players each take on the role of a fledgling cult leader to see who can create the best cult.

At the start of the game you have nothing but your charismatic personality. The game has six cult leader cards, each with his own special power and ideological focus. As the game progresses, you may gain cards representing beliefs that your cult adheres to as well as material assets you've managed to get your mitts on. The beliefs may be in line with your leader or completely unrelated — just watch out for contradictions!

The goal of Cult Classic is to be the first cult to reach ten points, thereby becoming the most powerful cult on the block.

Nix the Six is a chess variant, using a chess board, with common six-sided dice for pieces.

This is the first in a planned series of games that will attempt to rival the richness and complexity of the largest wargames, with nothing more than a common 8x8 chess/checker board, and a bunch of common 6 sided dice.

The game starts with 16 dies each for black and white, with the beginning layout and piece powers similar to chess. But from there, complexities abound, as pieces can not only capture, but can also steal powers from the opposing pieces, and trade powers among themselves.

The game ends when one side no longer has any number 6 dies left, hence the title!

Hedralites is a skirmish wargame in which players create bands of ancient Mediterranean soldiers in an attempt to control the besieged city of Khard. Models gain experience and items over a series of scenarios with an ever-changing board and set of objectives.

Play requires a set of polyhedral dice, extra d6s, and of course a copy of the rules, available for free online.

Buckle your scimitar and join your pirate brethren for a battle to become the Pirate King of the Caribbean. Whether through ruthless combat or economic dominance, become the master of the Caribbean waters and prove that you have what it takes to be crowned the conqueror.

Treacherous Waters: Caribbean Conquest is for 2-6 players and offers up war and adventure on the high seas. Aim to win by either eliminating all opponents or playing all of your pieces. Keep your flagship afloat and sink your enemies to wipe them from the board or systematically engage all of their ports and fleets to erase them from existence.

Combat is simple but intense. Players roll a die to move, attack, and defend. Stock plenty of garrisons in your ports to stave off enemies, and protect your vulnerable flag ship as it can take only six hits before descending to the depths. Loot cards can further your advantage (or your opponent's disadvantage) by providing bonuses (or penalties) to moving, attacking and defending.

You choose between one of six pirates, each with his own background and color, and conquer ports and trade routes to generate larger fleets and greater resources. As the emphasis of the game is on conquering your enemies and making bold moves, the one who dares the most will have an edge toward winning.

Frisbee Finger Golf   
A Finger Disc Golf Game.  Frisbee Finger Golf is a Disc Golf game for your finger, using finger dexterity and disc control.  You manipulate a disc around an 18 hole course played around your house, trying to aim and shoot the disc into a mini disc catcher.  With each hole you must avoid terrain and course hazards, and possibly other discs.  The goal is to obtain the lowest score by shooting the least amount of shots for each hole to win the game.

Number of players: 1 to 4

Components:
[4] Nerf, plastic or card stock Frisbee disc.
[1] Disc Catcher – acrylic or card stock 
[1] Finger Disc Golf Tee Box
[18] Preset Frisbee Finger Disc Golf 18 hole course layouts
[XX] Terrain markers and obstacles - these are anything you find around the house such as plant, chairs, couch, etc...
[1] Random terrain generated chart.  You can take existing courses and re-create them throughout your house.
[1] Rules of play

You can manipulate the finger discs in a number of ways:

1.  The finger flick - By using the tip of your index finger and thumb while the disc is resting on the Tee box, you flick the disc forward for long range flight.  The tip of the finger must always be in contact with the disc before flicking it.  This type of shot is good for long range angled shots.

2.  The Thumb flip - By laying the disc on the side of your index finger and using your thumb nail to flip the disc end over end for a lob type flight.  This type of shot is good for short range shots that need to clear high obstacles in front of you disc.

3.  The roll - By using your two fingers OR your palm as a ramp, you roll the disc toward the goal.  This type of shot is good for short close range shots.

4.  The push - By using the tip of your index finger and hitting the edge of the disc, you push the disc short distances.  This type of shot is good for short accurate close range shots.

5.  The nose drop - By rolling the disc off the edge of your nose, you aim for short range bouncing shots.

6.  The quarter’s toss - By holding the disc with your index finger and thumb, you throw the disc against the table like in the quarters drinking game and shoot for bouncing goal.
Etc…

The Frisbee Finger Disc Golf course can be created from anything found around the house or office.   Each hole is usually 6 to 12 feet long for 3 to 4 PAR holes.

Anything can be used as course obstacles; a plant, a chair, table edge, books, picture frame, phone, cat or dog, you name it! 
 The Mini Disc Cather is a specially designed miniature model of an original Disc Catcher.  All Finger discs must be flipped, tossed or flicked into the Mini Disc Catcher cage!

You begin by laying out the course; you setup the special Disc Tee box on a table, chair or on the floor and then placing the Disc Catcher 6’ to 12’ away on a table, chair or floor.  You designate and place course obstacles within or around the flight path of the finger disc to create a disc course that is either easy or hard.  Each obstacle must specified whether the disc will fly over, around or under the obstacle to clear the obstacle.

Normal Disc Golf rules apply.

All rules of original Frisbee golf apply.

boardgame: Hoe overleef ik al die vragen? (én antwoorden!)
description:
A card game themed around Dutch author Francine Oomen's books.

The title translates as: How will I survive all those questions? (and answers!)

In this card game you and your best friends test how good you know each other by making lists on what the others like best.

Everyone draws a table for herself on a piece of paper with the numbers one to five and the word points at the top of the columns.

One player draws a card and reads it out loud, for example: 
What do you think I think is important about friendship?
1. honesty
2. companionship
3. humor
4. trust
5. feeling comfortable

The others write down all five items in the sequence of which they think their friend finds most important. The one that drew the card writes down her top 5.

For every item that's on the right place, the players gain three points

The game goes on until everyone has drawn three cards. The player with the most points wins the game.

boardgame: Star Wars: X-Wing Miniatures Game – Imperial Aces Expansion Pack
description: Fly your TIE with pride! Imperial Aces features two TIE interceptor miniatures with alternate paint schemes that allow you to wage your X-Wing dogfights while representing either or both of two of the Empire’s most recognizable TIE units: the 181st Imperial Fighter Wing and the Emperor’s Royal Guard. The expansion also introduces several highly skilled new pilots, a dozen upgrade cards, and all the tokens and maneuver dials you need to field these ships, as well as a new mission in which the Empire seeks to utilize new, experimental technology in a high-intensity clash with Rebel pilots.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Game: Star Wars X-Wing Miniatures Game

description:
Life in the Fast Lane is a real-time card game playable only with the Strength Stack 52: Bodyweight Exercise Cards deck or with another deck of numbered cards. To start, deal the cards evenly among all players, with players leaving those cards face down in front of them. Place one card face up in the center of the playing area; this forms the "Fast Lane", and the final digit on this card (and each other card) is what's important during play.

Playing at the same time, everyone picks up and scans their cards, trying to find a legal play, specifically a card with a final digit one number higher or lower than the top card of the Fast Lane. As soon as someone plays such a card and announces the number of the card played, players must now find and play a card matching this new top card. If a player has a wild card, he can play it at anytime, announcing the number that it represents. The first player to play all of his cards wins, while the losing players must perform all of the exercises on the cards they are left holding. Anyone holding a wild card must perform their exercises twice!

For a speedier and wilder game, place two cards in the Fast Lane. Now everyone has two stacks on which to play!

Hermit Boxes' is a two player game from Games magazine, March 2013. It can be played on a 4x4 or a 6x6 board. At the start of the game, the board is empty. Off the board is a supply of three dimensional rectangular pieces having dimensions of 1x1x2 units, where a unit is about the same length as the side of a square on the board. The pieces come in three colors - red, yellow and blue.

Each player in turn chooses a piece of any color and places it on the board either upright, so that it covers just one square, or on its side, so that it covers two squares.

Pieces may only be placed on empty squares, and a piece may not be placed next to a piece of the same color, not even diagonally.

The first player who is unable to make a legal placement loses the game.

Can you get 10 ingredients into your burrito to win? Comes with optional play food and tortilla. Who doesn't remember this epic show? Now you can build your own everything burrito with things you've scavenged from all over. Combine these with the food that comes with the game and you have your very own unique classic. First player to wrap it all up wins! Awesome!

On a player's turn, they will roll the action die and either swap items form their set with another player, take an ingredient from the pile in the center, swipe an item from another player to add to their set, or draw a card.  Each card contains a description of an item the player must find around their home to add to their burrito (something red, something smooth, something shiny, etc.) Once a player turns over a card an hourglass is flipped giving the player 2 minutes to find the necessary item and add it to their pile.  After the 10th item is placed in the pile the player must be able to fold their tortilla and make two opposing edges touch in order to become the winner.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Welcome to Pellinoria, land of knights and dragons, jesters and jousts, gold and chivalry. The king will reward the chivalrous, while the queen favours the wealthy. Great fame and glory will be bestowed on those who manage to defeat one of the many dragons that prowl the land, but they will not be able to do so without the aid of a knight. Those seeking a gentler path may make a deal with the merchant, court the princess or gain a small favour from the jester. And the belligerent will seek to harm their opponents by banding with the black knight or by persuading a thief to perform his nefarious deeds. Can you become chief noble by the end of your time in Pellinoria?

The game is played over 4 years of 4 seasons each. Before each year, the cards are laid out so that each corresponds to a number. Then, during each season, the players roll their dice, assign pairs of them to cards that match the value rolled and take the relevant actions. At the end of the game, the player with most victory points is the winner.

boardgame: Bolt Action: Armies of Italy and the Axis
description: While many nations flocked to the side of the Allies, others joined forces with Germany as part of the Axis. This volume is the definitive guide to the armies of Italy, Hungary, Bulgaria, Romania and Finland. This supplement for Bolt Actions contains army lists and theatre rules to fight the Winter War against the Soviets, march against the British in North Africa, or help shore up the German offensives on the Eastern Front.

boardgame: imagine I Can... Catch a Pirate!
description:
The small games in the imagine I Can... collection of mini-games-in-a-tin are geared at young children 3 and up.

Catch a Pirate! plays 2 to 5. The game contains 27 pirate cards in three colors, a colour die, a pirate die and a pirate die, as well as a wooden pirate captain figure.

After placing the captain in the middle of the table, arrange all 27 pirate cards face up around him to form a square island. Players take turns rolling the three dice. As the dice are rolled, players try to be the first to grab the card or cards that corresponds to the dice roll. If the pirate die shows the pirate captain, players make a grab for the pirate figure. The players that grabs the captain may choose any card of the table, then returns the captain to the center of the table. If the card that corresponds to the dice roll has already been taken for the table, the dice are passed to the next player. Game ends when a player collects 5 cards.

For beginners or very young players, remove the pirate die and wooden captain from play and match cards to the number and colour dice only.

The small games in the imagine I Can... collection of mini-games-in-a-tin are geared at children 3 and up.

Cheese Chase plays 2. The game contains two red and two blue wooden mice, two wooden cheese pieces and 1 die.

Once players choose their colour mice, each player will place his/her mice in a row, one after the other, at the starting line. The cheese pieces are placed 12 inches (or more for a longer game) from the mice across the table. Players take turns rolling the die and move their mice forward according to the number they rolled; the mouse in the back moves immediately in front of the mouse in the front, the number of times indicated on the dice. Game is over when a player's mouse reaches a piece of cheese.

The small games in the imagine I Can... collection of mini-games-in-a-tin are geared at children 3 and up.

Castle Climb plays two to three. The games contain 21 wood castle pieces and one colour die.

Each players gets one blue castle base and one of each 6 different colored castle pieces. Players stack colored castle pieces on their base according to the color they rolled on the die. If the color rolled is already in play of if pink is rolled, that player loses a turn. Game ends when a player has built a complete castle using all the pieces ending with the hot pink piece at the top.

Fresco: Expansion Modules 8, 9 and 10 consists of, as the name suggests, three additional expansions for Fresco! They can be played individually or combined with any or all other expansions for the game. These expansions are:


    8. The Bells – The bishop wants a new bell for the cathedral, and the fresco painters can contribute by buying "bell certificates", gaining prestige (victory points) immediately and additional prestige at game's end depending on how much they contributed relative to everyone else.



    9. The Wall Fresco – By refreshing sections of a wall fresco, the players can gain fame, glory, and a regular paint supply, thus saving money. What fresco painter would pass up such an opportunity?



    10. The Medico – The time schedule for restoring the fresco is hard enough to meet, but now the apprentices are taking ill, one after the other. How can you finish the work on time? Only skillful planning and the healing drugs of a traveling medico at the market will help.

Warlords and Sellswords is card game about building an army to impress the local king so that he will hire your army to fight his war. You will compete against other new armies over a ten round game. The king has outlawed open combat between all armies applying for the job but, what the king does not know will not hurt him. You can murder, steal, and sabotage the other armies to make yours look the most appealing for war. The king will make his decision at the end of 10th day but everyday leading up to it he will send an advisor to form an option of the each army.

Players will spend 10 rounds, in 4 phases.  The Draw phase, the Action phase, the Cleanup phase and the Evaluation phase form a single round. During play, players will build an army, by spending resources and hiring troops from their hand. Players do not manage a personal deck, instead they build their own personal army to gain points and disrupt other players.

At the end of each round the player with the most points receives a recommendation from the King's advisors.  The winner is the player after ten rounds with the most power in their army, and the most reccomendations.

boardgame: Only Fools and Horses DVD Board Game
description: Based on the BBC Series, Only Fools and Horses, this is a game of watching clips and answering questions.

boardgame: Monopoly: Only Fools and Horses Edition
description: A version Monopoly themed with the BBC Series, Only Fools and Horses.  Travel around the board from series to series, remembering your favourite episodes including Mother Nature’s Son, Yuppy Love, and A Touch of Glass.

boardgame: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: Ninja Moves
description:
Jumbo combat card game. Perform the ninja moves shown on the card to defeat the villain hiding on the other side. All players move at once, and moves performed by more than one player at a time means the villain gets away!

Each of the jumbo cards features a villain and a points value on one side of the card. On the other is one of four ninja moves: swing, kick, spin, or jump. Cards are divided into four equal decks with the villain face down; players should have plenty of room to move actively without hitting each other. Together, all players shout "1 - 2 - 3!" and perform the ninja move showing on top of any deck. If a shown ninja move is performed by only a single player, that player turns over the card and captures the villain on the other side, scoring those points. If more than one player performs the same move, even if the move is showing on more than one card, no points are scored and those cards are discarded. The game ends when one deck runs out of cards. Points are counted to determine the winner.

The box declares this a game for ages 5+. The jumbo cards feature artwork from the 2013 Nickelodeon Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles cartoon.
family: Comic Books: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles

description:
In few next years Earth will be a target of attack from alien massive spaceship. Almost all of its surface will be destroyed. Few powerful nations move all their leaders to safe location from where they will try to rebuild and strike back at the powerful enemy.

In Earth: Last Days players will take a role of leader of one of six most powerful nations on earth and side by side they will fight with Alien invaders and build structures and units. This simple strategy game is meant to be a quick and fun but with a lot of option.

Games will be based on chips that will represent units and buildings. Each player will have a voting cards - because in this game - You are not dictator and You will have to listen to the people... or eradicate them!

During their turns players will build structures and units to fight with alien invasion. They will have to use Voting cards to see how their people react on every decision they make. To win humans need to make a breach in the mothership hull and send an unit there to destroy the core.

User summary:

A booklet with a new tragedy set (plots, roles, incidents) and 10 sample scenarios.

As the first extra tragedy set, it is not a complete turn-away from the Basic Tragedy set from the original rules: a few roles and incidents are similar to the Basic Tragedy set from the base box.

This set's theme is human mystery. All supernatural aspect are gone.

User summary:

The second extra Tragedy Set for Tragedy Looper. This is a booklet with 7 extra scenarios for Tragedy Looper and extra rules to go with them, and scenario construction hints. The theme of this tragedy set is typically horror flick roles, with zombies, vampires, ghosts, nightmares and other ghoulish creatures.

boardgame: Monuments: Wonders of Antiquity – Vergil: Writers of Antiquity
description:
Vergil: Writers of Antiquity consists of three additional cards for the board game Monuments: Wonders of Antiquity. Now when Vergil, Homer or Ovid mention your monuments in their epics, you'll become more and more famous.

By paying a certain amount of monument cards, you can attract a writer's attention, making one of your monuments more famous than the same monument owned by an opponent or making your historians work more effectively. When you take the "earn victory points" action, the writer's "money bag" joker symbols count as a helmet, ship or scroll,
earning you addition victory points.

Vergil: Writers of Antiquity, the fifth expansion for Monuments, is a limited edition item from OSTIA Spiele.

Sag's Pfeilgeschwind is a Sesame Street theme based educational game for 2-4 children.

There are 12 fields around a rotatable arrow. At the beginning of the game, 12 cards will be shown openly and the players have to memorize the character, and depending on the player's age, what he's doing. The cards are flipped to hide the image and the arrow will be moved. The fastest player who answers correctly what is hidden on the card receives the card. The player with the most cards win the game.

Patrol Angis is a 15 mm skirmish level miniature game set in The Ion Age universe. The Prydian, Marcher Barons and Khanate Empire have been revealed as factions in this future/scifi setting.

Patrol Angis focuses on one platoon of miniatures per force with a player controlling one or more forces in play. The game uses the often-overlooked D8, the eight-sided die.

Some minis in the line are available, and the rules are in playtest. A 28 mm miniature range is also in development.

boardgame: Impact and Melee: Persian Wars – 499-479 BC
description:
"A Simple Game of Ancient Warfare between the Greek City State Alliance and Persian Empire From 499-479 BC."

From the rulebook:

IMPACT and MELEE: Persian Wars - 499-479 BC
A simple and quick war game of a GREEK vs. PERSIAN battle

Game pieces are represented by top down full color photos of Greek and Persian warriors of the time of PERSIAN WARS (@ 499-479 BC).    Print out the game board (6 x 4 rectangle of squares sized on an 8.5”x 11” piece of paper). You will need to provide your own six-sided dice.


Players set up on opposite sides of the map, and maneuver their units on the squares to attack their opponent's units.  Victory occurs when one side has eliminated the enemy units that were designated in the "How to Win" victory conditions.
French personality from boardgaming website, Trictrac.net, has survived the zombie apocalypse thus far.  This set of survivor and zombivor figures of Mr. Phal is a promo that will appear in France.

boardgame: Ascension: Chronicle of the Godslayer – Ogo Tracker Promo Card
description:
Lifebound Hero
Cost = 4
Victory Points = 2
Card Text: Gain 2 runes. Once per turn, while Ogo Tracker is your discard pile, gain 1 rune.

boardgame: Ascension: Chronicle of the Godslayer – The Looking Glass Promo Card
description:
Enlightened Construct
Cost = 3
Victory Points = 2
Card Text: you may acquire cards under this as if they were in the center row. If there is no card under this, reveal the top card of the center deck and put it under this. If this leaves play, banish all cards under this.

boardgame: Ascension: Chronicle of the Godslayer – Eye of the Serpent Promo Card
description:
Lifebound Construct
Cost = 6
Victory Points = 4
If you would gain runes or honor from a lifebound hero, gain twice that amount instead.

Welcome to the wildest and funniest, "screw your neighbor" game for 2-4 players. It is a fun game you play with your friends, as you put them down one card and one lap at a time.  Racing is the motif, but not always the main goal.

•    Play as the Rednecks, Hippies, Soccer Moms or the Old Folks
•    Each team has a unique flavor and sense of humor
•    Use your hand of cards to help yourself and hinder your opponents – BEWARE THE TERNADER!
•    100 Action cards all with different art will ensure that you enjoy looking at, as well as playing this game
•    This is a “screw your neighbor” game – play with your friends and destroy them

Game Summary
Every play gets to use a custom racing team of their choice.  16 teams to choose from, each with a unique racing team and car!  You appear to be racing around a race track trying to be the first to finish the race (on one of 3 tracks), but really you are just trying to survive while you and your opponents try to wreck each other out of the game.

Each player has a hand of seven cards at the start of each players turn and when you are trying to move you are playing good cards on yourself while everyone else is playing cards to try to hurt your chances.  It is a rough place to drive but oh so much fun.

When your turn is over you and the other players start trying to ruin the day for the next player’s turn.

Thrash-Car uses a combination of a hand of cards (resource management) and dice (roll for your life) to push the players around this track in the exciting race of their lives.  You have to make sure you have enough cards to help yourself while still being able to mess with your opponents.

The first player to cross the finish line OR the last car running will be the victor in this game.

Key Features
•    Play as a hilarious over the top character – own the persona and enjoy the ride
•    Each team has a unique look and sense of humor
•    You have to carefully manage your cards to ensure you can hold off the other players while still being able to wreck them out of the game
•    Car art from over 10 unique artists make the game as visually appealing and unique as the game play itself

From the game's website:

"You play the role of the insurgent leader rebelling against the Japanese Occupiers. The player with the most number of Valour points by the time the Commanding General is defeated wins the game."

Battlebox is a deckbuilding game. Players start the game with a Tactical Deck of 10 cards, made up of 7 basic resource cards and 3 Solo Vigilante mission cards, and 80 Life Points.

During every turn, players draw 5 cards from their own Tactical Deck and use the 5 cards to acquire more powerful cards (Resources, Plans or Mission). Players may also play Plan or Mission cards if they are part of the 5 drawn cards. Plan cards help players complete Missions.

Playing a Mission card leads to a draw from the Warzone deck. If the player fulfills the requirements of the drawn Warzone card, he completes the mission and gains the Valour points and benefits listed on the card. A failed mission leads to loss of Life Points listed on the same card. Reaching 0 Life Points leads to the skipping of the next turn, and reduction of maximum Life Points by 20. When the maximum Life Points reaches 0, the player is out of the game.

The player with the highest number of Valour points at the end of the game wins.

Game description from the publisher:

The year is 1822 — Singapore, a young trading hub is undergoing urban redevelopment better known as the Jackson Plan. Settlers from different ethnic backgrounds have brought their beliefs and culture to this new land.

Legends & Lore is a competitive horror mystery board game based on true stories passed down by generations. You play the role of an investigator searching for clues to solve paranormal cases. The clock starts ticking once the night claims its first victim. You will require all the knowledge you have gleamed from your investigation to hunt down this legendary horror before the darkness overwhelms you.

During every round of the game, players place investigation tokens on various locations to investigate them. Depending on the location being investigated, players may gain clues, hunt cards (improve one's own attributes), find the Authority token (becoming the starting player for following round), or pick up case files. Solving cases result in rewards and, more importantly, valor points.

When the first "Untimely Revelations" case card is revealed, the Horror Track is triggered; at the start of every subsequent round, it moves up one space. When the Horror Track reaches 7, the game ends and the player with the highest Valor score wins.

Board game where players in three rounds answer trivia questions about Croatian history, culture, sport, music, geography, etc.

Društvena igra na ploči u kojoj igrači u tri kruga odgovaraju na pitanja vezana za hrvatsku povijest, kulturu, sport, glazbu, zemljopis…

Solitaire statistical replay game on Soccer, where each team is rated for a lot of statistics (shots on goal, fouls for, fouls against, possession percentage, counterattack, defense, etc.) and the player has to fill a game sheet before each game - as each match differs according to the comparison of the opposing teams.

When he/she has fill up the game sheet, he alternate rolling a percentage dice on the Game Flow chart to see what happens in the match. Result may vary from Missing opportunity, shot on goal, change of possession, penalty, goal and many other possibilities.
Each match lasts about half an hour after you have taken confidence with the system.

At the moment the following sets are available (each a complete game by itself): Champions League 2011/12, Champions League 2012/13, Italian Serie A 2012/13, English Premier League 2012/13, Spanish Liga 2012/13, German Bundesliga 2012/13

In Keep the Crown, players take turns putting pieces on the board, trying to earn points and claim the crown that's awarded to the point leader at any given moment. They use their five drawn playing pieces to create sets of similar coins or gems on the board, with more difficult combinations earning more points. Special pieces such as the Wizard and the Thief each have their own unique powers to assist in making sets. The board slowly fills up, and when it's full, the player with the crown wins!

The game's simple set-collection mechanism appeals to players of all ages, whereas its tile placement and hand management strategies will appeal to more serious gamers.

Special Ops Issue #4 includes

ASL/ASLSK articles:
· Into the Land of the Rising Sun by Chas Argent
· Exit...Stage Right! by Daniel M. Preston

ASL scenarios:
· O7 Broken Wings (Pete Shelling)
· O8 Crucifix Hill (Pete Shelling)

ASLSK scenarios:
· S54 Operation Natzmer (Joe Gochinski)
· S55 The Fire Brigade (Joe Gochinski)

Scenarios for "It never snows":
· 5.9 The Red Devils & The Free Poles
· 5.10 All Americans
· 5.11 Screaming Eagles
· 5.12 The Guards

The magazine also contains the game What Price Glory? and articles on MMP's other game lines.
family: Series: Advanced Squad Leader Starter Kit

description:
Epic Picnic is a fast and crazy card game inspired by a verbal memory game from childhood, corrupted with humor from adult life (ages 17+!). You might bring figgy pudding on a picnic, fashion a makeshift weapon out of Peter Piper's pickled pecker, or inherit a Nicaraguan death squad from your grandfather.

Best of all, it's super easy to pick up and play!

Players reveal cards from a common deck to represent the ridiculous and terrible things they are bringing on the picnic (or one of 9 other scenarios) while at the same time trying to stay focused and remember what everyone else is bringing in order. Miss just one card and you're out of the game in this last-man-standing, brain-hemorrhaging face-off!

The rules also contain several variations and plus an alternate method of play -- Picnic Frenzy -- where players recite just one card per turn, making for much faster gameplay. Played in rounds and featuring 5 second turns, it is perhaps even more challenging than the original!

In Cosmos, players take turns exploring the universe, building far-flung colonies, and collecting taxes (for once, we're on the right side of taxes!).  The goal of the game is to make the most money from the economic opportunities of exploring space, while elbowing out the market competition.  Central market mechanics allow players to compete by increasing or decreasing universal resource prices, while limited resources make each region of space a hotly-contested competition for colonies.  The game ends when the universe is totally explored--meaning if you're sitting in dead last without a hope, you can explore your heart out and rush the game to an untimely conclusion.

Cosmos is very straightforward to learn, and a typical game takes around 30-45 minutes. Cosmos is simple enough for casual gamers to play (and win), but complex enough to keep the interest of more competitive or experienced gamers. Turns are quick, and the game mechanics are easy to remember.

Most conflict in Cosmos is indirect, making this a great game for two players--even those who may be mismatched in competitive nature or gaming experience (read: the hobbyist and the spouse). At the same time, Cosmos allows up to eight players, making it a decent party game, although it gets a bit crowded and far more competitive at six.

Overall, this is a highly replayable, very entertaining game with a really broad appeal and low commitment requirements.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: World of Tanks: Rush — AMX 12t, a promotional card for the World of Tanks: Rush base game available at conventions and through hobby stores, features a light French tank that costs 1, gives 1 resource, and has no special ability.

IconGame
Do you know how you say "pencil" in Spanish? And "rubber"? And "sharpener"? Learn all these words and many more with IconGame.
It's easy: You choose a card, and if you know how to say it correctly in Spanish, you win the corresponding Picnics (points) according to their degree of difficulty. At the end of the game, the player who accumulates more Picnics wins.

     Includes 50 vocabulary cards (with pronunciation guide) and plastic sandwich.


IconGame
¿Sabes cómo se dice “lápiz” en inglés? ¿Y “goma”? ¿Y “sacapuntas”? Aprende todas estas palabras y muchas más con IconGame.
Es muy fácil: Eliges una tarjeta, y si sabes cómo se dice correctamente en inglés ganas los Picnics (puntos) correspondientes, según su grado de dificultad. Al final de la partida, gana el jugador que más Picnics acumula.

    Incluye 50 cartas de vocabulario (con guía de pronunciación) y sandwichera de plástico.

Bicycle Race is a fast-playing 18-card microgame of bluff and luck for 4–6 players, from designer Sebastian Bleasdale.

You’re a cyclist competing for position in the peloton, but overtaking the leaders isn’t easy. You’ll need skill in bluffing and a healthy dose of luck. Can you out-think the other riders and reach the head of the pack in time to seize victory?

GeoPicnic
Do you know what is the name of this province? And the name of the capital?
Have fun learning and practicing spanish geography. And win 10 Picnics (points) for each correct answer.

     Includes 52 cards, a plastic sandwich box, and "Picnic" tickets.


GeoPicnic
¿Sabes cuál es esta provincia? ¿Y su capital? ¿Y la comunidad autónoma a la que pertenece?
Diviértete aprendiendo y practicando un poco de geografía de España. Y gana 10 Picnics (puntos) por cada respuesta acertada.

    Incluye 52 cartas, sandwichera de plástico y pack de billetes Picnics.

Matematikus
Math can be fun and here's the proof. How many combinations can you make with your 8 cards? And which is the best considering that addition and subtraction are worth 10 Picnics (points) and the multiplications and divisions are worth 20 Picnics? Also consider that the cards have a number on one side and a mathematical sign in the other, and you can only use one of them. You will see that it is quite a challenge.

Matematikus
Las matemáticas pueden ser muy divertidas y aquí está la prueba. ¿Cuántas combinaciones matemáticas puedes formar con tus 8 cartas? ¿Y cuál es la mejor teniendo en cuenta que las sumas y restas valen 10 Picnics (puntos) y las multiplicaciones y divisiones valen 20 Picnics? Ten en cuenta también que las cartas tienen una cifra en una cara y un signo matemático en la otra, y que sólo puedes usar una de ellas. Verás que es todo un reto.

Plyt is a great new family board game for players of ALL ages and abilities to play together in a fun, competitive and challenging way and, even better, it improves your numeracy skills!

The game is easy to play and works around multiplying a number of 12 sided dice together within the agreed time, negotiating the Plyt squares and racing against each other to be the first to reach the winner square.  But there’s a balance of risk and reward. Plyters decide how many dice to throw to try and get ahead, and when it comes to it, who they will push back or help.  It starts off gently to let you get the feel of it but as the play gets closer to the end, positions can change quickly and the result isn’t certain until the very last throw!

But Plyt isn’t just about beating your opponents – it’s also about challenging yourself – pushing yourself out of your comfort zone and feeling the buzz of getting it right!
There are lots of different playing options you can choose (all described in the rules and on the website) – it all depends how competitive you want the game to be and how long you want it to last.  The basic game normally lasts between 25-45 minutes but you can make this shorter or longer – it’s up to you!

Come Forth and Multiply!
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Deck Delver is a solitaire dungeon crawler type that plays with a small deck. A portion of the cards are dealt face down in a variety of layouts such as "the Pyramid solitaire game", and then players will have a hand of 5 cards which will act as their inventory of potions and other items.

To explore, simply uncover a card that is free. If it is a monster, you need to fight it by playing a card from your hand, adding the bonus to beat at least 2 of its 3 stats. Once a monster beaten, it goes to your discard pile which in turn increases your inventory like a deck building game. On the other hand, if you miss then you remove cards from the top of your deck.

If your deck runs out its game over. The goal of the game is to reach the dungeon's boss and beat it to reclaim an artifact to become stronger for your next dungeon. In the rule book is included 8 different layouts you can set up the game.

Full color heavy ink version may be found in the link section since the file is too large to upload.

Rules have been updated to V1.3

There is an Expansion currently in the Alpha stage to include a multiplayer co-op rules and new cards too see details below

boardgame: Body Builders: A Tale of Taxidermy
description: In the light strategy game Body Builders, 3-6 players assume the role of lonely taxidermists who recruit vultures, hyenas, crocodiles and other critters to scavenge for animal parts that can be combined to build themselves a friend. Players recruit these scavengers with dice through a combination of choice and chance, and they use this army of scavengers to invade opposing taxidermists to try to steal the parts they need to complete their new pal and thus win the game.

From Publisher Information

Diddly

The Game of squat!

In this card game less is better, but Diddly is best. On a turn, draw a card to replace a card. Match cards to make them worth Diddly. There’s Nothing to it! But wait, there’s more! (Or is it less?) Special cards let you peek at your face-down cards, dig through the discard pile, swap your cards, or halve the value of a card. Look Ma, no hands!! Cards are played in front of a player not held in a hand.

Diddly is an easy and addictive game for children and adults. To win Diddly, stop at nothing… or as close as you can!

Ages: 6 and Up
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Game Information:
This game is intended for 2-6 players, ages 7 and up, preferably with an adult. Calculators can be a helpful tool and are allowed to be incorporated for use. A game should take 30-45 minutes from start to finish.
Players take turns playing out their hands of numbers and sign cards to get their totals as close to the randomly selected red target number card.

Object:
The first player to win five or more red target cards is the winner.

Monster Math cards are some of the best learning aids for mathematics, as opposed to flash cards which have a suggested problem preformed on the card. Monster Math allows the tutor to rearrange the numbers and signs so that the student can see how they would interact in real time. By simply moving a number from the bottom of the equation to the top, the student would get to experience and make the connections themselves as to how the math interacts with the numbers and signs, and the tutor can demonstrate the relationship of numbers and signs with proficiency so that the student could repeat the process for themselves.

Romans vs Britons plays out an ambush of a small troop of Roman soldiers marching along a Roman road sometime in the mid first century AD. They are in a valley when they spot fearsome Britons coming down from the surrounding hills.  Orthogonal movement and custodial capture Captains (2 each) can capture more that one enemy. Three different levels of win depending on whether the loser manages to escape, surrender or is entirely defeated.  Leather board and wooden gaming pieces.
family: Series: Play with History (Gothic Green Oak)

description: The Defence of Pictland plays out a battle between Northumbrian warriors aiming to expand their Kingdom northwards into Pictland in the seventh century. The Picts however are more than ready to defend their land and meet up with their enemy in a boggy valley. Movement and capture differ on dry land (knight's move and replacement) and in the bog (orthogonal and custodial). Winner captures all opposing men.  Leather board and wooden gaming pieces.
family: Series: Play with History (Gothic Green Oak)

description: Vikings vs Saxons plays out a skirmish between the Saxon settlers of dark age Britain and incoming Vikings some time in the 8th century. They meet at a bridge and fight to the death. There are three categories of men: Swords-men (movement: ortho/diag 3/2 squares); Axe-men (movement: ortho/diag 2/1 squares); Spear-men (movement: knights move). Two levels of winning depending on whether the loser surrenders/escapes or is entirely defeated. Leather board and wooden gaming pieces.
family: Series: Play with History (Gothic Green Oak)

description:
War in the Welsh Marches represents a battle between a Marches Lord and Welsh Raiders on land between two castles in the frontier territory of the borderlands between England and Wales in the 12th century. Movement orthogonal; capture by short leap. Aim to capture the opposing chief or to enter three men into the opposing castle square. 
Leather board and wooden gaming pieces.
family: Series: Play with History (Gothic Green Oak)

description: Royalists vs Roundheads represents a battle in the first few years of the Civil Wars of the 17th century.  Royalists and Roundheads line up their Cavaliers, Pikemen and Musketeers and the battle begins.  Three types of men each with different movement: Pikemen (movement: ortho/diag forwards only 1 square, capture by replacement); Musketeers (movement: ortho/diag 3/2, capture by replacement); Cavaliers  (movement: ortho/diag 1 square, capture by long leap). Aim to capture opposing general ( a Cavalier). Leather board and wooden gaming pieces.
family: Series: Play with History (Gothic Green Oak)

description: Tudor Galleons at War represents a sea battle between old enemies, England and France, at the beginning of modern naval warfare in the Tudor period. Three classes of ship each with differing degrees of  orthogonal and diagonal movement. Capture is custodial but depends on the size of the ships capturing and the ship being captured. Winner is the player with the most points in the navy that survives the battle. Leather board and wooden gaming pieces.
family: Series: Play with History (Gothic Green Oak)

description: Tomb Robbers and Temple Raiders plays out a clash between two groups of treasure hunting robbers and raiders amongst the temple ruins of Egypt in the 1920s. All men on both sides move by the knight's move (x1 or x2) and capture by  alignment. Secret passages allow an extra degree of movement across the board. Two levels of win depending on whether the losing side can allow their last man to escape or is defeated entirely. Leather board and wooden gaming pieces.
family: Series: Play with History (Gothic Green Oak)

boardgame: Lie Your Face Off!: Your Go-To Lies
description:
A mini-expansion to Lie Your Face Off! These are the lies that have always gotten you out of tricky situations, even when you were a baby.

If you don't have a Go-To Lie in you hand and you have to draw a card, you draw a Go-To Lie. Playing a Go-To Lie will always make you draw another one. These lies are more powerful than their base game counterparts, but if either the base game or the expansion deck runs out of cards, the game ends. Additionally, you'll need to complete another story for every expansion deck that you are using to end the game.

"18 giugno 1815: Waterloo" is a hex and counter wargame simulation of the battle of Waterloo, for two opponents and two possible difficulty levels.

The counters represent all the units that actually took part into the battle. A unit is normally at regimental level.

Game length is between 10 and 12 turns. Each turn corresponds to 60 minutes of real time.

Victory is calculated according to points gained for fulfilled objectives minus points lost following the elimination of the gamer's own units.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
K-Day: Kaiju War! is an action point allowance system solo dice game. You will need a print out of the game sheet, 11 6-sided dice (6 in 1 color, 5 in another color) and tokens to represent the kaijus (giant monsters).  You play Zillasaur, a kaiju on a rampage to wreck havoc in Japan.  Getting in your way are pesky human defense force and Guardian Kaijus that emerge to roam and protect the land.

To win the game, Zillasaur needs to score 300 victory points by destroying cities and battling the Guardian Kaijus, and stay alive at the end of 14 days.  At the start of each day, Zillasaur has 4 action points to either move, attack cities, gain hit points or engage in a Kaiju Battle using various modes of dice roll.

Will you be able to survive the tank onslaughts, deluge of air strikes, and ferocious kaiju-on-kaiju battles?
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
"La Battaglia del Lago Ghiacciato" (The Iced Lake Battle) is a paper-miniatures wargame based on the battle of Lake Peipus (1242) as inspired by Sergej Ejzenstejn's movie "Alexandr Nevskij".

The map is divided into squares and the units are beautiful stand-up paper soldiers (that the players have to cut and mount) designed by Guido Crepax, representing Russians and Teutonic knights.

Victory is gained when a certain difference between players' points is reached or, alternatively, with the elimination of one of the two armies. Points are assigned according to the quality and the quantity of eliminated enemy units.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Children's educational game about European flags. Players roll two dice with a colour on each face, and must find a tile showing a flag containing the two resulting colours, and then place it on the right space on the map which forms the game board. Each correct match and placement yields a coin; identifying and placing one's own country's flag gives a bonus coin in addition to this. The player who has collected the most coins when all tiles have been placed is the winner.

No upper limit on the number of players is given or implied; at least up to five players seems reasonable.

A fantasy based CCG set in a chinese/medieval universe.

From the Xi Cards website:

"Set in the 'World of Xi', Xi cards lets you battle with four factions and two ancient powers to determine the fate of the planet."
The game uses "time" units as a resource which allows players to draw cards, play spells and save time for future turns.

Players win by reducing their opponents from 40 "life points" to 0.
Xi Cards has four card types, "units", "point spells", "lasting spells" and "statements".

The game is shown and explained as a two player game although the rules say that a multiplayer game is possible.

Exploration game based on the first two Norwegian children's television series about the eponymous "brødrene Dal" (the Dal brothers). Players paddle their canoes around a labyrinthine river, searching for crystals to power time machines needed to travel in time and space to places where the lost Dal brothers might be stuck.

Get ready for an ultimate Skylanders adventure as you battle Kaos and his minions. Place one of the fighting-action lenticular cards into your mover base and start exploring the 6 different islands. Defeat villains in order to collect keys and charms, but watch out! If Kaos shows up, all active Skylanders lose some of their power.

Contents: 6 island game boards, 32 lenticular Skylanders cards, 34 reward tiles, 34 challenge tiles, 8 starter activation tokens, 4 keys, 4 card holder movers, game die, rules.

From the box:

Play the hit NBC tv game show at home.

Match 12 complete strangers with their own identity as you try to collect the most money and win.

Contents: 100 stranger cards, 12 card stands, instructions and solutions booklet, wipe off money board, easel, wipe off pen and guessing pad.

The 300 Mile Race Game is a board game which simulates a stock car race with 30 participants.  The object of the game is to pick the winning car from among the 30 cars in the game.  Dice are rolled against a sequence of tables to determine which cars get to advance around the track.  The tables also have results which can cause cars to suffer pit stops, crashes, or mechanical failures.  The first car around the track wins, along with the player who picked that car (if any).

Players strive to become the surrogate of Luxembourg's national comic superhero: Superjhemp. To do so, they must fulfill missions and take control of Luxembourg's cantons by proving their knowledge about Luxembourg's history and present.

In this trivia game, every player draws a secret mission card at the start of the game. This tells the player which cantons (pairs of spaces on the map) have to be controlled by the player in order to win.

Players traverse the map from north to south. Every time they land on one space, a card from that space is drawn and a random trivia question is asked. If the player knows the answer, he can put a marker on the space. If both spaces from one canton are marked, the player controls that canton. It is possible for players to wrest control of a half or full canton away from a player controlling them.

Players try to gather Maja's friends together.

Every player has a board with eight characters depicting Maja's friends. Each character has two slots for a colored pin to place in. The winner is the first player to place a pin in all sixteen slots.

To place a pin, the current player spins the central spinner. There are spaces for the eight characters, Maja, and a lightning bolt. If Maja is the result, the player may place two pins in any slots. If the lightning bolt occurs, the player must remove two pins. If any of the eight characters are spun, the player places one pin on the character.

Players may also place a pawn near one spinner space to potentially be able to place two pins on the character, if it is spun.

The goal of Mutiny is to stay alive while eliminating your opponents. Keeping your allies safe is also a good idea. But... better them than you right? You’re a pirate after all!

Players are given role cards to determine their allegiance and who they will play as for the game. Players can be divided into 3 groups, Traitors, Loyal shipmates and the captain.

Traitors try to eliminate the captain, who is known by everyone, and the loyal mates while the shipmates try to defend the captain and kill all the traitors. The captain's goal is simply to stay alive, but since he's the only one oblivious to the other players loyalty, it can be harder than it looks.

The two main groups will then decide, one after the other, which meal they want to poison and once this is done the round can start. One after the other, players will switch their meals with one another. Once everyone did their action, everyone will reveal their meal card. Those who have been poisoned are removed from the game and those who survive continue to the next round.

This goes on until every traitors or mates has died or until the Captain dies.
Tram-Spel (Tram game) is about horsecars and was made in the early 1900's by Werner & Schumann, Berlin. It is typical roll and move board game. The first player who cross 63 stops, wins the game.

The Take is about crooked cops taking money. It is played over the course of several rounds. Each round, each player takes a bill(card) worth between 1 and 5 thousand dollars. In the second half of the game each player gets an opportunity to take an action after all players have taken their money for the round.

There are three ways to win the game:

    If the deck of cards runs out, the player with the most money wins at the end of the final round.
    If a player correctly accuses a player of having the most money,the accuser immediately wins.
    If a player correctly accuses another player of having the least money, the player with the most money wins.


There are 4 basic actions that players can take by spending money from their hand.

    Stakeout: Adjust turn order.
    Audit: Look at another player's cards.
    Accuse: Accuse a player of having the most or least money.
    Defame: Block a player from taking an action.

Back to school! Here comes the annual retreat for monsters from all hierarchies to sharpen their skills after playing bad guys in a variety of board games for the past year. The most anticipated event, of course, is the legendary lunchtime battle! No one wants to be sent off on an errand to buy lunch for all the others, so brace yourself and fight for your monster's dignity!

In Monster College: Lunchtime Battle, each player chooses one monster from his hand to join the "counting circle" each turn. Based on the die roll and a counting process, 1-3 unfortunate monsters are fingered to buy lunch for others (earning minus points along the way for the corresponding players) — but these candidate monsters are so childish that in later turns they'll use their special abilities to bend the counting rules in their favor. In the end, the player whose collected candidate monsters show the most overall "hunger" loses the game and must go buy lunch (or whatever else they agreed to before the game) for everyone else!

一年一度的進修日又到了，平時在各大遊戲扮演稱職壞蛋的魔物們再度回到校園，認真複習各種魔物必修技能。好不容易撐過早上的課程，魔物們最期待的午餐時間終於到了！今年究竟是哪個班級的魔物要當跑腿小弟去幫大家買便當呢？這場年度便當大戰即將揭開序幕！

In Dystopian: the manhunt, one to four players assume the role of special agents of the Dystopia Police Department (called Sector 9), with the goal of investigating a murder case, finding the criminal, among four suspects and, eventually, catching him, it or her, before he gets away.
The agents will move around the megalopolis of Dystopia, investigating the numerous locations (distributed among districts and suburbs) meeting people, talking to them, gaining and finding hints and leads that make the investigation progress, bringing them closer and closer to the murderer.
Dystopia is a City controlled by two rival Corporations (Varcom and Infracorp), that are in the business of AI-Roids: artificially intelligent robots that live with the humans.
Dystopia features a plethora of many different locations, where the players will have to conduct their investigation.

Dystopian: the manhunt features the following mechanics: worker placement, variable player powers, action selection, network building, cooperative/solitaire play, press your luck.

The players will have to roll dice, and allocate them on their agent's sheet to gain these resources: 
1) warrants: to enter a location
2) attack: to respond to an attack from an encounter
3) evade: to hide from an attack from an encounter
4) interact: to interact with encounters or locations
5) analyze: to analyze hints and gain evidence about who committed the crime

Such resources will be used to deal with encounters the players will meet around the city, to advance their agent's abilities, ultimately, to catch the suspect who is found guilty.

The game is divided in 2 stages: the first is the Search, where players have to discovered who, among the 4 suspects, committed the crime; and the second, the Chase, where the suspect tries to hide in Dystopia, and it's up to the agents to seize him (it or her).

Rush is the primary title.

The entire title would be...

Colorado's Green

Rush

It is (as the name implies) about the 21st century rush currently underway in Colorado, meaning...

The legalization of Marijuana.

It is a game about trying to make money in this crazy industry.

It's like the Wild-Wild-West all over again!!!

Rush is an economic game that involves the fact that law enforcement is always angling to shut down your enterprise.

We here at Common Man Games have always taken a special interest in games that are themed after modern-day subject matters, and involve Law Enforcement.

We think this is a very interesting topic, and one that is evolving right before our eyes.

We began work on this game back in 2013 and hoped to release it in 2014, but getting it right has been a long slow process and one we don't want to "Rush" (sorry), so exactly when it will be released is a tricky question to answer, but please...

Stay Tuned!

Castle Rising is a German-style board game in which you choose an economic strategy to try to create the most prosperous kingdom. Players acquire resources (food, wood, stone, metal and gold) to train troops and construct buildings to earn benefits at different stages of the game and victory points to win the game.

Players can acquire resources in three ways, the main one being through your peasants, with each player starting with three. You decide which resources to produce, but some are easier to get than others, so you can play it safe or risk trying for a few more precious resources. You can also gain resources through rangers, which you must first train, which takes several units of food and wood.

The last way to gain resources is through the bidding phase. Each turn, all players bid secretly on one of five different resource bundles.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
A desperate tale of rodents and river crossings, Of Mice and Lemmings sees between five and eight players attempt to cross a thunderous stretch of water in order to reach the safe haven of a brand new cheese factory on the other bank. However, while the mice will be looking to fill their stomachs with all that high-quality dairy product, the lemmings have another idea: Scupper the bridges that are being built and plunge into the torrent below! Combining elements of both teamwork and individual play, you'll be looking to choose the right time to reveal your true nature while screwing over your opposite numbers.

In the game, players add cards to the central pool, numbered from 0 to 10. Depending on how many people are playing, a certain target number must be reached; 35, for example, with eight players. Each round, one player MUST reveal whether she is a mouse or lemming, then rely on her fellow rodents to get them all across the bridge — or not, as the case may be! Reveal too early and you could be putting too much faith in the other players; reveal too late and there's a good chance you may not be successful!

Of Mice and Lemmings is a quirky update on the trick-taking genre and adds hidden identities, a healthy amount of bluffing, and rodents with one-track minds.

This game challenges players to "Discover Hawai'i" better and quicker than their fellow 'travelers'. This is done by earning at least 500 Discovery Points and being the first one to return to the "Aloha Square"

The game consists of one board, one die, four pawns, one pack of "Kokua" Cards, One pack of "Pilikia" cards, Discovery Notes and Four Discovery Guides.

The board resembles a "Monopoly" Game board, but does not mimic the Monopoly game mechanism.

Magical Restaurant is a quick-playing game in which players are small restaurant owners, trying to cater to the varying tastes of idols from the nearby production office and capture their hearts. There are some eccentrics among the customers, so players have to carefully balance upgrading their restaurant furniture and food skills with feeding the passing customers.
