description:
Grid-Hammer is a tabletop game for 2 to 4 players that's played on a 9x9 square board called the "Grid". The object of the game is to move any one of your ten pieces across the Grid and past your opponents' defenses, then exit with this piece off the opposite side of the board.

Pieces move, climb, rotate, capture, transform, and combine into new hybrid pieces as they surge across the board and off the Grid.

Grid-Hammer is themed on the Grid itself. What would an ecosystem populated by grid creatures look like?

boardgame: Mise en Place: The Culinary Knowledge Game
description: Mise en Place: The Culinary Knowledge Game is a fun and educational game designed to be played by up to six players or teams. Players are immersed in a restaurant environment where they encounter nearly 1200 questions based on culinary terms and concepts. Players begin as entry-level employees and are prompted to answer questions in each of the six certification areas in order to be promoted to Apprentice, Sous Chef, and finally to Executive Chef to win the game. Along the path to promotion, players encounter tally it up questions on measurements, recipe conversions, and the use of fractions and percentages.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Battle Mice is a near-perfect information card game for two players.

The deck consists of three identical sets of mice numbered 1–8. Each player receives one deck, then the last "neutral" deck is shuffled, two of those cards are put aside, and the rest dealt three to each player. Each player then places his first eight mice in a 3x3 grid with the middle square left empty. The neutral mice are kept as a hand of cards.

On a player's turn, he can move one mouse one step. A mouse that moves into the middle empty square has escaped, and the opponent's mouse with the same number is defeated (turned over). Each mouse has a special ability that affects only the two adjacent mice; for example, the fighter mouse protects the adjacent mice from being defeated, the nurse mouse heals a defeated mouse, the speed mouse allows adjacent mice to move twice, and so on. Each time a mouse exits, the active player can place a neutral mouse from his hand on one of the corner squares, if available.

At the end of the game, players receive one point per escaped mouse, but some of the mice give an extra bonus depending on the order in which they escaped. This order is determined by the two neutral mice set aside at the start of the game.

GötzenDiener is a quick set-collecting card game.

The deck consists of five types of cards representing objects of worship, numbered 1–5, of various quantities. On a player's turn, he picks a card, then either keeps it or sends it to another player who cannot refuse the card. If a player gets a hand of four cards, he must discard all cards of any one type from his hand, applying the effect on those card(s).

The game ends when either the deck runs out, someone has a hand of three identical cards, or someone discards a 5. All players then show their hands, earning points for the most common object of worship in hand and the runner-up, and being penalized for everything else, those objects being heretical symbols.

In Cavern Crawl, players take on the role of one of 4 adventurers to explore the caverns, fight monsters and collect loot. It can be played either cooperatively or competitively.

Players start on the same "Start" card, and have slightly different attack, search and defense abilities depending on their character. On their turn, they first draw a map card and connect it to the already placed cards, expanding the caverns and creating more opportunities for battles and loot.

Combat and gaining coins works through dice rolls. Players have to beat certain defence scores of the enemies in their roll, and they gain the associated rewards if they're successful. This can also include treasure cards which provide bonuses and extra abilities.

Players can pool their dice rolls together if they are on the same space, to make it easier to gain rewards or defeat monsters. In this case, the rewards are split.

Players can also attack each other for the chance to steal coins from their opponents.

The goal of the game is to be the first to find 10 coins and make it back to the entrance. Each character has an additional secondary objective which provides an alternative winning condition.

Fisherman's Profit is a simple bluffing "outsmart the others" card game based on the Japanese proverb "If snipe and seashell fight, the fisherman profits", meaning that if two struggle, a third party wins.

From a deck of either blue or red seashells of different values, three cards are placed face down on the play area, and in turn, players peek at the unoccupied seashells, then place either a fisherman or a snipe on one of them. When all have placed, the seashells are turned up and resolved: Blue seashells can be taken only by snipes, and red only by the fishermen. If two have placed on the same, the latter will steal a previously claimed seashell from the former — but if both fisherman and snipe are on the same seashell, the fisherman profits according to the old proverb and both gets the seashell and catches the bird.

User summary:

A simple he-thinks-that-I-think card game.

Players have a set of cards numbered 1–6, where 6 is the king and 1 is a simple pawn. The back of each card is the Pariah, which is zero.
Each turn, players roll a die each and may play face down any card of that number or lower. All turned face up, and you count number of wins. The King wins over all cards except the fool (2), all other cards win over the fool. If a player has played a card higher than the number he rolled, he cannot turn the card, and it's a Pariah, which loses to all. Each win is awarded 1 point.

Every card has a special power, that usually affects his ability to play next turn. For example, the Pariah allows the player to not roll the die, but choose die result for the next turn.

This is repeated for 5 rounds, which counts as one hand. The player with most points after three hands wins the game.

User summary:

A solitaire card game based on the concept of rhythmic video games.

The player has a band of five dancers from a selection of 11, numbered 1–5. The dancers are put in a line, where each dancer is either in the front row or back row. From a shuffled deck of notes also numbered 1–5, five cards are lined up. Each turn, the cards of the lest represented colour will "fall down" (are slid down a bit) and must be "caught" by the team of dancers. However, in order to achieve a "perfect rhythm", a note must be caught by a dancer with the same number, if caught by a dancer with a number at most 1 more or less than the note is "good" if the note and the dancer are of the same colour; anything else is a "fail".
Before each catch, the player can only move one dancer one step, either from back row to front row, or sideways, in which case they swap places with whoever was beside them. However, moving sideways to a spot where no dancer is is not counted as a move, and will also cause the dancer who now ends up in the same column as the moved one, to move in the opposite direction, a so-called "cross move".

Being in the back row gives the dancer one extra turn to catch the note. Also, apart from the current row of falling notes, the player will have another row of notes above it, enabling the player to plan his moves one turn ahead.

The final score is decided by the number of perfects and good rhythm catches. Also, before each game, the player chooses which song to dance to; each song having a special effect on the game. Each dancer also has a special power to activate once per game.

D.D. Dogma is a party-style card game simulating the back-and-forth dialogue between roleplayers and the game master in an RPG session.

Each player selects a team of heroes from the available line-up, then receives six quest cards and a number of play cards that signify dice numbers.

Taking turns, players ask an opponent for a quest; that player unveils one of his quests, then the asking player tries to solve the quest by rolling as many dice as he has heroes, activating their abilities to generate resources such as "battle", "magic", "thievery" and so on. Each quest has its own clear requirements, and if the player manages to generate the correct resources, he gains the victory points the quest generates; if not, these points go to the quest owner.

After the dice roll, players can adjust the values of the dice by playing cards from their hands, either elevating, lowering or re-rolling the dice rolled. After this is done, the dice are applied to the lineup of heroes, activating different abilities.

For the first time, a game about Marshall amps. Explore the exciting life of Jim Marshall as the Father of Loud, a great musician and champion engineer.

The only Marshall trivia game. 50 years of rock history. A tribute to the gods of riffs.

The loudest rock game ever.


1,600 trivia questions ranging from early rock and metal to current artists. All questions have three levels of difficulty: Poser, Fan and Scientist. All players are always involved since they bet on each other’s rock knowledge.

The goal is to be the first to finish one lap around the board through the use of rock knowledge, clever betting and a bit of luck. Players answer questions about anecdotes, myths, rockers, albums, songs and Sex, Amps & Rock ‘n’ Roll. You can also be asked to hum the riffs of well-known rock songs.

Features:

Authentic Rock Science Marshall Tortex game paws by DUNLOP, made exclusively for this game.

Inspired by the classic Marshall look of the 50th anniversary amps, the game even has its own Marshall model number.

Exclusive limited edition poster, Rock Science Marshall picks and Marshall stack card holders, found only in this game.

Can you tell a good story?

In the board game Story, you and your opponents match pictures, explain why they belong together and then vote for the player who told the best story. The reason why your picture is the best match is up to you. No explanation is more correct than another.

After each round, the players show who they have voted for and points are given accordingly. The player who receives the most votes is awarded 1 point. Every player who has voted for that player’s story receives 3 points. Each player receives between 0 and 4 points each turn.

The player that first reaches 20 points or more wins.

Trivia game about Stockholm with 1500 questions.

The object of the game is to be the first to claim an area of the city by answering questions or being lucky with the dice.

Event cards can be used to steal questions and more.

In Yumekaki, players play cards and tell a story that intertwines together. Cards have special symbols in their drawings that tell the player in what way they should tell their part of the story, e.g., telling the story in ten syllables, creating a dialogue with the items in the art, or telling an unfinished story that the next player must pick up. The game ends when players play a card with a popped bubble indicator the second time.

The game comes in two sets: the Starbox and the Moonbox. They can be combined for a larger game.

/Intro_Start

Welcome to the Crash 'Em Arena. It is in this simulation that players compete in a mechanized brawl by controlling their assigned Crash 'Em Bot. This is done by inputting /Programs (/Program cards)  into their Bot to initiate commands for movement, attacking an opponent, or even hacking their systems in order to sabotage their plans. Once enough points are awarded, usually through beating on opponents, the combatant who earns those points first is declared the victor!

Let the games begin.

/End_Intro

Rune Crash is an abstract board game from 1 to 4 players where the goal is to have the most knowledge points at the end of the game.

Combine 3 or more runes in a line and get the knowledge points. Explore the power of the special runes and be careful with the bombs that will sabotage your discoveries.

In the Solitary rules your objective is to get as many Dolmens as possible before the 40 round.

•••

Rune Crash es un juego abstracto jugable de 1 a 4 jugadores donde la meta es obtener la mayor cantidad de puntos al final del juego.

Combina 3 o mas runas en linea para obtener los puntos de conocimiento. Explora el poder de las runas especiales y ten cuidado con las bombas que sabotearan tus descubrimientos.

En el modo solitario el objetivo es obtener la mayor cantidad de dolmenes antes del round 40.

Its not easy to be a Master of Evil. The working hours suck... Finding efficient minions is a pain... and keeping your well-earned treasure safe from the hands of the so called "Heroes" ends up being a full-time job. But... this is YOUR JOB and fame in the Inner Circle of Evil is everything to you so why not show the other Masters how a demonic job is really done! Masters of Evil is a game for 1 to 6 players where each player takes the role of an Evil Master that is repelling the attack of a variety of heroes trying to gain as much reputation as possible by imprisoning the heroes when defeated.

At the beginning of the game 5 hero cards are dealt to each player so 3 can be chosen. Those heroes will be attacking your Den. Each turn a Monster is Summoned and you will have to place one of your minion tokens on one of your heroes that have that monster symbol. If you complete a card by filling all the monster, traps, and spells symbols you defeat that hero and can claim it as a reward by imprisoning him/her into your dungeon cell. Some heroes provide a special reward when defeated... from adding an extra monster to getting a special Overlord ability.

After placing minions a die is rolled to determine if a type of hero attacks. To repel an attack you can remove monsters from that hero card for a value equal, bigger or lower than the hero attack (defined by the roll of 1D6). If you repel the complete attack you are undamaged. If you repel a portion you can get the rest of the damage by reducing your life (which begins with 10). Also you can simply nullify the attack of a hero by distracting them with one of your treasures. Spells can be used to change the summoned monster type and Traps can nullify any attack (even if they are not in the card of an attacking hero).

When the 6th hero is collected by any player the game is over and the scoring phase begins. Players get the points in the hero cards but also get points for the most types of each monster and if they collect at least 3 cards of a Hero Type.


No es facil ser un Señor del Mal. Las horas a las que hay que levantarse apestan... Encontrar sirvientes eficientes es un dolor de medula... Y conservar tu "bien merecido" tesoro de las garras de los heroes termina siendo un trabajo full-time. Pero es tu OFICIO y la fama adquirida en el circulo Interno de la Maldad lo es todo para ti... asi que porque no mostrar a los demas Señores como se hace un buen trabajo demoniaco! En los Señores del Mal los jugadores toman el rol de Señores Oscuros que estan repeliendo el ataque de audaces heroes aprisionandolos cuando son derrotados para asi ganar la tan preciada fama.

Al comienzo del juego se entregaran 5 cartas de heroe a cada jugador de las que seran elegidas 3. Esos heroes atacaran tu Guarida. Cada turno sera Invocado un Monstruo y tendras que colocar un token de minion en uno de los heroes que tenga en su carta a dicho monstruo disponible. Si completas todos los slots de la carta derrotas al heroe y puedes reclamar la recompensa aprisionandolo en una celda. Algunos heroes proveen recompensas especiales cuando son derrotados... desde otorgar un monstruo extra a darle al Overlord una habilidad permanente.

Luego de colocar los minions se tira un dado para determinar el tipo de heroe que atacara. Para repeler un ataque se pueden remover monstruos de la carta de dicho heroe por un valor igual, superior o inferior que el ataque del heroe (definido por un D6). Si repeles completamente el ataque no eres dañado. Si solo se repele una porcion recibes el resto del daño reduciendo tu vida (que comienza en 10). Tambien puedes anular un ataque entregandole uno de tus tesoros. Los hechizos pueden ser usados para invocar un monstruo diferente del que se ha invocado y las Trampas anulan cualquier ataque (incluso si no estan en la carta del heroe atacante)

Cuando un jugador aprisiona a su sexto heroe el juego termina y comienza la ronda de puntaje. Los jugadores obtienen puntos por cada heroe capturado, pero tambien por mayoria de determinado tipo de monstruo y variedad de heroes.

You remember the outside world and you remember liking it. But by now, you’ve been in Camp 16 so long, you’re forgetting what it looked and felt like. Inside the camp you face certain death, outside the camp you face starvation, sickness, bitter cold, enemy territory, and even death at the hands of your own comrades. Struggle is life. In Prisoner’s Dilemma: Camp 16 you play as either Prisoner #75044 or  Prisoner #44233, two prisoners unaware of their crime, and hungry for freedom.

In this two player game, each player has a prisoner, and chooses one of three possible win conditions (Martyr/Survivalist/Comrade).  Players will take turns reading a scenario, then bargaining with their fellow prisoner to come to a conclusion about whether to choose a selfless [o] path or a selfish [x] path. Based on the combination of the two player’s choices, players will take or gain damage according to the Results table beneath the scenario. If the players survive and meet their individual win conditions, they win. If they die, they lose. You must make good decisions to keep yourself alive, though sometimes that may mean making personal sacrifices in order to stay alive in the long run.

description:
Your goal in Sexism is to move from the doll house to the White House, with the first player to cross the finish line winning. What you win, however, depends on your gender and whether you proceed to the "Man's Goal" or the "Woman's Goal". If you win as a woman, the game instructs others that "You are now a person and must be treated as such for 24 hours"; if a man, you receive a cartoon of a large thumb pushing a woman down and a declaration that "Congratulations, you've won — or have you?"

Gameplay consists mostly of drawing cards, reading the text on them (e.g., "You're all for women's liberation, but nobody's going to catch YOU going to a woman doctor. Go back one step."), then moving forward or backward as dictated. Some cards require the active player to act out a role, then others guess who or what the player was trying to be.

The game rules suggest playing as the opposite gender "because this can cause some hilarious role-playing situations".

boardgame: A Game of Thrones: The Card Game – Spoils of War
description:
"Your prize will be the doom of you. Krakens rise from the sea, Theon, or did you forget that during your years among the wolves?"
    –George R.R. Martin, A Clash of Arms

Spoils of War is the first Chapter Pack in the "Conquest and Defiance" cycle for A Game of Thrones: The Card Game. In Spoils of War, each of the six Great Houses of Westeros enjoy the plunder and glories of their victories...even as those trophies place them at risk to lose their positions of power. Among the Chapter Pack's sixty cards (three copies each of twenty individual cards), you'll find captives, spies, vengeful schemes, righteous fury, and deadly, crippling treachery.

Many of the new cards from Spoils of War feature the "prized" keyword, which balances tremendous risks and rewards in deck-building and play. These cards lend material, in-game value to the riches you plunder, the captives you seize, and the castles you successfully siege. But can you hold them once you win them? Prized cards give your opponents power when they enter your discard or dead piles, so it’s up to you to leverage your spoils toward victory!
family: Books: A Song of Ice and Fire
Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Game: A Game of Thrones – The Card Game (LCG)

description:
Portholes is a space strategy game set in the LUNA-2 space prison.

Plot: A digital voice: "auto-destruct sequence initiated"... The doors of the prison cells open automatically. Four convicts: thieves, assassins, tax evaders and politicians, have to find a way to ignite the safety pod engines and flee LUNA-2 space station.

Gameplay: miniature tactical game with cards, equip items, custom dice combat system and tile portals.

One of the players controls the guards, and the other player(s) control the convicts.

GOAL: Find the the RED key needed to reach the safety pod, place the "Power cell", re-activate KAL 9000, ignite the engines and flee LUNA-2 space prison.

If the convicts' HP reach zero, the guards win.

The Choose One! Promo Pack is a pack of seven extra cards to add to your Choose One! game. Great for handing out to people to give them an idea of how the game plays, or as a stocking stuffer or small gift for someone who already has the main game.

The Choose One Promo Pack includes the following cards:

     Bert or Ernie
     Spinach or Broccoli
     Born with superpowers or Transformed as an adult
     Kirk or Picard
     Religion or Politics
     Goatee or Sideburns
     Nightmare about falling or Nightmare about being chased

Aka Fuji is a dice-based game that uses 15 numbered craft sticks. The sticks have illustrations on either side: a red Mount Fuji on one side, and a blue Mount Fuji on the other. Six cards, separated into three red Mount Fuji cards and three blue, are passed out to players. The players' goal is to use the custom die results to turn the sticks over to the side that matches their card. The player, or team, who first flips all the sticks to the side shown on their card wins.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Deductio is a French card game for 2-8 players.

The rules of the game

Beginner

Choose the number of cards you want to play with. To start, 3 cards per player are dealt.

Example : you have 3 players and you decide to play with 3 cards. Take the numbered cards from 1 to 10. Mix them up, and distribute them face down. The last card is placed onto the table.

The oldest player begins. He multiplies a couple of his cards and then announces the result.

Example : the first player has cards 2, 5 and 8. Consequently he can associate 3 couples of cards : 2 and 5, 2 and 8, 5 and 8. He chose the first and declares : "10".

His opponents have to deduce which couple it is.

Example : to obtain 10, you can either have the 2 and the 5, or the 1 and the 10. In that case, the second player has the 1 : thus he validates the first hypothesis.

Players take turns in a clockwise direction declaring a multiple, until one player has identified which card is on the table. He says "Deductio" and declares the number of the hidden card without showing the card to the others. If he is right, he scores a point. If he can identify the cards in each player's hand without any mistake, he scores a bonus point, and the hand is over. If he is wrong, he loses 1 point and the hand goes on.

The first player to score 10 points wins the game.

Experienced

Play with 4 cards or more following the same rules. The number of possible couples (for example, 6 couples for 4 cards) and the multiples related to each value increase.

In turns, each player can play the joker card. He puts it in front of him and turns it face down once used. It will allow him to pass his turn once without declaring any number. It can be played at any time.

In the not too distant future the world is ruled by massive corporations that wage war with each other over the control of empires. Literal wars fought by private armies proved to be overly costly in resources, profits, and public opinion, so the Wolf & Phoenix Marketing Conglomerate introduced an out-of-the-box way for corporate entities to resolve their differences: The Hostile Takeover Arena. Corporations that have reached an impasse in negotiations can avoid expensive, lengthy legal battles or risky sabotage by simply hiring a team of trained warriors to out-maneuver the competition in a quick, bloody, ratings-exploding spectacle.

In Hostile Takeover, players control a mercenary team trying to outwit and outgun the competition. Each Character has strengths and weaknesses that must be wisely used to gain the upper hand. Can you lead your team of mercenaries to victory amid the chaos of battle?

Hostile Takeover is played on a modular board (usually 30”x30”) filled with 1” Squares. Force fields and other terrain features are placed on the board to simulate a sci-fi battle arena. Players each use their team of 4 mercenaries to accomplish scenario specific objectives that earn them victory points. The first player to earn enough victory points (determined by scenario and number of players) is the winner. Each scenario is specifically designed to bring all teams together in a flurry of combat.

At the start of each Round players select an Action Card (Move, Melee, Fire or Aim/Overwatch) for each of their unwounded characters. Players also assign 4 Energy Tokens to their characters. Energy Tokens are used to boost the characters in a variety of ways and also enable the use of each character’s unique Special Action. Players then roll for Initiative. Finally, players go in Initiative order activating 1 of their characters. This continues until all characters have acted or become pinned or wounded. There is a quick Clean Up step at the end of the round and then a new round begins.

Ranged and Melee Combat checks are handled by rolling 3d6 and trying to equal or exceed a given target number. Each Character has a chart describing their ranged and melee weapons that quickly and easily provides the target number to roll against based on a variety of factors.

Augustus: Maximus Promo Card is a single card representing (as you might expect) the Emperor Augustus, who is now entering the game himself to aid one of the players.

During game set-up, the youngest player takes this card and places it in front of himself. During the game, when a token drawn from the bag doesn't allow the holder of Augustus to place a legion on a card, this player can choose to place one legion on a card (or move one legion), then pass the Augustus Maximus card to another player.

Mechabrick is a range of precision molded plastic kits to convert your minifigs into 1/144 scale fighting robots or 'mecha'. You can upgrade your mechs with weapons and accessories and then battle for supremacy in a fast-paced board game using buildings and scenery built from your favourite plastic toy building bricks.

The first game in the Mechabrick product line is Gangs of Neon City. Set in the back-streets of a futuristic far-eastern metropolis the game uses durable plastic gaming boards (supplied in the game box) printed with a brick-standard grid pattern (4-bricks square) upon which movement and shooting range of the robots is measured.

It's up to you and your imagination to build, change and rebuild the buildings and scenery on your game board from plastic toy building bricks and create your perfect battle environment.

The game sets two or more players in a head-to-head battle of turn-based play using 'action points' to choose whether each robot should move, shoot or both in each turn. Customisation and upgrades to your robots are key to a decisive win, but be careful not to weigh down your mech with too many upgrades and make them too slow to fight!

The printed rulebook supplied with the boxed game is only 4 pages long and is designed to be fun and fast-paced for all the family. We will be publishing free add-ons and expanded rules for the game to keep even the most hardened gamers interested for many years to come.

The boxed game includes four of the 'Wakazashi-Class' mech kits (two black and two white), four plastic game boards, a decal sheet to customize your mechs and your scenery, two D6 dice and a full rule/instruction booklet.

Zeppeldrome, is a serious steampunk race through silly obstacles with a hazardous, modular board and cards that are used to help you or mess with opponents as you all race to be the first to travel the length of the airship raceway.

Game-Play
Each turn, players look at the top half of the cards in their hands and choose one that shows the route they will attempt to take that turn in their effort to get past (or through) silly, yet hazardous, obstacles. The players reveal the chosen flight plan cards simultaneously.

Next, each player uses the often silly, but always strategic, actions on as many of their remaining cards as they like. These actions will either disrupt their opponents, or help themselves (or both).

Once card-play is finished, the last-place player moves any obstacles that need moving on the board (such as the falling Lemmings in the "Tragic Lemming 'Migration'"), and then the players move their dirigibles along the now-final path that resulted from their initial plan, plus cards that were played on them.

As players move, obstacles on the board (including the other players’ dirigibles) affect movement in positive or negative ways (depending on how skillful and lucky the players were in their card-play).

The first player over the finish line is the winner!

description:
These are additional cards made by gaming industry veterans and bonus cards that can be added to enhance Adventurer: The Card Game. They were made available from retailers, web orders, and other venues:


     Golden Portal: Found only in the Limited Edition Castle Deck 
     Shade the Trickster: This is a Guest Designer Card by Gary Gygax. This is available from the website and from select retailers. We also have the great honor in knowing this is also the very last thing Gary designed before he passed away in March. 
     Warden's Pool of Darkness: This is a Guest Designer Card by James M. Ward and pays homage to the great gold box Dungeons and Dragons game he help create to the computer. This is available from the website and from select retailers. 
     Blunderbuss: This is a Guest Designer Card by Chris Clark. This is available from the website and from select retailers. 
     Slooch the Cleric: This is a Guest Designer Card by Tom Wham. This is available from the website and from select retailers. 
     Count Lodrac: These are extra Lodrac cards that you can add to your game to increase the chance of finding the vampire and likewise speeding up the game. This is available from the website and from select retailers. 
     Lodrac's Twin: This is an extra hero card that you can add to your deck to allow for more players.. This is available from the website and from select retailers. 
     Crown of Immortals: This card is free when you refer a friend to buy the game.

Invasion Z is a Zombie Survival Game. The story is about an outbreak of Zombie-like creatures after a Meteor disintegrates in the atmosphere raining a strange color into Buenos Aires City. This rain causes the population to get sick and then die, only to become the dreaded ghouls that crave for the flesh.

Gameplay has 5 basic steps:

1) MAINTENCANCE: Operatives untap all the used items and Profile card, and Mission penalties are applied to the main Counters.

2) OPERATIVES STEP: Heroes move around the board, hunting zombies, rescueing people, finding useful items and trying to avoid the inevitable fate that awaits them.

3) ZOMBIES MOVE: All zombies move closer to the base in the center of the board.

4) EVENTS STEP: Depending on the levels of the Event Counter one or two events might happen. A special die is rolled and the event takes effect.

5) ZOMBIE STEP: Depending on the levels of the Zombie Counter one or two extra zombies will be added to the Zombie Roll. Usually 1 die per rescuer is roll (between 3 and 4 die total) plus any extra die added by the Zombie Counter placing zombies in the suburbs of the map. A 6 rolled is a miss and do not adds any zombie.

The objective of the game is to rescue the 8 survivors that stand hidden on the locations of the outer ring of the board and then return to the center tile to be retrieved by the rescue team.

The Heroes lose if at any time there are 3 zombies on the central location tile.

The Items deck works as a counter that will increase the difficulty as the game progresses with some "bad luck" cards that can be a devastating force against the heroes.

There are several ways of losing the game and only one way to win so usually players will feel very rewarded when they win as it´s usually on the edge of oblivion.

description: Superfight: The Green Deck — an expansion for the Superfight party game in which players pit unusual characters with superpowers and superweaknesses against one another — consists of one hundred kid-friendly cards from fairy tales to cartoons to food weapons.

Superfight: The Red Deck — an expansion for the Superfight party game in which players pit unusual characters with superpowers and superweaknesses against one another — adds "one hundred really terrible and likely offensive cards" to the base game.

Superfight: The Blue Deck — an expansion for the Superfight party game in which players pit unusual characters with superpowers and superweaknesses against one another — consists of one hundred cards that feature locations in which the characters can fight, from comic book conventions to the wild west to oceans of gelatin.

Superfight: The Purple Deck — an expansion for the Superfight party game in which players pit unusual characters with superpowers and superweaknesses against one another — consists of one hundred scenario cards that feature everything from land mines to kitten stampedes to disabling electric devices, adding an extra level of hilarious fun to every round.

Superfight: The Orange Deck — an expansion for the Superfight party game in which players pit unusual characters with superpowers and superweaknesses against one another — consists of one hundred cards that add geek-friendly subjects to the fights, from sci-fi to fantasy to comic book nerdery.

Superfight: The Hero Machine Deck — an expansion for the Superfight party game in which players pit unusual characters with superpowers and superweaknesses against one another — adds one hundred new cards to your Superfight game that take your available characters in a whole new direction. The cards are split into three decks. Each deck contains words that combine to create a character. When a player plays a "TO THE HERO MACHINE" card, he or she chooses one card from each Hero Machine deck.


    Deck 1 contains words like: Super, Giant, Flying, Retired, Flaming, Mutant
    Deck 2 contains words like: Monkey, Zombie, Girlfriend, Dino, Bat, Hero, Sumo
    Deck 3 contains words like: Assassin, Swarm, Babies, Cyborg, Army, Duo, Love Triangle


Players choose one card from each deck, and place them in order to generate combinations such as "Flying Girlfriend Army", "Giant Zombie Babies", and "Retired Dino Love Triangle". This is a powerful and very funny deck, and will add thousands of possible characters to your game.

Dragon and Bandits is a fantasy-themed game in which the players are bandits who sneak around a sleeping dragon, trying to acquire treasure.

Cards accumulated can either be treasure, movement, or dragon movement cards. The treasure cards are saved until the end of the game and added up for points. The movement cards allow the character to move, but at the cost of making noise. As more noise is made, counters are removed from the dragon; if all the counters are removed, the dragon awakes, and every player in the dragon's line of sight must discard all treasure. Players can affect the dragon's line of sight by playing dragon movement cards.

The game ends when the deck of sixty cards is empty, and whoever has the most accumulated treasure wins.

The world has come to an end, and zombies, vampires and other horrors run rampant in the street. You and your group of survivors have one chance to get out alive. You've got to mow down hordes of oncoming undead and gather enough supplies to escape the city. Of course some of you might not make it out alive...

Eat Lead, Undead! is a frantic game of dart gun mayhem! Each turn, players move their survivors amidst hordes of swarming undead, which lurk behind stand-up scenery. Players then all fire their dart guns as much as they can for thirty seconds to protect their survivor — or perhaps wound a rival. Any survivor in a clear sector may gather equipment, while those who are wounded return home empty-handed. The first person to get the right combination of equipment escapes the city and wins!

Eat Lead, Undead! combines elements of Run, Fight or Die and City of Horror with the unique dexterity mechanism of dart guns for a riotous and unique co-op/competitive experience.

boardgame: Warmachine: High Command – Into the Breach
description:
Bolster your armies with Into the Breach, the second expansion for Warmachine: High Command!

Into the Breach contains five copies each of 16 different cards, plus four additional warcaster cards to bring new strategies to your Cygnar, Khador, Protectorate of Menoth, and Cryx decks.

Requires Warmachine: High Command core set to play.

boardgame: France Fights On: An Alternative History Wargame of June 1940
description:
This alternative history wargame examines the strategic and operational possibilities inherent in the campaign that would have occurred within France in June 1940 if that country’s government had resolved not to give up, as was the case historically. The assumption is that they instead chose to evacuate as much of the French Army as possible to France d’Outre Mer (Algeria). The resultant campaign, then, is one of strategic retreat and pursuit. The German player must try to destroy as much of the evacuating French force as possible, while keeping his own losses to a minimum in doing so.

Both sides’ orders of battle are entirely historical. The game length is held to only one month because, had the Germans not triumphed in that amount of time, the delay thereby caused to their plans for the next year would’ve then amounted to a major defeat in itself in the wider war.

Each game turn represents two days. Each hex represents eight miles (13 km) from side to opposite side.
 Components: One 22" x 34" map & 88 counters

Magazine: World at War #39

Modern War #13, Sept-Oct 2014

"The Next War in Lebanon" is an operational level wargame of a speculative Israeli invasion of Lebanon in the near future. The context is an Israeli invasion with the goal of eliminating the presence of Hezbollah terrorist group, and presumes a much larger invasion than the 2006 incursion (which is also included as a scenario). One player (the IDF) is assigned the role of the IDF (Israeli Defense Force) commander, and the other player is assigned the role of the Hezbollah forces, as well as Syrian and Iranian forces if certain events occur to cause their entry into the game (or players may agree to have these additional forces in the mix from the beginning of the game instead).

The game map features northern Israel and southern Lebanon, divided into 21 large hex territories, representing three categories of terrain: Urban (a major population center, such as the cities of Tyre, Sidon and Nabatiye), Populated (full of villages and small towns, and numerous roads), and Remote (rough terrain with few inhabitants or roads.) Stacking in any hex zone is unlimited, and units move and conduct operations within these hex zones to win the game.

description:
The Exciting Mini BasketBall Game. (From the Box)

The players take turns to flip mini basketballs into a multi hoop stand each hoop scoring different points, 4 balls per player with 9 hoops to target, highest score wins. An Alternative / Solo game option of 'three in a row / Tic-Tac-Toe' challenge is also suggested in the how to play guide.

This is a game of a series using the W-39 (Wrzesien 1939 - September 1939) system rules.

The Battle of the Bzura (or Kutno or Battle of Kutno — German name) was a battle in the opening campaign of World War II during the 1939 German invasion of Poland, fought between 9 and 19 September, 1939, between Polish and German forces. Finally, the Germans outflanked the Polish forces and took all of western Poland. It was the single largest battle in the 1939 September campaign and took place to the west of Warsaw, near the Bzura River. In it, a Polish breakout attack gained initial success but eventually faltered after a concentrated German counterattack.

The 4th edition includes the book "Piotrków 1939" per publisher webpage.

description:
Takuran means "Brood Parasite" in Japanese.

Takuran is a game played with 20 illustrated cards. 5 cards can be combined to complete an illustration, and there are 4 sets. The cards are shuffled, and each player receives 5 cards. The players then say how many cards they need to exchange in order to complete their set. The players have to decide who they will exchange their chosen number of cards with. This is done twice.

After the second exchange of cards, the player that completes their set, or has the most complete set, wins.

A promotional card included with the purchase of Dead of Winter: A Crossroads Game from Plaid Hat Games Online Store.

This promo introduces a new character to play with in your game, as well as a Cardboard Standee and Crossroads Card that you can use in the game.

Part of the Dead of Winter series.

"Ships in space. Lots of them. There was peace, then shots were fired, and then there was war." 
-Official statement, as provided by Anonymous Pilot-

Indeed, there was peace. But once a package is opened, up to 16 various Pilots (players) have stumbled into the growing conflict, and each turn shots are exchanged until only 1 leaves the battlefield alive.

Each package (the game comes in booster wrapping to provide a random drafting feel similar to various card game tournaments) includes the instructions and enough randomly assorted Pilot cards for a full game of 2-16 players (16 players means that each player only gets 1 Pilot for a game, but additional packs or fewer players allow for more Pilots per player.) The front of each card includes their ship name, their health/damage values, and up to 4 actions (1 on each card edge)that the played Pilot can choose to take for a turn.

Combat and damage plays out in a form similar to Rock, Paper, Scissors with a couple of twists. Each turn, all players play a Pilot card from their hand face-down, with the action of their choice being pointed towards the center of the group of players. The active player then chooses a target, and both of them flip over their Pilot cards and resolve the combat, the loser reducing and keeping track of their remaining health. If the targeted player has chosen a "Pass" action, then the target becomes the player in the direction of the Pass and they participate/resolve the combat instead. These steps continue until only one player has any Pilots remaining.

Kickstarter third character role set. Includes eight additional character role cards:

8- Prince/Princess
7- Whisperer
6- Patrician
5- Doctor
4- Bishop
3- Barrister
2- Swindler
1- Assassin

Set includes additional rules for:

- Badge cards:
  - Assassin: Death Badge
  - Barrister: False Evidence
  - Whisper: Rumor
- Prince/Princess card with 'promises' binding agreement [player that breaks a promise is immediately executed]
- Activation Order clarifying rule [start with highest ranked player]

Here comes the Boar King! It messes up the crops and attacks other boars and people. To keep peace in the mountain, the brave boar hunters are recruited for the hunt!

Are you ready? Don’t forget to put some glorious tattoo on your face as a token of blessings and braveness!

Deal one player aid card and one hunter marker to each player. The game starts with the youngest player.

In clockwise order, each player rolls the die and everyone acts according to the image shown on the die result. The player who is the last to perform the appropriate action must be painted a brush of tattoo on his face by one of the other players.

The game ends immediately when at least one player’s face has been painted with six brushes of tattoo.

boardgame: Not A Step Backwards (Ne Shaga Nazad) 1941-1942
description:
Easy to play Strategic/operational wargame Series covering the entire Eastern Front. This first game covers 1941-42. 10 Miles to the hex. 5-10 day turns depending on weather and time of the year. Divisional/Brigade/Regimental-level for Germans and Allies and Corps/Divisions/Brigades for Soviets. Logistics, air and naval effects. Two campaign games are included: June 1941 and 1942. And, includes several one and two map scenarios.

Command and variable movement system that includes cards that add random events and historical flavor, but do not drive the game. Political rules and both sides have their own unique way of play to show their historical capabilities and tendencies. New unit production and reinforcements.

The final days of the battle for Zverograd have arrived. The three blocs send their last forces into Zverograd in final attempts to take the city—or what’s left of it —no matter the cost. The Doomed City has been transformed into a giant killing field, riddled with minefields, bunkers, trenches, and other fortifications. Life there is cheap: death awaits around every corner, in every building.

(Currently in open beta, will be available free when beta is completed, estimated early 2014)

boardgame: ZULU!! Battles of Rorke's Drift and Isandlhwana 1879
description:
Zulu!! is Richard Berg’s game series (Great Battles for Empire).  Zulu!! has no hexes, no squares, and is not point-to-point. It also has counters with no ratings. In some ways, it’s like cardboard miniatures … with fewer rules. In that sense, it will be something new for many boardgamers.

Using a slight variation of Ye Olde Igo-Hugo play sequence, the game system highlights the firepower of the British Martini Henry rifle, the value of a solid defensive position, and the greatly superior numbers of the three Zulu Amibutho (divisions) that gave them something like 40-1 numerical superiority at times. And all of this without having to move hundreds of counters around, another innovation. The map scale is 1” = 4 yards. All of which plays in a couple of hours.

description:

The bioships, and Species 8472 themselves, were resistant to assimilation by the Borg and had enough firepower to destroy a Borg ship with only a couple of shots. This ship is part of the 4th wave of expansion packs for the Star Trek: Attack Wing game.

description:
Each player controls a number of characters. Each character receives a rating in each of the following attributes: strength, mobility, senility, and vigor. The player divides twelve points among these four attributes but no attribute may have a rating greater than six. The gameboard is a 17 x 11 hex grid devoid of obstacles.

A player’s turn consists of three phases wherein a player may try to move any or all of his/her units: Senility check (roll a D6, if the die roll is greater than the unit’s senility rating, then the unit may not move this turn), Movement (any unit that can move may do so), and Combat (units in the same hex as an opponent’s hex may duke it out).

A player is eliminated from play when all of his/her units are eliminated. The sole survivor is the winner.

This game takes up the last 4 1/2 pages of the Macho Women with Guns: The Final Chapter (Part One) rules supplement.

New York, New York... is a game covering the invasion of this key American colonial city by what was the largest invasion force - men and ships - ever assembled by the British in 1776.

The game can be played by one or two players, or more, if they like to argue about who gets to do what. Which would provide an interesting piece of Reality. The heart of the game is the Activation system, which requires the players to purchase (with a constantly varying number of Initiative Points). The game-map uses a scale of ca.650 yards per hex. Each game-turn (there are ten) represents a day of real time. Most units are regiments or battalions.

The game includes: •• 1 33”x 22” game map •• 1 Sheet of 280 counters •• 20 Op Cards (10 British, 10 American) •• 12 Pages of rather Revolutionary rules.

Maciejowice 1794 depicts the last battle of Thaddeus Kosciuszko. Poles were opposed by a Russian Army led by general Ivan Fersen. Mechanics of the game are based on random leader activations that decide sequence in which units fire, perform movement and melees. Almost all units can participate in ranged combat, not only activated ones, so the battle can rage on all parts of the map. Many rules are identical to Szczekociny 1794 ruleset - additional rules include Limbered Artillery, effectiveness of first salvo, firing upon enemy from rear or flank and collapse of fighting units.

Scale of the game:
Infantry - companies
Cavalry - squadrons
Artillery - batteries of 2 to 6 guns
One hex - 100 meters
One turn - 15 minutes

An additional card offered as a Kickstarter exclusive.

The Summoner allows you to copy any Patron that is played by another player and cash him in for the same amount of Victory Points points as that other Patron card.

Help Nancy find all her fancy accessories in this 3D board game.

Build Nancy's two story house, making eight rooms that Nancy has misplaced her 16 accessory pieces in the 24 possible places in the house. The accessories are round tokens that are randomly placed in 24 slots (three available in each room).

Then the 32 cards (two of each accessory) are shuffled and players take turns, drawing a card from the pile and attempting to location the making token, showing everyone the chosen token. If it matches the card, you keep the token and the card. If not, you put the token back, and put the card at the bottom of the draw pile.

If the card you draw is one someone has already matched, set it aside and draw again. The first player to find 4 accessories wins.

For younger players allow them to look at the tokens they are placing in the various slots.

Players must obtain six different colored headquarters chips by correctly answering trivia questions about the University of Notre Dame.  Question order is determined based on where a player lands on the board using a die roll. Once all six chips have been obtained, the player moves to a leprechaun space in the center of the board and answers a question in the category of their opponent's choosing.  Once this is answered correctly, the player must return to their start space and answer another question of the category that matches the start space color. If incorrect, the player loses that color chip and must move around the board to correctly answer a question of that category and retrieve the missing chip.  If correct, the game is won.

Operational level wargame based on Japanese animation series Space Battleship Yamato 2199.

One player controls Terran's space battleship Yamato and the other player controls the Dommel fleet of Gamilas Empire. The Dommel fleet has various organization at random.

Each turn, players determine initiative. If initiative roll is tied then some event occurs. The initiative players take turn managing movement and combat.

The Gamilas player wins when they sink the Yamato after the capture the Yurisha Iscandar event occurs. The Terran player wins when they prevent the Gamilas player from doing so.

The game includes a rulebook, a game map, and 50 die-cut counters.

boardgame: Yamato 2199 Game: Chapter 7 – The Plot to Assassinate Desler
description:
Political conflict game based on Japanese animation series Space Battleship Yamato 2199. The name and cover art is inspired from The Plot to Assassinate Hitler, but the game mechanics are totally different.

Players control one of the political factions in the Gamilas Empire. Each faction has a different goal and special action.

Each turn, players take three actions from any combination of Draw new chit, Special actions, and Recruit a character.

The drawn chit represent a character or a event. An event is resolved by using the characters. The result affects the victory points of the faction and the stability of the Empire. Most events need cooperation to achieve.

The winner is determined by victory points.

The game includes a rulebook, a game map, and 50 die-cut counters.

This is a party game based on guessing what type of panties a person might wear if they were to go on a date. 5 situation cards are laid on the table. These cards ask "What kind of girl is she," "What kind of guy is he," "How did they meet," "When is their date," and "Where will they go on their date?" Each player fills out the back of the situation card in dry-erase marker.

The answers to the situation are shown, and one designated player has to decide what style and color of panties a person in the presented situation would wear. They also lay out 3 pairs of underwear that they do not think would be appropriate for this situation. To denote this, 4 point tokens are placed face down, marking which style they think is best, ok, neutral, and inappropriate.

The remaining players then have to vote on which pair they think is the best. The face down point tokens are shown, and points for correct guesses are accumulated.

This process repeats as the players rotate, so that each player has the chance to try every role.

description:
Kokkuri Jinro is a simple and quick Werewolf-game using the mechanism from the Japanese traditional "boogie board" fortune-telling method in which people sitting around a table keep their index finger on a coin that glides around a piece of paper with letters on it.

In this take on the traditional Werewolf game — with villagers trying not to be eaten while the secret werewolf player tries to do snack on the villagers — no one ever closes their eyes; instead only the Werewolf player is allowed to steer the coin, and the other players try to find out who that guy is.

If the coin reaches a player, that player loses a life; each player has two lives to start, and if they lose both lives, they're out of the game. After someone loses a life from the coin, the players discuss and vote on who to string up, and that player also loses one life.

Players out of the game reveal who they were. The game continues until either the Werewolf dies (in which case the villagers win) or only two players remain, with the Werewolf being one of them (in which case the Werewolf wins).

User summary:

A Werewolf-type game where there is no hidden killing during the night phase, but instead all killed players vote on who to curse to death.

Each player also has an open character that has a special power.

Make Rule is an (almost) ruleless card game that comes with 36 cards in six types numbered 1–5 and one each with a star.

Lacking rules, the game comes with instructions on how to create rules. Rules can basically be anything, but should generally be "who wins" and "how do you play the cards". Each player gets to suggest three rules, and each rule is voted on. If at least half of the players vote yes for the rule, it's accepted.
publishers: ワイビーワイゲームズ　(YbY Games)

description:
User summary:

Naked Formular (sic!) is a Formula 1-themed hand management card game.

During setup, the race track is decided by placing a set number of track cards face down in a circle. During the game, one card at a time will be turned face up, and for each track card, players will play cards from hand, competing for being in first place for that specific card. What cards are good to play depends on the number and type (straight, chicane or corner) of the track card.

When a player plays a card, if another player has 2 cards of that number in his hand, he can nullify the card played, steal it and put in in front of him. This protects him a while from getting overtaken.

User summary:

An idol dice-rolling game based on the anime "Love Live!"

Players are dealt a hand of cards representing idols. Each idol has 2 effects: one that is applied pre-dice rolling and one that can be applied after.

From a selection of possible concerts, players choose simultaneously which one to try on, and in turn, they roll 6 dice, trying to get as high a total as possible; the higher total, the higher points, depending on the concert card. Prior to rolling, the player can play one card from his hand to apply the "pre-rolling" effect (which essentially decides certain outcomes), and after rolling, the player can play any number of cards applying the "post-rolling" effect; which usually change one dice into another or adds to the total.

The player with the most points when all concerts are finished wins.

The title is short for "Bokura no Live, Kimi to no dice", meaning "Our live, the dice with you".

User summary:

A battle card game themed as a fight between 10-year-olds.

Each player gets a team of 3 kids with traits in attack, defence, imagination and ability to carry. These are deployed onto 3 areas (with a max of 2 rom each side in one area). Each turn, cards are drawn from the item deck and the active player can do one action with each of his characters: pick and equip an item, attack the opponent in the same area, move to another area, use an equipment or imagine.

Points are awarded to defeat the opponent by inflicting 4 damage, or by reaching the imagination limit of that character. First to 6 points wins.

Items include all sorts of weird things like love letters, rain boots and bus stops.

boardgame: Big Picture Apples To Apples Snack Pack Game
description:
Play Big Picture Apples To Apples™ Snack Pack™ Game as a standalone game, or mix it with the cards in the Big Picture Apples to Apples big box or with other Apples to Apples games.

Make comparisons by playing picture cards. Descriptions picked by the judge win an Apple Chip. First player to collect five chips wins the game.

Note: Walmart exclusive. Actual color and style may vary by store.

boardgame: Adventurous Apples To Apples Snack Pack
description:
Adventurous Apples to Apples Snack Pack can be played as a standalone game or combined with any other Apples to Apples game to bring new choices into play.

Either way, Apples to Apples consists of two decks of cards: Things and Descriptions. Each round, the active player draws a Description card (which features an adjective like "Hairy" or "Smarmy") from the deck, then the other players each secretly choose the Thing card in hand that best matches that description and plays it face-down on the table. The active player then reveals these cards and chooses the Thing card that, in his opinion, best matches the Description card, which he awards to whoever played that Thing card. This player becomes the new active player for the next round.

Once a player has won a pre-determined number of Description cards, that player wins.

Welcome to the 1920's and the Appalachian Mountains! You have been commissioned to create a section of the Appalachian Trail, something that will eventually extend 2,200 miles through 14 states! It is your job to forge the best trail possible, with varying terrains to cross, scenic views, and majestic native wildlife. Unfortunately, others have also been commissioned to create sections of the trail and might try to stop you!

In Appalachian Trail each player is given a section of the trail to complete. This is accomplished by matching up similar terrain cards to each other. However, your opponents might put an obstacle in your path, forcing you to change the direction of your trail.

There are also trail improvements that increase the value of your section of the Appalachian Trail so that it attracts more hikers.

Completing your trail first is important but winning is based on victory points which are accumulated through: trail completion, longest trail, set collection, and more.

The seaside would not be the seaside without donkeys on the beach! This wonderful game from the height of the Victorian era is full of superb graphics and of course is great fun to play as well. It will provide hours of fun as you learn the antics of the donkeys!

The game is spin/move. Players spin the indicator and may not move initially until they obtain a "donkey boy." Whenever a player spins a 6 or "donkey boy" they get another turn. Every few spaces there are different circumstances to overcome on the game board. To end the game a player must spin the exact amount of spaces necessary to move to the finish line.

From box:

The all new Act One is the Exciting game where everyone gets to make a scene! In a race against the clock, players do almost anything to try and get their teammates to guess the titles of popular movies and TV shows. The sooner teams guess correctly, the more points are scored. But who cares who wins, when it's this much fun!

Simple to play: Guess the Movie or TV title correctly and score points. Player race against the clock (40 sec.) per card. Players must "act: (read) each scene on the card. Better acting will help. We suggest first to 20 points wins.

Print and Play this game***
        
    


Motukoreaite is a rare and expensive commodity. You are scientists and have been sent to an island to recover this precious mineral. Unfortunately, your hot air balloon crashes, with parts and equipment spread over the island. With your balloon badly damaged and your team separated, you must work together to survive and get off the island. Little do you know the island is not unoccupied. A roving creature senses your presence and hears your movements. Sometimes it will take what you are searching for; sometimes it will affect your well-being.

Finding lost equipment will help you explore the island more easily. Each player can carry only a limited number of items, though, so you may be forced to leave something valuable behind so that you can carry balloon parts. To win the game, you must keep track of your team's health and get to the balloon crash site with the missing parts before the creature does.

Browns Island consists of a modular board. Players start with a set of four cards, which are played to gain lost equipment as they move around the board. Some equipment gives them added advantages to collect parts for their crashed balloon. Players also try to avoid a roving creature. If the creature discovers a part from your balloon, players lose happiness tokens and must try to take the items back by using their gained skills (via action cards) and found equipment.

2 - 4 players with a SINGLE-PLAYER variant.

User summary:

A 2 player card driven battle game based on the manga with the same name by Kentaro Miura.

Players draw 5 cards from a common deck; cards are numbered 0–5. Each player puts down a card, and in turn, turn them face up to either move as many spaces as the card shows or attack (if the distance to the enemy is as many spaces as the card shows) and then puts down another card. Hence moves are programmed one turn in advance.

The first player to inflict 2 damage wins. The game also has special character cards, and some rule-changing cards to draw at random if players want to spice up the game.

User summary:

A cooperative game based on various SF-themed anime TV shows. Players are pilots of various space craft and try to destroy the Dead Star before the enemy invades their base.

The game is played on a 12x3 square gridded board. All players line up their crafts to the left, and the enemy crafts are lined up on the board's right. Players are dealt 5 cards each of which they choose 3 to play face down. When all have chosen, players turn their cards face up and the players execute their cards in order of speed on the cards. Players who end up having the same speed are blocked and their turns are skipped.

After all players have done, dice are rolled to determine what the enemy crafts do (attack or move). If the players manage to reach the far end of the game board, they win. If all players are killed, or the enemy reaches the left of the game board, the players lose.

The game also comes with optional difficulity rules, and one of them include a traitor mechanism.

Play

One player is black the other white.

On a turn, you move a friendly pawn to an empty adjacent stack of tiles that is 1 higher, 1 lower or the same height as the one it was on, then remove one empty tile from an adjacent stack.

Objective

There are two ways to win,

1) The player who makes a straight line out of his three pawns either orthogonally or diagonally (the line of pawns must be on adjacent stacks of the same height) or
 2) The player last to move.

language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Be the CEO of one of five corporations competing to set up mining operations in the asteroid belt. Demands for resources are at a record high. Corporations, fueled by their desire for wealth and power, race for the chance to be the supplier of these resources. In seven years, the rest of Earth's corporations will have caught up and the ridiculous prices for these precious materials will start to drop. Be the corporation that profits the most and show the galaxy who is the best.

Asteroid Miner is a worker placement and resource management board game in which players are the CEO of one of the first corporations to make mining the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter profitable. Players play for a maximum of six rounds, which represent a year in space. Each round, players must pick a special subcontractor card which gives special powers for the rest of the round. During the round players must choose where to send their crews: mine, blast, upgrade, or pick up contracts.

User summary:

A dice-driven tournament style battle game. Each player picks 2 mages and 2 fighters to form 2 teams, and place them on the tournament grid. Each fight is performed by both sides rolling the number of dice specified by the fighter, trying to reach as high a total as possible. The mage in each team can use spells to affect the dice, and the fighter can in turn have abilities that help the mage. Game is won by combination of fighter-mage, skillful use of spells and some dice luck.

The player who finishes first in the tournament wins the game.

This game shares the same world view as Alchemist Atlier and Seven Force

User summary:

An cooperative adventure game where players explore a map, trying to empower themselves as much as possible before the time runs out and they have to battle the Demon Lord.

The game is played with a music player playing instructions, randomized from a play list – the voice of the captive princess that guides the players (in the right or wrong direction). Instructions include "go west" or "don't go north" or "gain a life" and so on.

The game includes a possible traitor mechanism: One of the players might be the demon lord in disguise, which is why players also have to be wary.

The game ends when a player decides to fight the Demon Lord, or the random player has selected "place a token on the Demon Castle" three times, at which point the Demon Lord is revealed and the players compare their strengths with the enemy. Most powerful side wins.

The princess's voice is done by Kurumi Miru.

User summary:

The third extra Tragedy Set to Tragedy Looper; this booklet contains a new set of roles, plots and events, instructions and hints on how to create scripts with them, and 5 prewritten scripts to play.

It's based on alternate worlds, and the prevailing main rule is that each other day, Paranoia and Intrigue counters switch roles.

User summary:

A script collection for "Tragedy Looper", from a script creation competition held by Bakafire. Only three scripts are by Bakafire, the rest by competition participants. It contains 30 scripts, and 2 expansion characters, the Illusionist and the Scientist.

The scripts in the collection are for different tragedy sets:

     4 scripts are for the First Steps Tragedy set (from the base box)
     16 scripts are for the the Basic Tragedy set (from the base box)
     8 scripts are for the Mystery Circle Tragedy set (released separately)
     2 scripts are for the Haunted Stage Tragedy set (released separately)

Marches of Mists is a fantasy board/card game using the exclusive DCSP (Dynamic Card Placement System) mechanic, created by Bruno Gaia and co-designed by Youri Faja.

Using the mechanics of deck building, popular since Dominion, Marches of Mists widely differs from its ancestor due to the DCPS, a mechanic that allows and favours direct interaction while adding various notions to the placement of cards (like variation of speed in the player's progress on the board, disruption of the player's orders, targeting the opponent's positions, etc.) The DCPS is the result of years of research conducted by Bruno Gaia to find a brand new way of using cards in board games. 
  
In Marches of Mists, players battle to slay enemy hero tokens and destroy the opponent's population. The game ends -and the counting of victory points starts- whenever a player's population reaches zero, four recruitment piles are empty or a player has lost his last hero token.

The game is played by two players or any multiple of two, using an extra copy of the game for each extra pair of players. When more than two players are around the table, Marches of Mists is played as a team battle with phases of interaction between allies known as Councils; it is an epic experience that needs perfect knowledge of the game's cards and strategies, especially when more than four players are involved.

Set in a singular fantasy world, Marches of Mists is currently illustrated by Camille Mazauric and it is the first game developed by Bruno Gaia with the help of Youri Faja, the designer of "Requiem".

Juego de cartas de fútbol táctico.
Cada jugador forma su equipo, y después juegan el partido.
Cada jugador tiene unas habilidades, y para hacer jugadas hay que lanzar dados.


Super Gol (or Supergol) simulates a football (soccer) game. It was sold in an initial set that included a manual, a play mat, a balloon, a pair of dice and a few player cards to mount a couple of teams. Additional players were also sold.

Each player forms a team with 11 players and 5 on the bench, which can not exceed 100 points (each player had a cost).

To play, each player throws or one or two d6, to which they add the ability value.

description:
This box combines Reversi and Barbacan.

Barbacan can be played by two to six players. They try to be the first to get their men from the board's circumference into the castle by moving the pieces along the defined lines. An edition published in the 1920s also permitted to move them around the two circles outside the castle, which were omitted from later designs.

In Reversi, players must trap other pieces between their own to capture them (flip them to their color). Pieces will change sides (colors) dozens of times. The winner is the one with the most pieces of their color when no more pieces can be played on the board.

Exciting adventures await you with the game of super secret agents between cities, deserts, wetlands and glaciers. Find maps for credentials or the Top Secret Papers. There are two ways to win the game: passing the skills tests 5 or dial the name ROBINSON using the Top Secret papers.
Roll the die and move your secret agent around the Board. On the box where you stop get the info you need to win the game.
Along the way on board take TOP SECRET papers or CREDENTIALS cards or confronts evidence that are required.

boardgame: Blocks in the East: Chrome Expansion
description:
The Blocks in the East: Chrome Expansion is an expansion for Blocks in the East and includes:


    1 card deck, with 12 Special Events cards (400g, double laminated, UV matted, 3d)
    31 new units (wooden counters plus stickers)
    8 Axis Minor Factories with dedicated flags
    18 Production Trackers (1 Yellow, 1 Red and 1 Blue for 6 powers: Soviet Union, Germany, Finland, Romania, Hungary and Ukraine)
    20 Black Plastic OIL DRUMS 
    10 White Plastic Out of Supply Marker 


The Second Edition has a new packaging and includes a set of NATO Stickers.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
June 1944. WWII was in its fifth year, and the German army still controlled most of Western Europe. Field Marshall Erwin Rommel’s Army Group B had erected strong fortifications along the coast of Holland, Belgium, and France, including the French Mediterranean coast, creating The Atlantic Wall. The bunkers and minefields were backed up by some of Germany’s best troops. Allied forces had been gathering in England and the Mediterranean for more than two years, preparing to assault Hitler’s Fortress Europe.

On D-Day, 6 June 1944, they attacked on what Rommel called “the longest day.” Success of the invasion was far from guaranteed, but the Allied victory on the beaches signaled the beginning of the end for the Germans in Western Europe.

Paul Koenig's Fortress Europe recreates the war in Western Europe from the D-day invasion to VE-Day, what General Eisenhower dubbed “the mighty endeavor.” As the Allied player, you must choose a landing site and then get ashore with a successful landing. This is followed with a breakout of the beachhead and a drive across France, culminating with a deep penetration into Germany itself. As the German player, you must choose a wise defensive alignment of your forces, including hidden units, and hope that you are strong enough at the point of the actual landing to defeat the Allied invasion. If you fail at the water's edge, you must conduct an orderly withdrawal across France, constantly delaying the Allies, until you are strong enough to launch a winter counter-attack (historically, the Battle of the Bulge).

What's In The Box?:

•  One 40-page Rulebook
•  One 26" x 38" paper game map
•  Three sheets of double-sided, laser-cut game pieces
•  Four Player Aid cards
•  One six-sided 12mm die
•  One white, 9" x 11 7/8" Deluxe cardboard VPG game box
•  One beautiful box cover sleeve
•  One "Wipes-A-Lot" napkin
•  One charcoal dessicant packet
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Each player's force consists of ten pieces. The object of the game is for each player to try to get two of his pieces on to the squares of his opponent. That is, if he starts from the Starred squares, his object is to get two of his pieces on any two of the Crescent squares.

The game is won when one of the players succeeds in getting any Two of his pieces upon the squares from which his opponent's pieces started, or it is won if one of the players entirely destroys the other player's forces.

Athletic Sports is a typical spin and move board game: move the indicator and move down the track as many spaces as spun. First player to cross the finish space wins the game.

Contents: Instructions on the inside of the box top, 4 Wood pegs (2 are larger), 2 spinners, and the game board, which is on the bottom of the box.

Metropolys: Extension Cards, an expansion for the Metropolys board game, consists of four additional objectives for the family version of the game and four alternate objectives for the expert version.

Metropolys: Extension Cards is included in the Ystari Box of expansions for six Ystari games.

Amyitis: The Palace consists of a deck of 20 cards that replaces those in the Amyitis base game with mostly new abilities, a palace board and 2 additional characters, the Courtier and Noble. The expansion cards bear a orange frame to allow for easy recognition compared to the original ones.

Amyitis: The Palace is included in the Ystari Box of expansions for six Ystari games.

Caylus Magna Carta: The Favors consists of a deck of components to add a fifth player, new Prestige buildings, Resource and Neutral cards and favor board.

Caylus Magna Carta: The Favors is included in the Ystari Box of expansions for six Ystari games.

Sylla: Caveant Consules consists of new Events, Characters and a new Great Work to add to the base game.

Sylla: Caveant Consules is included in the Ystari Box of expansions for six Ystari games.

boardgame: Star Wars: The Card Game – Knowledge and Defense
description:
Knowledge and Defense is the third Force Pack in the "Echoes of the Force" cycle for Star Wars: The Card Game, continuing the cycle's journey into the power and mystery of the Force. As the forces of the dark and light sides clash over the galaxy, you'll find new tricks alongside iconic ships and characters in this Force Pack. You'll find Kyle Katarn's starship, Moldy Crow, searching for the Valley of the Jedi, even as the Rebel Alliance makes a return to Hoth. The Scum and Villainy of the galaxy grow rich off of the slave trade, and a new darkness is rising on the planet Dromund Kaas under the leadership of the deadly Sith Prophetess, Sariss. Whichever factions you choose to support in the battle for the galaxy, you'll be sure to reap the benefits of devoting yourself to the Force.

With the ten objective sets (two copies each of five different objective sets) included in this Force Pack, you will experience the unleashed power of the Force, whether you use it for knowledge and defense, or for your own personal gain!

Contains objective sets 107-111.
